title: The Wrong Chat Window
text_zh: 我想给好朋友发一条语音消息，吐槽新来的同事。我说了一大堆：他说话太慢了，开会的时候总是跑题，而且喝咖啡的声音特别大。说完很爽。然后我看了一眼屏幕——我发到了新同事的对话框里。我的手开始抖。我马上点撤回，系统提示：已超过两分钟，无法撤回。那天下午我一直没敢看手机。第二天早上，新同事走到我桌子前，递了一杯咖啡，笑着说：「以后有什么想法可以直接跟我说，不用发语音。」他喝了一口自己的咖啡，声音确实挺大的。
text_pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng gěi hǎo péngyou fā yì tiáo yǔyīn xiāoxi, tùcáo xīn lái de tóngshì. Wǒ shuō le yí dà duī: tā shuōhuà tài màn le, kāihuì de shíhou zǒngshì pǎo tí, érqiě hē kāfēi de shēngyīn tèbié dà. Shuō wán hěn shuǎng. Ránhòu wǒ kàn le yì yǎn píngmù — wǒ fā dào le xīn tóngshì de duìhuà kuāng lǐ. Wǒ de shǒu kāishǐ dǒu. Wǒ mǎshàng diǎn chèhuí, xìtǒng tíshì: yǐ chāoguò liǎng fēnzhōng, wúfǎ chèhuí. Nà tiān xiàwǔ wǒ yìzhí méi gǎn kàn shǒujī. Dì'èr tiān zǎoshang, xīn tóngshì zǒu dào wǒ zhuōzi qián, dì le yì bēi kāfēi, xiào zhe shuō: 'yǐhòu yǒu shénme xiǎngfǎ kěyǐ zhíjiē gēn wǒ shuō, bú yòng fā yǔyīn.' Tā hē le yì kǒu zìjǐ de kāfēi, shēngyīn quèshí tǐng dà de.
text_en: I wanted to send my good friend a voice message complaining about the new colleague. I went off: he talks too slowly, goes off-topic in every meeting, and drinks coffee absurdly loud. Felt great to say it. Then I looked at the screen — I'd sent it to the new colleague's chat window. My hands started shaking. I hit recall. System: 'More than two minutes have passed. Cannot recall.' I didn't touch my phone all afternoon. Next morning, the new colleague walked to my desk, handed me a coffee, and said with a smile: 'If you have thoughts, just tell me directly — no need for voice messages.' He took a sip of his own coffee. It really was pretty loud.

questions.q_zh: 「我」本来想把语音消息发给谁？
questions.q_en: Who did 'I' intend to send the voice message to?
questions.explanation: "I wanted to send my good friend a voice message" quietly carries the central idea.

questions.q_zh: 为什么消息撤回不了？
questions.q_en: Why couldn't the message be recalled?

options.text: 因为已经超过两分钟了
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yǐjīng chāoguò liǎng fēnzhōng le
options.text_en: more than two minutes had passed

options.text: 因为微信不能撤回语音
options.pinyin: yīnwèi Wēixìn bù néng chèhuí yǔyīn

questions.explanation: Between the lines of "System: 'More than two minutes have passed" sits the real point.

questions.q_zh: 新同事怎么回应的？
questions.q_en: How did the new colleague respond?

options.text: 递了一杯咖啡，笑着说以后可以直接跟他说
options.pinyin: dì le yì bēi kāfēi, xiàozhe shuō yǐhòu kěyǐ zhíjiē gēn tā shuō
options.text_en: handed over coffee, said to tell him directly next time

options.pinyin: qù gēn lǎobǎn gào zhuàng le
options.text_en: went to complain to the boss

questions.explanation: "If you have thoughts, just tell me directly — no" reveals the how.

title: Why Old Buildings Have Courtyards
text_zh: 在中国的很多老城区，传统的房子都有一个院子。这不只是为了好看，而是有很多实用的原因。首先，院子可以让阳光照进屋子里，这样房间就不会太暗。其次，夏天的时候院子能让风流通，比没有院子的房子凉快很多。另外，院子还是邻居们交流的地方——大家可以在院子里聊天、下棋、晾衣服。不但老人喜欢在院子里活动，小孩子也喜欢在院子里玩。可以说，院子不只是一个空间，它是一家人的「户外客厅」。现在的新楼房大部分都没有院子了，住在高楼里的人只能在阳台上养几盆花。虽然生活方便了很多，但是有些老人说，没有院子的房子少了一种「人情味」。
text_pinyin: Zài Zhōngguó de hěn duō lǎo chéngqū, chuántǒng de fángzi dōu yǒu yí gè yuànzi. Zhè bù zhǐ shì wèile hǎokàn, érshì yǒu hěn duō shíyòng de yuányīn. Shǒuxiān, yuànzi kěyǐ ràng yángguāng zhào jìn wūzi lǐ, zhèyàng fángjiān jiù bú huì tài àn. Qícì, xiàtiān de shíhou yuànzi néng ràng fēng liútōng, bǐ méiyǒu yuànzi de fángzi liángkuai hěn duō. Lìngwài, yuànzi hái shì línjūmen jiāoliú de dìfang——dàjiā kěyǐ zài yuànzi lǐ liáotiān, xià qí, liàng yīfu. Búdàn lǎorén xǐhuan zài yuànzi lǐ huódòng, xiǎo háizi yě xǐhuan zài yuànzi lǐ wán. Kěyǐ shuō, yuànzi bù zhǐ shì yí gè kōngjiān, tā shì yì jiā rén de「hùwài kètīng」. Xiànzài de xīn lóufáng dà bùfen dōu méiyǒu yuànzi le, zhù zài gāolóu lǐ de rén zhǐ néng zài yángtái shàng yǎng jǐ pén huā. Suīrán shēnghuó fāngbiàn le hěn duō, dànshì yǒuxiē lǎorén shuō, méiyǒu yuànzi de fángzi shǎo le yì zhǒng「rénqíngwèi」.
text_en: In many old districts in China, traditional houses all have a courtyard. This isn't just for aesthetics — there are many practical reasons. First, a courtyard lets sunlight into the rooms, so they won't be too dark. Second, in summer the courtyard allows air to circulate, making it much cooler than houses without one. Additionally, the courtyard is a place for neighbors to socialize — people can chat, play chess, and hang laundry there. Not only do the elderly like being active in the courtyard, children like playing there too. You could say a courtyard isn't just a space — it's a family's 'outdoor living room.' Most new apartment buildings don't have courtyards anymore; people living in high-rises can only grow a few potted flowers on their balconies. Although life has become much more convenient, some elderly people say that a house without a courtyard lacks a certain 'human warmth.'

questions.q_zh: 院子在夏天有什么好处？
questions.q_en: What benefit does a courtyard have in summer?

options.pinyin: néng ràng fēng liútōng, bǐjiào liángkuai

questions.explanation: The key is "Second, in summer the courtyard allows air to circulate, making" — read it with the answer in mind.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的写作手法有什么特点？
questions.q_en: What is distinctive about the writing technique in this passage?

options.text: 以小见大，用细节传达深意
options.pinyin: yǐ xiǎo jiàn dà, yòng xìjié chuándá shēnyì
options.text_en: uses small details to convey big meaning

questions.explanation: The author earns the effect through moments like "In many old districts in China, traditional houses all have."

questions.q_zh: 老人说没有院子的房子少了什么？
questions.q_en: What do elderly people say houses without courtyards lack?
questions.explanation: The key is "Although life has become much more convenient, some elderly people" — read it with the answer in mind.

title: The Left-Handed Girl
text_zh: 从小到大，我一直用左手写字。小时候老师总是让我换成右手，说这样写字才「正常」。妈妈也曾经把筷子放到我的右手里，但是我怎么都用不习惯。后来妈妈放弃了，说：「算了，只要你自己觉得方便就好。」长大以后我才知道，世界上大概百分之十的人是左撇子。在学校里，左撇子的人用剪刀、写字都比别人不方便一些，因为大部分工具是为右手设计的。但是我从来不觉得这是一个问题。相反，我觉得这是我的一个特点。有时候新认识的人看到我用左手吃饭，会很惊讶地说：「你是左撇子啊！」好像发现了一个小秘密一样。我笑着说：「对啊，这是我跟世界不一样的地方。」
text_pinyin: Cóng xiǎo dào dà, wǒ yìzhí yòng zuǒ shǒu xiě zì. Xiǎo shíhou lǎoshī zǒng shì ràng wǒ huàn chéng yòu shǒu, shuō zhèyàng xiě zì cái「zhèngcháng」. Māma yě céngjīng bǎ kuàizi fàng dào wǒ de yòu shǒu lǐ, dànshì wǒ zěnme dōu yòng bu xíguàn. Hòulái māma fàngqì le, shuō: 「Suàn le, zhǐyào nǐ zìjǐ juéde fāngbiàn jiù hǎo.」 Zhǎng dà yǐhòu wǒ cái zhīdào, shìjiè shàng dàgài bǎi fēn zhī shí de rén shì zuǒpiězi. Zài xuéxiào lǐ, zuǒpiězi de rén yòng jiǎndāo, xiě zì dōu bǐ biéren bú fāngbiàn yìxiē, yīnwèi dà bùfen gōngjù shì wèi yòu shǒu shèjì de. Dànshì wǒ cónglái bù juéde zhè shì yí gè wèntí. Xiāngfǎn, wǒ juéde zhè shì wǒ de yí gè tèdiǎn. Yǒu shíhou xīn rènshi de rén kàn dào wǒ yòng zuǒ shǒu chī fàn, huì hěn jīngyà de shuō: 「Nǐ shì zuǒpiězi a!」 Hǎoxiàng fāxiàn le yí gè xiǎo mìmì yíyàng. Wǒ xiàozhe shuō: 「Duì a, zhè shì wǒ gēn shìjiè bù yíyàng de dìfang.」
text_en: For as long as I can remember, I've always written with my left hand. When I was little, teachers always told me to switch to my right hand, saying that was the 'normal' way to write. My mom also once put chopsticks in my right hand, but I could never get used to it. Eventually she gave up and said: 'Never mind, as long as you find it comfortable.' Only after growing up did I learn that about ten percent of people in the world are left-handed. At school, left-handed people find scissors and writing a bit less convenient, since most tools are designed for the right hand. But I never feel this is a problem. On the contrary, I think it's one of my characteristics. Sometimes when newly acquainted people see me eating with my left hand, they say in surprise: 'You're left-handed!' — as if they've discovered a little secret. I smile and say: 'Yes, it's the way I'm different from the world.'

questions.q_zh: 小时候老师让她做什么？
questions.q_en: What did the teacher tell her to do as a child?

options.pinyin: huàn chéng yòu shǒu xiě zì
options.text_en: switch to writing with the right hand

questions.explanation: "At school, left-handed people find scissors and writing a bit" gives you what you need.

questions.q_zh: 世界上大概多少人是左撇子？
questions.q_en: About what percentage of people in the world are left-handed?
questions.explanation: Go back to "Never mind, as long as you find it comfortable." That's where the answer lives.

questions.q_zh: 如果去掉这段话的最后一句，意义会怎样变化？
questions.q_en: If the last sentence were removed, how would the meaning change?

options.text_en: the overall feeling would be incomplete

questions.explanation: Follow the action in "For as long as I can remember" — that's how.

title: The Bicycle Bell
text_zh: 早上七点的老街道上，最多的声音不是汽车的喇叭，而是自行车的铃声。上学的孩子骑着自行车从巷子里冲出来，铃声叮叮当当的，像一首早晨的歌。卖菜的阿姨骑着三轮车慢慢地经过，车上堆满了新鲜的蔬菜。她不按铃，只是大声喊：「让一让！」邻居们早就习惯了她的声音。有一个白头发的老爷爷骑着一辆很旧的自行车，车篮里放着一个保温杯。他骑得很慢，慢到好像随时都会停下来。但是他脸上的表情很安静，好像世界上没有什么值得着急的事情。这条街上的自行车铃声已经响了几十年了，虽然越来越多的人开始开车，但是每天早上我还是能听到那些熟悉的声音。
text_pinyin: Zǎoshang qī diǎn de lǎo jiēdào shàng, zuì duō de shēngyīn búshì qìchē de lǎba, érshì zìxíngchē de líng shēng. Shàng xué de háizi qízhe zìxíngchē cóng xiàngzi lǐ chōng chūlái, líng shēng dīngdīng-dāngdāng de, xiàng yì shǒu zǎochén de gē. Mài cài de āyí qízhe sānlúnchē mànmàn de jīngguò, chē shàng duī mǎn le xīnxiān de shūcài. Tā bù àn líng, zhǐ shì dà shēng hǎn: 「Ràng yí ràng!」 Línjūmen zǎo jiù xíguàn le tā de shēngyīn. Yǒu yí gè bái tóufa de lǎo yéye qízhe yí liàng hěn jiù de zìxíngchē, chē lán lǐ fàngzhe yí gè bǎowēn bēi. Tā qí de hěn màn, màn dào hǎoxiàng suíshí dōu huì tíng xiàlái. Dànshì tā liǎn shàng de biǎoqíng hěn ānjìng, hǎoxiàng shìjiè shàng méiyǒu shénme zhíde zhāojí de shìqíng. Zhè tiáo jiē shàng de zìxíngchē líng shēng yǐjīng xiǎng le jǐ shí nián le, suīrán yuè lái yuè duō de rén kāishǐ kāi chē, dànshì měi tiān zǎoshang wǒ háishi néng tīng dào nàxiē shúxī de shēngyīn.
text_en: At seven in the morning on the old street, the most common sound isn't car horns but bicycle bells. Children heading to school burst out of alleys on their bikes, bells ringing ding-dong-ding, like a morning song. The vegetable-selling auntie pedals slowly by on her tricycle, piled high with fresh vegetables. She doesn't ring a bell — she just shouts: 'Make way!' The neighbors are long used to her voice. A white-haired grandfather rides a old bicycle with a thermos cup in the basket. He rides so slowly it seems he could stop at any moment. But his expression is serene, as if there's nothing in the world worth hurrying for. The bicycle bells on this street have been ringing for decades. Although more and more people are driving now, every morning I can still hear those familiar sounds.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的'留白'（没说的部分）有什么作用？
questions.q_en: What role does the 'white space' (what's unsaid) play in this passage?

options.text_en: gives the reader room to imagine

options.text: 说明作者写作能力不足
options.text_en: shows the author's lack of skill

questions.explanation: "A white-haired grandfather rides a old bicycle with a thermos" isn't decoration — it does real work here.

questions.q_zh: 卖菜的阿姨怎么让路人让路？
questions.q_en: How does the vegetable vendor ask people to move?
questions.explanation: The method is right there: "Make way!."

questions.q_zh: 这篇文章的结构安排说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the structural arrangement of this passage suggest?

options.text: 作者有意引导读者的情感
options.pinyin: zuòzhě yǒuyì yǐndǎo dúzhě de qínggǎn
options.text_en: the author deliberately guides the reader's emotions

questions.explanation: "At seven in the morning on the old street" — once you spot this, the answer clicks.

title: The Balcony Garden
text_zh: 对面楼六楼的阳台上有一个小花园。阳台不大，但是被主人安排得满满的：有红色的月季、绿色的薄荷、还有几盆不知道名字的多肉植物。每天早上，一个戴眼镜的中年女人会出来给花浇水。她动作很轻，好像怕吵醒还在睡觉的花。她给每盆花浇水的时间都不一样——月季多一些，多肉少一些。有时候她会蹲下来，仔细地看看叶子上有没有虫子。我觉得她照顾花的样子就像照顾孩子一样认真。下雨的时候，她会把怕水的花搬进屋里。刮大风的时候，她会用绳子把花盆固定好。因为她的照顾，那个阳台成了整栋楼最美的一个角落。
text_pinyin: Duìmiàn lóu liù lóu de yángtái shàng yǒu yí gè xiǎo huāyuán. Yángtái bú dà, dànshì bèi zhǔrén ānpái de mǎnmǎn de: yǒu hóng sè de yuèjì, lǜ sè de bòhe, hái yǒu jǐ pén bù zhīdào míngzi de duōròu zhíwù. Měi tiān zǎoshang, yí gè dài yǎnjìng de zhōngnián nǚrén huì chūlái gěi huā jiāo shuǐ. Tā dòngzuò hěn qīng, hǎoxiàng pà chǎo xǐng hái zài shuìjiào de huā. Tā gěi měi pén huā jiāo shuǐ de shíjiān dōu bù yíyàng——yuèjì duō yìxiē, duōròu shǎo yìxiē. Yǒu shíhou tā huì dūn xiàlái, zǐxì de kànkan yèzi shàng yǒu méiyǒu chóngzi. Wǒ juéde tā zhàogù huā de yàngzi jiù xiàng zhàogù háizi yíyàng rènzhēn. Xià yǔ de shíhou, tā huì bǎ pà shuǐ de huā bān jìn wū lǐ. Guā dà fēng de shíhou, tā huì yòng shéngzi bǎ huāpén gùdìng hǎo. Yīnwèi tā de zhàogù, nàge yángtái chéng le zhěng dòng lóu zuì měi de yí gè jiǎoluò.
text_en: On the sixth-floor balcony of the building across the way, there's a small garden. The balcony isn't big, but its owner has filled it completely: red roses, green mint, and a few pots of succulents whose names I don't know. Every morning, a middle-aged woman with glasses comes out to water the flowers. Her movements are gentle, as if afraid of waking flowers still sleeping. She waters each pot for a different amount of time — more for the roses, less for the succulents. Sometimes she crouches down to carefully check whether there are any insects on the leaves. I think she cares for the flowers as seriously as caring for children. When it rains, she moves the water-sensitive flowers inside. When strong winds blow, she ties the flowerpots down with rope. Thanks to her care, that balcony has become the most beautiful corner of the entire building.

questions.q_zh: 阳台上有什么植物？
questions.q_en: What plants are on the balcony?
questions.explanation: "The balcony isn't big, but its owner has filled" gives you what you need.

questions.q_zh: 她给不同的花浇水有什么区别？
questions.q_en: How does she water different flowers differently?

options.text: 月季多一些，多肉少一些
options.pinyin: yuèjì duō yìxiē, duōròu shǎo yìxiē
options.text_en: more for roses, less for succulents

options.text: 多肉多一些，月季少一些
options.pinyin: duōròu duō yìxiē, yuèjì shǎo yìxiē
options.text_en: more for succulents, less for roses

questions.explanation: The method is right there: "More for the roses, less for the succulents."

questions.q_zh: 刮大风的时候她怎么保护花？
questions.q_en: How does she protect the flowers in strong winds?

options.pinyin: bǎ huā bān jìn wū lǐ
yòng shéngzi bǎ huāpén gùdìng hǎo

questions.explanation: Look at "When strong winds blow, she ties the flowerpots down" — it shows the mechanism.

title: The Sound of Chopping
text_zh: 每天傍晚五点半，整个楼道里都能听到切菜的声音。咚咚咚，咚咚咚——每家的节奏都不一样。三楼的声音最快，好像在赶时间。五楼的声音最慢，一下一下的，特别有节奏感。四楼有时候会突然停下来，大概是在接电话。我住在六楼，回家以后也开始切菜，加入这场每天都会上演的「音乐会」。不但能听到切菜声，有时候还能闻到各种菜的香味从门缝里飘出来。三楼大概在炒肉，五楼好像在煮汤。这些声音和味道混在一起，组成了这栋楼傍晚的样子。虽然我不认识每一家的人，但是通过这些声音，我觉得我们住在同一栋楼里的人之间有一种看不见的连接。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān bàngwǎn wǔ diǎn bàn, zhěnggè lóudào lǐ dōu néng tīng dào qiē cài de shēngyīn. Dōng dōng dōng, dōng dōng dōng——měi jiā de jiézòu dōu bù yíyàng. Sān lóu de shēngyīn zuì kuài, hǎoxiàng zài gǎn shíjiān. Wǔ lóu de shēngyīn zuì màn, yí xià yí xià de, tèbié yǒu jiézòugǎn. Sì lóu yǒu shíhou huì tūrán tíng xiàlái, dàgài shì zài jiē diànhuà. Wǒ zhù zài liù lóu, huí jiā yǐhòu yě kāishǐ qiē cài, jiārù zhè chǎng měi tiān dōu huì shàngyǎn de「yīnyuèhuì」. Búdàn néng tīng dào qiē cài shēng, yǒu shíhou hái néng wén dào gè zhǒng cài de xiāngwèi cóng mén fèng lǐ piāo chūlái. Sān lóu dàgài zài chǎo ròu, wǔ lóu hǎoxiàng zài zhǔ tāng. Zhèxiē shēngyīn hé wèidao hùn zài yìqǐ, zǔ chéng le zhè dòng lóu bàngwǎn de yàngzi. Suīrán wǒ bú rènshi měi yì jiā de rén, dànshì tōngguò zhèxiē shēngyīn, wǒ juéde wǒmen zhù zài tóng yí dòng lóu lǐ de rén zhījiān yǒu yì zhǒng kàn bu jiàn de liánjiē.
text_en: Every evening at five-thirty, the sound of chopping vegetables can be heard throughout the whole stairwell. Thud-thud-thud — each family has a different rhythm. The third floor is the fastest, as if in a rush. The fifth floor is the slowest, one chop at a time, with a particular sense of rhythm. The fourth floor sometimes stops suddenly — probably answering a phone call. I live on the sixth floor, and after coming home I start chopping too, joining this daily 'concert.' Not only can you hear the chopping, but sometimes you can smell various dishes wafting out through the door cracks. The third floor is probably stir-frying meat; the fifth seems to be making soup. These sounds and smells mix together to form this building's evening character. Although I don't know everyone in every apartment, through these sounds, I feel there's an invisible connection between all of us living in the same building.

questions.q_zh: 这段话中的矛盾或张力来自哪里？

options.text: 理想和现实之间的差距

title: The Bridge at Dusk
text_zh: 城市里有一座很老的石桥，它连接着河的两岸。白天桥上车来车往，很热闹。但是到了黄昏的时候，桥上的人就少了。太阳慢慢落下去的时候，整座桥被染成了金色。有几个老人靠在栏杆上看河水，他们的影子被夕阳拉得很长。桥下面有几条小船停在那里，船上没有人。河水很平静，把桥的倒影完完整整地映在水面上，好像水里还有另一座桥。我喜欢在这个时间走过这座桥，因为每一步都好像走在一幅画里面。有时候我会在桥的中间停下来，看着太阳一点一点地消失在远处的楼房后面。这个城市白天太忙了，只有在黄昏的时候，它才肯把最美的样子给你看。
text_pinyin: Chéngshì lǐ yǒu yí zuò hěn lǎo de shí qiáo, tā liánjiēzhe hé de liǎng àn. Báitiān qiáo shàng chē lái chē wǎng, hěn rènao. Dànshì dào le huánghūn de shíhou, qiáo shàng de rén jiù shǎo le. Tàiyáng mànmàn luò xiàqù de shíhou, zhěng zuò qiáo bèi rǎn chéng le jīnsè. Yǒu jǐ gè lǎorén kào zài lángān shàng kàn hé shuǐ, tāmen de yǐngzi bèi xīyáng lā de hěn cháng. Qiáo xiàmiàn yǒu jǐ tiáo xiǎo chuán tíng zài nàlǐ, chuán shàng méiyǒu rén. Hé shuǐ hěn píngjìng, bǎ qiáo de dàoyǐng wánwán-zhěngzhěng de yìng zài shuǐ miàn shàng, hǎoxiàng shuǐ lǐ hái yǒu lìng yí zuò qiáo. Wǒ xǐhuan zài zhège shíjiān zǒu guò zhè zuò qiáo, yīnwèi měi yí bù dōu hǎoxiàng zǒu zài yì fú huà lǐmiàn. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì zài qiáo de zhōngjiān tíng xiàlái, kànzhe tàiyáng yìdiǎn yìdiǎn de xiāoshī zài yuǎnchù de lóufáng hòumiàn. Zhège chéngshì báitiān tài máng le, zhǐ yǒu zài huánghūn de shíhou, tā cái kěn bǎ zuì měi de yàngzi gěi nǐ kàn.
text_en: In the city there's a old stone bridge connecting the two banks of a river. During the day the bridge is busy with traffic. But at dusk, fewer people remain. As the sun slowly sets, the entire bridge is dyed gold. A few elderly people lean against the railing watching the river water, their shadows stretched long by the setting sun. Below the bridge, a few small boats sit still, empty. The river is calm, reflecting the bridge's image perfectly on the water's surface, as if there's another bridge in the water. I like to cross this bridge at this hour, because every step feels like walking inside a painting. Sometimes I stop in the middle of the bridge, watching the sun disappear little by little behind the distant buildings. This city is too busy during the day — only at dusk does it consent to show you its most beautiful face.

title: The School Gatekeeper
text_zh: 我们学校的门卫是一个退休的老军人，大家都叫他刘叔。他每天早上六点就到了，把学校大门打开，然后在门口站着迎接每一个进来的人。不管是学生、老师还是送快递的，他都会点头打个招呼。他有一个特别的本领——能记住几乎所有学生的脸。有一次一个陌生人想混进学校，被他一眼就认出来不是学生，拦住了。学生们都很尊重他，因为他不只是一个门卫，还是大家的「保护者」。下雨的时候，他会在门口准备几把备用伞；天冷的时候，他的小屋里总有一壶热水，学生们可以来倒。他说：「我虽然退休了，但是在这里我还是在站岗。只是以前保护国家，现在保护孩子们。」
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xuéxiào de ménwèi shì yí gè tuìxiū de lǎo jūnrén, dàjiā dōu jiào tā Liú shū. Tā měi tiān zǎoshang liù diǎn jiù dào le, bǎ xuéxiào dà mén dǎkāi, ránhòu zài ménkǒu zhànzhe yíngjiē měi yí gè jìnlái de rén. Bùguǎn shì xuéshēng, lǎoshī háishi sòng kuàidì de, tā dōu huì diǎn tóu dǎ gè zhāohu. Tā yǒu yí gè tèbié de běnlǐng——néng jìzhù jīhū suǒyǒu xuéshēng de liǎn. Yǒu yí cì yí gè mòshēngrén xiǎng hùn jìn xuéxiào, bèi tā yì yǎn jiù rèn chūlái búshì xuéshēng, lán zhù le. Xuéshēngmen dōu hěn zūnzhòng tā, yīnwèi tā bù zhǐ shì yí gè ménwèi, hái shì dàjiā de「bǎohùzhě」. Xià yǔ de shíhou, tā huì zài ménkǒu zhǔnbèi jǐ bǎ bèiyòng sǎn; tiān lěng de shíhou, tā de xiǎo wū lǐ zǒng yǒu yì hú rè shuǐ, xuéshēngmen kěyǐ lái dào. Tā shuō: 「Wǒ suīrán tuìxiū le, dànshì zài zhèlǐ wǒ háishi zài zhàn gǎng. Zhǐ shì yǐqián bǎohù guójiā, xiànzài bǎohù háizimen.」
text_en: Our school's gatekeeper is a retired soldier. Everyone calls him Uncle Liu. He arrives every morning at six, opens the school gate, then stands at the entrance greeting everyone who comes in. Whether it's students, teachers, or delivery people, he gives everyone a nod. He has a special talent — he can remember almost every student's face. Once a stranger tried to sneak into the school, but he recognized at a glance that the person wasn't a student and stopped them. Students all respect him, because he's not just a gatekeeper — he's everyone's 'protector.' When it rains, he keeps spare umbrellas at the gate; when it's cold, his small room always has a kettle of hot water for students to pour. He says: 'Although I'm retired, I'm still standing guard here. Before I protected the country; now I protect the children.'

title: The Shifting Shoes
text_zh: 我发现了一个奇怪的现象：每天晚上我把鞋子整齐地放在门口，但是第二天早上起来的时候，鞋子总是被移到了旁边。我一开始以为是自己半夜起来上厕所的时候不小心踢到的。但是有一天我在鞋子前面放了一个小东西做记号，早上发现记号没有动，鞋子却被移了。我开始怀疑是不是家里有老鼠。直到有一个周末，我比平时早起了一个小时，安安静静地走到门口一看——原来是我养的那只猫。它正把我的一只鞋叼到角落里，然后把自己缩进鞋子里面睡觉。难怪每次只有一只鞋被移动了。我看着它那满足的样子，决定以后给它买一张小床。但是买了以后，它看都没看一眼，还是每天晚上钻进我的鞋子里。
text_pinyin: Wǒ fāxiàn le yí gè qíguài de xiànxiàng: měi tiān wǎnshang wǒ bǎ xiézi zhěngqí de fàng zài ménkǒu, dànshì dì èr tiān zǎoshang qǐlái de shíhou, xiézi zǒng shì bèi yí dào le pángbiān. Wǒ yì kāishǐ yǐwéi shì zìjǐ bànyè qǐlái shàng cèsuǒ de shíhou bù xiǎoxīn tī dào de. Dànshì yǒu yì tiān wǒ zài xiézi qiánmiàn fàng le yí gè xiǎo dōngxi zuò jìhao, zǎoshang fāxiàn jìhao méiyǒu dòng, xiézi què bèi yí le. Wǒ kāishǐ huáiyí shì bu shì jiā lǐ yǒu lǎoshǔ. Zhídào yǒu yí gè zhōumò, wǒ bǐ píngshí zǎo qǐ le yí gè xiǎoshí, ān'ānjìngjìng de zǒu dào ménkǒu yí kàn——yuánlái shì wǒ yǎng de nà zhī māo. Tā zhèng bǎ wǒ de yì zhī xié diāo dào jiǎoluò lǐ, ránhòu bǎ zìjǐ suō jìn xiézi lǐmiàn shuìjiào. Nánguài měi cì zhǐ yǒu yì zhī xié bèi yídòng le. Wǒ kànzhe tā nà mǎnzú de yàngzi, juédìng yǐhòu gěi tā mǎi yì zhāng xiǎo chuáng. Dànshì mǎi le yǐhòu, tā kàn dōu méi kàn yì yǎn, háishi měi tiān wǎnshang zuān jìn wǒ de xiézi lǐ.
text_en: I noticed something strange: every night I place my shoes neatly by the door, but the next morning they've always been shifted to the side. At first I thought I accidentally kicked them getting up for the bathroom at night. But one day I placed a small object in front of the shoes as a marker — the marker hadn't moved in the morning, yet the shoes had been shifted. I started suspecting mice. Until one weekend, I woke up an hour earlier than usual and quietly walked to the door to find — it was my cat. She was carrying one of my shoes to the corner, then curling up inside it to sleep. No wonder only one shoe ever got moved. Seeing her satisfied look, I decided to buy her a little bed. But after I bought it, she didn't even glance at it. She still crawls into my shoe every night.

title: The Scar on My Hand
text_zh: 我的左手上有一条小疤，是八岁的时候留下的。那年夏天我在院子里爬树，不小心从树上掉下来，手被一块石头割了一条口子。妈妈带我去医院缝了三针，我哭了很久。现在那条疤已经变得很浅了，不仔细看几乎看不出来。但是每次看到它，我就会想起八岁那个夏天——想起院子里那棵大树，想起在树下乘凉的爷爷，想起妈妈着急地抱着我跑去医院的样子。有时候朋友看到我手上的疤会问怎么回事，我总是笑着说：「小时候爬树掉下来的。」他们会觉得很普通，但是对我来说，这条疤不只是一个伤口的痕迹，它是我和童年之间的一条看得见的线。只要它还在，那些记忆就不会消失。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de zuǒ shǒu shàng yǒu yì tiáo xiǎo bā, shì bā suì de shíhou liú xià de. Nà nián xiàtiān wǒ zài yuànzi lǐ pá shù, bù xiǎoxīn cóng shù shàng diào xiàlái, shǒu bèi yí kuài shítou gē le yì tiáo kǒuzi. Māma dài wǒ qù yīyuàn féng le sān zhēn, wǒ kū le hěn jiǔ. Xiànzài nà tiáo bā yǐjīng biàn de hěn qiǎn le, bù zǐxì kàn jīhū kàn bu chūlái. Dànshì měi cì kàn dào tā, wǒ jiù huì xiǎng qǐ bā suì nàge xiàtiān——xiǎng qǐ yuànzi lǐ nà kē dà shù, xiǎng qǐ zài shù xià chéngliáng de yéye, xiǎng qǐ māma zhāojí de bàozhe wǒ pǎo qù yīyuàn de yàngzi. Yǒu shíhou péngyou kàn dào wǒ shǒu shàng de bā huì wèn zěnme huí shì, wǒ zǒng shì xiàozhe shuō: 「Xiǎo shíhou pá shù diào xiàlái de.」 Tāmen huì juéde hěn pǔtōng, dànshì duì wǒ lái shuō, zhè tiáo bā bù zhǐ shì yí gè shāngkǒu de hénjì, tā shì wǒ hé tóngnián zhījiān de yì tiáo kàn de jiàn de xiàn. Zhǐyào tā hái zài, nàxiē jìyì jiù bú huì xiāoshī.
text_en: On my left hand there's a small scar, left from when I was eight. That summer I was climbing a tree in the yard and accidentally fell — my hand was cut by a stone. Mom took me to the hospital for three stitches; I cried for a long time. Now the scar has faded to where you can barely see it without looking closely. But every time I see it, I'm taken back to that summer when I was eight — I remember the big tree in the yard, my grandfather cooling off beneath it, my mother anxiously carrying me as she ran to the hospital. Sometimes friends notice the scar on my hand and ask what happened. I always smile and say: 'Fell out of a tree when I was little.' They find it ordinary, but to me, this scar isn't just the trace of a wound — it's a visible thread between me and my childhood. As long as it's still there, those memories won't disappear.

title: Mom vs. Autocorrect
text_zh: 我妈给家人群发消息：「今天去买彩，晚上做红烧鱼。」我姐问：「妈，你什么时候开始买彩票了？」我妈说她打的是「买菜」，手机自己改的。这种事每天都会发生。上周她想说「我到家了」，手机改成了「我倒下了」，我爸吓得打了三个电话。我帮她关掉了自动纠正。结果更惨——没有自动纠正以后，她打错字反而更多了，而且这次没人帮她改。她试了一个星期，最后对我说：「算了，你以后帮我检查消息再发。」从此我每天收到十条待审核的消息。
text_pinyin: Wǒ mā gěi jiārén qún fā xiāoxi: 'jīntiān qù mǎi cǎi, wǎnshang zuò hóngshāo yú.' Wǒ jiě wèn: 'mā, nǐ shénme shíhou kāishǐ mǎi cǎipiào le?' Wǒ mā shuō tā dǎ de shì 'mǎi cài', shǒujī zìjǐ gǎi de. Zhè zhǒng shì měitiān dōu huì fāshēng. Shàng zhōu tā xiǎng shuō 'wǒ dào jiā le', shǒujī gǎi chéng le 'wǒ dǎo xià le', wǒ bà xià de dǎ le sān ge diànhuà. Wǒ bāng tā guān diào le zìdòng jiūzhèng. Jiéguǒ gèng cǎn — méiyǒu zìdòng jiūzhèng yǐhòu, tā dǎ cuò zì fǎn'ér gèng duō le, érqiě zhè cì méi rén bāng tā gǎi. Tā shì le yí gè xīngqī, zuìhòu duì wǒ shuō: 'suàn le, nǐ yǐhòu bāng wǒ jiǎnchá xiāoxi zài fā.' Cóngcǐ wǒ měitiān shōu dào shí tiáo dài shěnhé de xiāoxi.
text_en: Mom texted the family group: 'Going to buy lottery today, making braised fish tonight.' My sister asked: 'Mom, since when do you buy lottery tickets?' Mom said she typed 'buy groceries' — the phone changed it. This happens every day. Last week she typed 'I'm home' and the phone changed it to 'I've collapsed.' Dad called three times in a panic. I turned off her autocorrect. That was worse — without autocorrect she made even more typos, and now nothing was fixing them. She lasted one week, then told me: 'Forget it, you check my messages before I send them from now on.' Since then I get ten messages a day awaiting review.

questions.q_zh: 手机把「买菜」改成了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the phone change 'buy groceries' to?
questions.explanation: The answer sits right in "Going to buy lottery today, making braised fish tonight.'."

questions.q_zh: 关掉自动纠正以后怎么样？
questions.q_en: What happened after turning off autocorrect?

title: The Breakfast Stand Couple
text_zh: 我家楼下有一个卖煎饼的早餐摊，摊主是一对中年夫妻。丈夫负责摊煎饼，妻子负责加料和收钱。他们配合得特别好，从来不需要说话就知道对方要做什么。丈夫把煎饼摊好以后，往左边一推，妻子就接过来加鸡蛋、加生菜、刷酱。整个过程又快又流畅，像一支双人舞。他们每天早上五点就开始准备，一直忙到九点。最忙的时候排队的人有十几个，但是他们从来不慌。有时候妻子会偷偷往我的煎饼里多加一个鸡蛋，说：「你太瘦了，多吃点。」我觉得这份煎饼不只是早餐，它里面还包着一种只有在街边小摊才能感受到的温暖。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià yǒu yí gè mài jiānbing de zǎocān tān, tānzhǔ shì yí duì zhōngnián fūqī. Zhàngfu fùzé tān jiānbing, qīzi fùzé jiā liào hé shōu qián. Tāmen pèihé de tèbié hǎo, cónglái bù xūyào shuō huà jiù zhīdào duìfāng yào zuò shénme. Zhàngfu bǎ jiānbing tān hǎo yǐhòu, wǎng zuǒbiān yí tuī, qīzi jiù jiē guòlái jiā jīdàn, jiā shēngcài, shuā jiàng. Zhěnggè guòchéng yòu kuài yòu liúchàng, xiàng yì zhī shuāngrén wǔ. Tāmen měi tiān zǎoshang wǔ diǎn jiù kāishǐ zhǔnbèi, yìzhí máng dào jiǔ diǎn. Zuì máng de shíhou páiduì de rén yǒu shí jǐ gè, dànshì tāmen cónglái bù huāng. Yǒu shíhou qīzi huì tōutōu wǎng wǒ de jiānbing lǐ duō jiā yí gè jīdàn, shuō: 「Nǐ tài shòu le, duō chī diǎn.」 Wǒ juéde zhè fèn jiānbing bù zhǐ shì zǎocān, tā lǐmiàn hái bāozhe yì zhǒng zhǐ yǒu zài jiēbiān xiǎo tān cái néng gǎnshòu dào de wēnnuǎn.
text_en: Downstairs from my home there's a breakfast stand selling jianbing, run by a middle-aged couple. The husband makes the crepe; the wife adds toppings and collects money. Their coordination is perfect — they never need to speak to know what the other is about to do. When the husband finishes the crepe, he pushes it to the left, and the wife takes over to add egg, lettuce, and sauce. The whole process is fast and fluid, like a pas de deux. They start preparing at five every morning and stay busy until nine. At the busiest times, over a dozen people line up, but they never panic. Sometimes the wife sneaks an extra egg into my jianbing, saying: 'You're too thin — eat more.' I think this jianbing is more than breakfast. Wrapped inside it is a warmth that you can only feel at a street-side stand.

title: The Old Mailbox
text_zh: 巷子口有一个绿色的旧邮筒，已经在那里站了至少三十年了。以前每天都有邮递员来开它、取信。但是现在几乎没有人寄信了，邮递员大概一个月才来一次。邮筒上的绿色油漆已经掉了很多，露出了下面的铁锈色。有时候路过的小孩子会把手伸进邮筒的口里玩，被妈妈拉开。有人提议把这个旧邮筒拆掉，因为它又旧又没有用。但是住在附近的老人们不同意。一个老奶奶说：「这个邮筒比你们很多人的年纪都大。我年轻的时候，每个星期都往这里面投信。它见证了我们这条巷子的历史，不能说拆就拆。」后来大家投票决定把它留下来。有人还在旁边种了一盆花，让这个老邮筒看起来没那么孤单。
text_pinyin: Xiàngzi kǒu yǒu yí gè lǜsè de jiù yóutǒng, yǐjīng zài nàlǐ zhàn le zhìshǎo sānshí nián le. Yǐqián měi tiān dōu yǒu yóudìyuán lái kāi tā, qǔ xìn. Dànshì xiànzài jīhū méiyǒu rén jì xìn le, yóudìyuán dàgài yí gè yuè cái lái yí cì. Yóutǒng shàng de lǜsè yóuqī yǐjīng diào le hěn duō, lùchū le xiàmiàn de tiěxiù sè. Yǒu shíhou lùguò de xiǎo háizi huì bǎ shǒu shēn jìn yóutǒng de kǒu lǐ wán, bèi māma lā kāi. Yǒu rén tíyì bǎ zhège jiù yóutǒng chāi diào, yīnwèi tā yòu jiù yòu méiyǒu yòng. Dànshì zhù zài fùjìn de lǎorénmen bù tóngyì. Yí gè lǎo nǎinai shuō: 「Zhège yóutǒng bǐ nǐmen hěn duō rén de niánjì dōu dà. Wǒ niánqīng de shíhou, měi gè xīngqī dōu wǎng zhè lǐmiàn tóu xìn. Tā jiànzhèng le wǒmen zhè tiáo xiàngzi de lìshǐ, bù néng shuō chāi jiù chāi.」 Hòulái dàjiā tóupiào juédìng bǎ tā liú xiàlái. Yǒu rén hái zài pángbiān zhǒng le yì pén huā, ràng zhège lǎo yóutǒng kàn qǐlái méi nàme gūdān.
text_en: At the mouth of the alley there's a green old mailbox that's been standing there for at least thirty years. In the past, a mail carrier came every day to open it and collect letters. But now hardly anyone mails letters — the carrier comes perhaps once a month. Much of the green paint has peeled off, revealing the rust underneath. Sometimes passing children stick their hands into the mailbox slot to play, and their mothers pull them away. Someone proposed removing the old mailbox, since it's old and useless. But the elderly residents nearby disagreed. One grandmother said: 'This mailbox is older than many of you. When I was young, I mailed a letter here every week. It's witnessed the history of our alley — you can't just tear it down.' Eventually everyone voted to keep it. Someone even planted a pot of flowers next to it, so the old mailbox wouldn't look so lonely.

title: The Clothesline Between Buildings
text_zh: 我住的这栋楼和旁边那栋楼之间，有人拉了一条晾衣绳。我不知道是谁拉的，也不知道是什么时候拉的。但是每天都能看到绳子上挂着不同的东西：有时候是白色的床单，有时候是小孩子的衣服，有时候是一件男人的工作服。这条绳子把两栋楼连在了一起，像一座看不见的桥。有风的日子，衣服在空中飘来飘去，像在跳舞。下雨的时候，主人会赶紧把衣服收回去。但是偶尔有忘记收的时候，衣服就在雨里被淋得湿透了。第二天太阳出来，那些衣服又被重新挂了出去。这条简简单单的绳子，安安静静地记录着一个家庭每天的生活。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de zhè dòng lóu hé pángbiān nà dòng lóu zhījiān, yǒu rén lā le yì tiáo liàng yī shéng. Wǒ bù zhīdào shì shéi lā de, yě bù zhīdào shì shénme shíhou lā de. Dànshì měi tiān dōu néng kàn dào shéngzi shàng guàzhe bùtóng de dōngxi: yǒu shíhou shì bái sè de chuángdān, yǒu shíhou shì xiǎo háizi de yīfu, yǒu shíhou shì yí jiàn nánrén de gōngzuòfú. Zhè tiáo shéngzi bǎ liǎng dòng lóu lián zài le yìqǐ, xiàng yí zuò kàn bu jiàn de qiáo. Yǒu fēng de rìzi, yīfu zài kōng zhōng piāo lái piāo qù, xiàng zài tiào wǔ. Xià yǔ de shíhou, zhǔrén huì gǎnjǐn bǎ yīfu shōu huíqù. Dànshì ǒu'ěr yǒu wàngjì shōu de shíhou, yīfu jiù zài yǔ lǐ bèi lín de shī tòu le. Dì èr tiān tàiyáng chūlái, nàxiē yīfu yòu bèi chóngxīn guà le chūqù. Zhè tiáo jiǎnjiǎn-dāndān de shéngzi, ān'ānjìngjìng de jìlùzhe yí gè jiātíng měi tiān de shēnghuó.
text_en: Between my building and the one next to it, someone has strung a clothesline. I don't know who put it there or when. But every day you can see different things hanging on it: sometimes white bedsheets, sometimes children's clothes, sometimes a man's work uniform. This rope connects the two buildings like an invisible bridge. On windy days, the clothes drift back and forth in the air, as if dancing. When it rains, the owner hurries to take the clothes in. But occasionally they forget, and the clothes get soaked through in the rain. The next day when the sun comes out, those clothes are hung out again. This simple rope quietly records a family's daily life.

title: The Elevator Mirror
text_zh: 我们楼的电梯里有一面大镜子。物业说装镜子是为了让电梯看起来更大一些。但是我发现，镜子的真正作用比这有趣得多。每天早上，上班的人会在镜子前面整理头发、检查衣服。有的人会对着镜子练习微笑，好像在准备面对新的一天。下午放学回来的小孩子会在镜子前面做鬼脸，然后自己笑半天。傍晚回家的人在镜子里看到自己疲倦的样子，有时候会叹一口气。同一面镜子，在不同的时间映出不同的表情。它见过每一个人最真实的样子——因为在电梯里那短短的几十秒，大部分人都觉得没有人在看，所以会放下所有的伪装。其实电梯里的镜子，是这栋楼最诚实的记录者。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen lóu de diàntī lǐ yǒu yí miàn dà jìngzi. Wùyè shuō zhuāng jìngzi shì wèile ràng diàntī kàn qǐlái gèng dà yìxiē. Dànshì wǒ fāxiàn, jìngzi de zhēnzhèng zuòyòng bǐ zhè yǒuqù de duō. Měi tiān zǎoshang, shàngbān de rén huì zài jìngzi qiánmiàn zhěnglǐ tóufa, jiǎnchá yīfu. Yǒude rén huì duìzhe jìngzi liànxí wēixiào, hǎoxiàng zài zhǔnbèi miànduì xīn de yì tiān. Xiàwǔ fàng xué huílái de xiǎo háizi huì zài jìngzi qiánmiàn zuò guǐliǎn, ránhòu zìjǐ xiào bàntiān. Bàngwǎn huí jiā de rén zài jìngzi lǐ kàn dào zìjǐ píjuàn de yàngzi, yǒu shíhou huì tàn yì kǒu qì. Tóng yí miàn jìngzi, zài bùtóng de shíjiān yìng chū bùtóng de biǎoqíng. Tā jiàn guò měi yí gè rén zuì zhēnshí de yàngzi——yīnwèi zài diàntī lǐ nà duǎnduǎn de jǐ shí miǎo, dà bùfen rén dōu juéde méiyǒu rén zài kàn, suǒyǐ huì fàng xià suǒyǒu de wěizhuāng. Qíshí diàntī lǐ de jìngzi, shì zhè dòng lóu zuì chéngshí de jìlùzhě.
text_en: In our building's elevator there's a big mirror. Property management says it was installed to make the elevator look larger. But I've found the mirror's real function is far more interesting. Every morning, commuters fix their hair and check their clothes in front of it. Some practice smiling at the mirror, as if preparing to face a new day. In the afternoon, children returning from school make funny faces in the mirror, then laugh at themselves. In the evening, people coming home see their tired faces in the mirror and sometimes sigh. The same mirror reflects different expressions at different times. It has seen everyone's most authentic self — because during those brief seconds in the elevator, most people assume nobody is watching, and they drop all pretense. Really, the elevator mirror is this building's most honest recorder.

title: The Man Who Feeds Pigeons
text_zh: 广场上有一个老人，每天下午三点准时来喂鸽子。他总是坐在同一张长椅上，从口袋里拿出一袋玉米粒，慢慢地撒在地上。鸽子们已经认识他了，他还没坐下来，它们就从四面八方飞过来了。有时候鸽子会飞到他的肩膀上、手上，他就那样一动不动地坐着，好像自己也变成了广场的一部分。路过的人会停下来拍照，但他从来不看镜头。有小孩子想跑过去抓鸽子的时候，他会轻轻摇头说：「别吓它们。」等玉米粒撒完了，他就安静地站起来，拍拍身上的灰，慢慢走回家。鸽子们看到他走了，还会在他后面飞一段路，好像在送他回家。
text_pinyin: Guǎngchǎng shàng yǒu yí gè lǎorén, měi tiān xiàwǔ sān diǎn zhǔnshí lái wèi gēzi. Tā zǒng shì zuò zài tóng yì zhāng chángyǐ shàng, cóng kǒudài lǐ ná chū yí dài yùmǐ lì, mànmàn de sā zài dì shàng. Gēzimen yǐjīng rènshi tā le, tā hái méi zuò xiàlái, tāmen jiù cóng sìmiàn-bāfāng fēi guòlái le. Yǒu shíhou gēzi huì fēi dào tā de jiānbǎng shàng, shǒu shàng, tā jiù nàyàng yídòng-bùdòng de zuòzhe, hǎoxiàng zìjǐ yě biàn chéng le guǎngchǎng de yí bùfen. Lùguò de rén huì tíng xiàlái pāi zhào, dàn tā cónglái bú kàn jìngtóu. Yǒu xiǎo háizi xiǎng pǎo guòqù zhuā gēzi de shíhou, tā huì qīngqīng yáo tóu shuō: 「Bié xià tāmen.」 Děng yùmǐ lì sā wán le, tā jiù ānjìng de zhàn qǐlái, pāipai shēn shàng de huī, mànmàn zǒu huí jiā. Gēzimen kàn dào tā zǒu le, hái huì zài tā hòumiàn fēi yí duàn lù, hǎoxiàng zài sòng tā huí jiā.
text_en: There's an old man on the square who comes to feed the pigeons at exactly three every afternoon. He always sits on the same bench, takes a bag of corn kernels from his pocket, and slowly scatters them on the ground. The pigeons already know him — before he even sits down, they come flying from all directions. Sometimes pigeons land on his shoulders, on his hands, and he sits perfectly still, as if he too has become part of the square. Passersby stop to take photos, but he never looks at the camera. When children try to run and catch the pigeons, he gently shakes his head and says: 'Don't scare them.' When the corn is all scattered, he quietly stands up, brushes the dust off himself, and slowly walks home. The pigeons see him go and fly behind him for a stretch, as if escorting him home.

title: The Overpass at Rush Hour
text_zh: 每天早上八点，这座天桥上全是赶着上班的人。大家走得很快，眼睛看着手机，耳朵戴着耳机，好像每个人都活在自己的世界里。天桥很窄，只要有一个人走得慢一点，后面就会堵住。我每天都要从这座天桥走过，用的时间大概三分钟。但是这三分钟里，我能感受到整个城市的节奏。脚步声、手机铃声、远处的汽车喇叭声混在一起，组成了一首城市的交响乐。偶尔有人在天桥上停下来接电话，马上就会被后面的人推着往前走。不知道从什么时候开始，我也学会了在天桥上一边走一边看手机。只有在周末的时候，天桥才会安静下来。那时候我才发现，站在天桥上能看到很远的地方。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang bā diǎn, zhè zuò tiānqiáo shàng quán shì gǎnzhe shàngbān de rén. Dàjiā zǒu de hěn kuài, yǎnjing kànzhe shǒujī, ěrduo dàizhe ěrjī, hǎoxiàng měi gè rén dōu huó zài zìjǐ de shìjiè lǐ. Tiānqiáo hěn zhǎi, zhǐyào yǒu yí gè rén zǒu de màn yìdiǎn, hòumiàn jiù huì dǔ zhù. Wǒ měi tiān dōu yào cóng zhè zuò tiānqiáo zǒu guò, yòng de shíjiān dàgài sān fēnzhōng. Dànshì zhè sān fēnzhōng lǐ, wǒ néng gǎnshòu dào zhěnggè chéngshì de jiézòu. Jiǎobù shēng, shǒujī líng shēng, yuǎnchù de qìchē lǎba shēng hùn zài yìqǐ, zǔ chéng le yì shǒu chéngshì de jiāoxiǎngyuè. Ǒu'ěr yǒu rén zài tiānqiáo shàng tíng xiàlái jiē diànhuà, mǎshàng jiù huì bèi hòumiàn de rén tuīzhe wǎng qián zǒu. Bù zhīdào cóng shénme shíhou kāishǐ, wǒ yě xué huì le zài tiānqiáo shàng yìbiān zǒu yìbiān kàn shǒujī. Zhǐ yǒu zài zhōumò de shíhou, tiānqiáo cái huì ānjìng xiàlái. Nà shíhou wǒ cái fāxiàn, zhàn zài tiānqiáo shàng néng kàn dào hěn yuǎn de dìfang.
text_en: Every morning at eight, this overpass is full of people rushing to work. Everyone walks fast, eyes on their phones, earbuds in, as if each person lives in their own world. The overpass is narrow — if even one person walks a bit slow, it creates a jam behind them. I cross this overpass every day, taking about three minutes. But in those three minutes, I can feel the whole city's rhythm. Footsteps, phone ringtones, distant car horns all mix together into a city symphony. Occasionally someone stops on the overpass to take a call and is immediately pushed forward by the people behind. I don't know when I started, but I too learned to walk across the overpass while looking at my phone. Only on weekends does the overpass go quiet. That's when I discover that standing on the overpass, you can see very far away.

questions.q_en: Why do people walk fast on the overpass?
questions.explanation: Pay attention to "Every morning at eight, this overpass is full of people" — it answers the why.

title: The Canteen Auntie
text_zh: 大学食堂有一个打饭的阿姨，同学们都叫她「手抖阿姨」——因为每次她打菜的时候，手总是会抖一下，菜就从勺子上掉回锅里了。大家都开玩笑说她是「专业减量大师」。但是有一件事很多人不知道：每到考试周，她打菜的手就不抖了，而且还会多给一点。有同学发现了这个秘密，问她为什么。她笑着说：「考试的时候你们压力大，要多吃一点才有力气考好。」后来大家才知道，她的女儿去年刚参加完高考，她特别理解学生的辛苦。从那以后，再也没有人抱怨她手抖了。大家排队到她的窗口的时候，还会跟她说：「阿姨今天辛苦了！」她每次都笑着摆摆手说：「不辛苦不辛苦。」
text_pinyin: Dàxué shítáng yǒu yí gè dǎ fàn de āyí, tóngxuémen dōu jiào tā「shǒu dǒu āyí」——yīnwèi měi cì tā dǎ cài de shíhou, shǒu zǒng shì huì dǒu yíxià, cài jiù cóng sháozi shàng diào huí guō lǐ le. Dàjiā dōu kāi wánxiào shuō tā shì「zhuānyè jiǎnliàng dàshī」. Dànshì yǒu yí jiàn shì hěn duō rén bù zhīdào: měi dào kǎoshì zhōu, tā dǎ cài de shǒu jiù bú dǒu le, érqiě hái huì duō gěi yìdiǎn. Yǒu tóngxué fāxiàn le zhège mìmì, wèn tā wèishénme. Tā xiàozhe shuō: 「Kǎoshì de shíhou nǐmen yālì dà, yào duō chī yìdiǎn cái yǒu lìqi kǎo hǎo.」 Hòulái dàjiā cái zhīdào, tā de nǚ'ér qùnián gāng cānjiā wán gāokǎo, tā tèbié lǐjiě xuéshēng de xīnkǔ. Cóng nà yǐhòu, zài yě méiyǒu rén bàoyuàn tā shǒu dǒu le. Dàjiā páiduì dào tā de chuāngkǒu de shíhou, hái huì gēn tā shuō: 「Āyí jīntiān xīnkǔ le!」 Tā měi cì dōu xiàozhe bǎibǎi shǒu shuō: 「Bù xīnkǔ bù xīnkǔ.」
text_en: In the university canteen there's a serving auntie the students all call 'Shaky Hand Auntie' — because every time she serves food, her hand trembles and some falls off the spoon back into the pot. Everyone jokes she's a 'professional portion-reducer.' But there's something most people don't know: during exam week, her hand stops shaking, and she even gives a bit extra. When a student discovered this secret and asked why, she smiled and said: 'During exams you're under a lot of pressure — you need to eat more to have energy to do well.' Later everyone learned that her daughter had just taken the college entrance exam last year, so she especially understands students' hardships. After that, nobody complained about her shaky hand again. When they line up at her window, they say: 'Auntie, you've worked hard today!' She always waves them off with a smile: 'Not hard at all.'

title: The Footprints on the Roof
text_zh: 下了一夜的雪，早上我上天台去看雪景。但是让我吃惊的是，天台的雪地上已经有了一串脚印。脚印从天台的门走到栏杆旁边，然后又走了回来。谁会在半夜上来天台呢？我问了楼里的邻居，没有人承认是自己。保安说他半夜没有看到任何人上楼。这件事让大家讨论了好几天。有人说是小偷，有人说可能是风把雪吹出了脚印的形状。直到有一天，我遇到了住在顶楼的张奶奶。她有点不好意思地说：「是我。昨天半夜睡不着，想上来看看雪。月光照在雪上面特别好看，我站在那里看了很久。」我突然觉得，那串孤独的脚印变得温暖了起来。
text_pinyin: Xià le yí yè de xuě, zǎoshang wǒ shàng tiāntái qù kàn xuě jǐng. Dànshì ràng wǒ chījīng de shì, tiāntái de xuědì shàng yǐjīng yǒu le yí chuàn jiǎoyìn. Jiǎoyìn cóng tiāntái de mén zǒu dào lángān pángbiān, ránhòu yòu zǒu le huílái. Shéi huì zài bànyè shàng lái tiāntái ne? Wǒ wèn le lóu lǐ de línjū, méiyǒu rén chéngrèn shì zìjǐ. Bǎo'ān shuō tā bànyè méiyǒu kàn dào rènhé rén shàng lóu. Zhè jiàn shì ràng dàjiā tǎolùn le hǎo jǐ tiān. Yǒu rén shuō shì xiǎotōu, yǒu rén shuō kěnéng shì fēng bǎ xuě chuī chū le jiǎoyìn de xíngzhuàng. Zhídào yǒu yì tiān, wǒ yùdào le zhù zài dǐng lóu de Zhāng nǎinai. Tā yǒudiǎn bù hǎoyìsi de shuō: 「Shì wǒ. Zuótiān bànyè shuì bu zháo, xiǎng shàng lái kànkan xuě. Yuèguāng zhào zài xuě shàngmiàn tèbié hǎokàn, wǒ zhàn zài nàlǐ kàn le hěn jiǔ.」 Wǒ tūrán juéde, nà chuàn gūdú de jiǎoyìn biàn de wēnnuǎn le qǐlái.
text_en: It snowed all night, and in the morning I went up to the rooftop to see the snow. But to my surprise, there was already a set of footprints in the rooftop snow. The footprints went from the rooftop door to the railing and then back. Who would go up to the rooftop in the middle of the night? I asked the neighbors in the building — nobody admitted it was them. The security guard said he didn't see anyone go upstairs at night. People discussed it for days. Some said it was a thief; others said the wind might have blown the snow into footprint shapes. Until one day I ran into Grandma Zhang, who lives on the top floor. A bit embarrassed, she said: 'It was me. Last night I couldn't sleep and wanted to come up and see the snow. The moonlight on the snow was especially beautiful — I stood there looking for a long time.' Suddenly I felt those lonely footprints had become warm.

title: The Handwriting
text_zh: 整理旧东西的时候，我在抽屉里发现了一叠小时候写的作文。纸已经发黄了，但是上面的字还看得清楚。那时候我的字写得又大又歪，一行字能占两行的位置。老师用红笔在旁边写了批注：「字写得再小一点就好了。」我把这些作文和现在写的字放在一起比较，简直不敢相信是同一个人写的。现在的字小了很多，也整齐了很多，但是少了一种小时候那种不管不顾的自信。小时候写字从来不怕写错，错了就涂掉重新写。现在每次下笔之前都会想半天，怕写得不够好。
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ jiù dōngxi de shíhou, wǒ zài chōuti lǐ fāxiàn le yì dié xiǎo shíhou xiě de zuòwén. Zhǐ yǐjīng fā huáng le, dànshì shàngmiàn de zì hái kàn de qīngchu. Nà shíhou wǒ de zì xiě de yòu dà yòu wāi, yì háng zì néng zhàn liǎng háng de wèizhì. Lǎoshī yòng hóng bǐ zài pángbiān xiě le pīzhù: 「Zì xiě de zài xiǎo yìdiǎn jiù hǎo le.」 Wǒ bǎ zhèxiē zuòwén hé xiànzài xiě de zì fàng zài yìqǐ bǐjiào, jiǎnzhí bù gǎn xiāngxìn shì tóng yí gè rén xiě de. Xiànzài de zì xiǎo le hěn duō, yě zhěngqí le hěn duō, dànshì shǎo le yì zhǒng xiǎo shíhou nà zhǒng bùguǎn-bùgù de zìxìn. Xiǎo shíhou xiě zì cónglái bú pà xiě cuò, cuò le jiù tú diào chóngxīn xiě. Xiànzài měi cì xià bǐ zhīqián dōu huì xiǎng bàntiān, pà xiě de bú gòu hǎo. Yěxǔ zhǎng dà jiù shì zhèyàng——wǒmen xué huì le bǎ zì xiě de piàoliang, què wàng le xiǎo shíhou nà zhǒng xiǎng xiě jiù xiě de yǒngqì.
text_en: While sorting through old things, I found a stack of childhood essays in a drawer. The paper had yellowed, but the characters were still legible. Back then my handwriting was big and crooked — one line of characters could take up two lines' worth of space. The teacher wrote in red pen beside them: 'A bit smaller would be better.' I placed these essays next to my current handwriting and could hardly believe the same person wrote them. My current characters are much smaller and neater, but they've lost a certain fearless confidence from childhood. As a child I was never afraid of writing wrong — if I made a mistake, I'd cross it out and write again. Now I deliberate for ages before putting pen to paper, afraid it won't be good enough.

title: The Quiet After Rain
text_zh: 刚下完雨的城市有一种特别的安静。空气被洗过一样干净，能闻到泥土和树叶的味道。地上到处都是水洼，像一面面小镜子，映着天空和楼房。有几只鸟飞回来了，停在电线上叫，声音特别清脆。路上的车比刚才少了很多，只有几辆出租车慢慢地开过去，轮胎压过水洼的声音很好听。便利店门口有一个小孩子专门在踩水洼玩，每踩一下水就溅起来，他笑得很开心。他妈妈在旁边一边叹气一边笑。我站在路边等公交车，看着这一切，觉得这就是城市最真实的样子——不是白天的忙碌，不是夜晚的灯光，而是雨后那几分钟的安静和干净。可惜这种感觉很快就会消失，等太阳出来，一切又会恢复到以前的样子。
text_pinyin: Gāng xià wán yǔ de chéngshì yǒu yì zhǒng tèbié de ānjìng. Kōngqì bèi xǐ guò yíyàng gānjìng, néng wén dào nítǔ hé shùyè de wèidao. Dì shàng dàochù dōu shì shuǐwā, xiàng yí miàn miàn xiǎo jìngzi, yìngzhe tiānkōng hé lóufáng. Yǒu jǐ zhī niǎo fēi huílái le, tíng zài diànxiàn shàng jiào, shēngyīn tèbié qīngcuì. Lù shàng de chē bǐ gāngcái shǎo le hěn duō, zhǐ yǒu jǐ liàng chūzūchē mànmàn de kāi guòqù, lúntāi yā guò shuǐwā de shēngyīn hěn hǎotīng. Biànlì diàn ménkǒu yǒu yí gè xiǎo háizi zhuānmén zài cǎi shuǐwā wán, měi cǎi yíxià shuǐ jiù jiàn qǐlái, tā xiào de hěn kāixīn. Tā māma zài pángbiān yìbiān tàn qì yìbiān xiào. Wǒ zhàn zài lù biān děng gōngjiāo chē, kànzhe zhè yíqiè, juéde zhè jiù shì chéngshì zuì zhēnshí de yàngzi——búshì báitiān de mánglù, búshì yèwǎn de dēngguāng, érshì yǔ hòu nà jǐ fēnzhōng de ānjìng hé gānjìng. Kěxī zhè zhǒng gǎnjué hěn kuài jiù huì xiāoshī, děng tàiyáng chūlái, yíqiè yòu huì huīfù dào yǐqián de yàngzi.
text_en: A city right after rain has a special quiet. The air is as clean as if it's been washed — you can smell the earth and leaves. Puddles everywhere on the ground, like little mirrors reflecting the sky and buildings. A few birds have flown back, perching on power lines and calling out in crisp voices. There are far fewer cars on the road now — a few taxis driving slowly past, the sound of their tires over puddles pleasant. In front of a convenience store, a child deliberately stomps in puddles, water splashing up with each step, laughing happily. His mother stands beside him, sighing and smiling. I stand at the roadside waiting for the bus, watching all of this, feeling that this is the city's most authentic self — not the busyness of daytime, not the lights of night, but the quiet and cleanliness of those few minutes after rain.

title: The Tea Thermos
text_zh: 爸爸有一个用了十几年的旧保温杯，银色的外壳已经被磨得到处都是划痕。妈妈买了好几次新杯子给他，他都不用，还是用那个旧的。每天早上他都会泡一杯浓茶放进去，然后带着它去上班。中午的时候再泡一次，喝到晚上下班。他说这个杯子是刚工作的时候一个老同事送给他的，那个同事后来去了别的城市，他们已经很多年没有联系了。但是每天用这个杯子喝茶的时候，他就会想起那个人。我问爸爸为什么不联系那个同事，他说：「有些人不用联系也能记住。只要我还在用这个杯子，就好像他还在我身边一样。」也许对爸爸来说，这个旧保温杯已经不只是一个杯子了，它是一段友情的容器。
text_pinyin: Bàba yǒu yí gè yòng le shí jǐ nián de jiù bǎowēn bēi, yín sè de wàiké yǐjīng bèi mó de dàochù dōu shì huáhén. Māma mǎi le hǎo jǐ cì xīn bēizi gěi tā, tā dōu bú yòng, háishi yòng nàge jiù de. Měi tiān zǎoshang tā dōu huì pào yì bēi nóng chá fàng jìnqù, ránhòu dàizhe tā qù shàngbān. Zhōngwǔ de shíhou zài pào yí cì, hē dào wǎnshang xiàbān. Tā shuō zhège bēizi shì gāng gōngzuò de shíhou yí gè lǎo tóngshì sòng gěi tā de, nàge tóngshì hòulái qù le biéde chéngshì, tāmen yǐjīng hěn duō nián méiyǒu liánxì le. Dànshì měi tiān yòng zhège bēizi hē chá de shíhou, tā jiù huì xiǎng qǐ nàge rén. Wǒ wèn bàba wèishénme bù liánxì nàge tóngshì, tā shuō: 「Yǒuxiē rén búyòng liánxì yě néng jìzhù. Zhǐyào wǒ hái zài yòng zhège bēizi, jiù hǎoxiàng tā hái zài wǒ shēnbiān yíyàng.」 Yěxǔ duì bàba lái shuō, zhège jiù bǎowēn bēi yǐjīng bù zhǐ shì yí gè bēizi le, tā shì yí duàn yǒuqíng de róngqì.
text_en: My dad has an old thermos he's used for over a decade. The silver shell is covered in scratches from wear. Mom has bought him new cups many times, but he never uses them — still the old one. Every morning he brews a cup of strong tea, puts it in the thermos, and takes it to work. At noon he brews another cup, drinking through to the end of the workday. He says this cup was given to him by an old colleague when he first started working. That colleague later moved to another city, and they haven't been in touch for many years. But every day when he drinks tea from this cup, he thinks of that person. I asked Dad why he doesn't contact the colleague. He said: 'Some people you can remember without being in touch.

questions.q_en: Why doesn't Dad use the new cups Mom bought?

options.text: 因为他不喜欢换新东西

options.text: 因为旧杯子的保温效果更好
options.pinyin: yīnwèi jiù bēizi de bǎowēn xiàoguǒ gèng hǎo
options.text_en: the old cup keeps tea warmer

questions.explanation: "He says this cup was given to him" — the motivation is clear.

options.pinyin: qù le biéde chéngshì, hěn duō nián méi liánxì le
options.text_en: moved to another city, no contact for years

jīngcháng lái bàba jiā zuò kè

questions.q_en: What deeper meaning does the 'repetition' in this passage carry?
questions.explanation: Between the lines of "My dad has an old thermos he's used for over" sits the real point.

title: The GPS Argument
text_zh: 我爸开车的时候有一个习惯——喜欢跟导航吵架。导航说「请在前方路口右转」，他偏要直走，然后导航就会说「正在为您重新规划路线」。每次听到这句话，他就会不高兴地说：「我在这个城市住了三十年了，还需要你告诉我怎么走？」但是他自己选的路经常会堵车，最后还是得听导航的。妈妈坐在旁边，每次都忍不住笑。有一次我们全家出去旅游，在一个完全不认识的城市里，爸爸终于安安静静地听了导航的话。但是到了目的地以后，他还是说了一句：「其实我刚才想到了一条更近的路。」全家人都笑了。妈妈说：「你爸不是跟导航吵架，他是不愿意承认自己不认路。」
text_pinyin: Wǒ bà kāi chē de shíhou yǒu yí gè xíguàn——xǐhuan gēn dǎoháng chǎo jià. Dǎoháng shuō「qǐng zài qiánfāng lùkǒu yòu zhuǎn」, tā piān yào zhí zǒu, ránhòu dǎoháng jiù huì shuō「zhèngzài wèi nín chóngxīn guīhuà lùxiàn」. Měi cì tīng dào zhè jù huà, tā jiù huì bù gāoxìng de shuō: 「Wǒ zài zhège chéngshì zhù le sānshí nián le, hái xūyào nǐ gàosu wǒ zěnme zǒu?」 Dànshì tā zìjǐ xuǎn de lù jīngcháng huì dǔ chē, zuìhòu háishi děi tīng dǎoháng de. Māma zuò zài pángbiān, měi cì dōu rěn bu zhù xiào. Yǒu yí cì wǒmen quán jiā chūqù lǚyóu, zài yí gè wánquán bù rènshi de chéngshì lǐ, bàba zhōngyú ān'ānjìngjìng de tīng le dǎoháng de huà. Dànshì dào le mùdìdì yǐhòu, tā háishi shuō le yí jù: 「Qíshí wǒ gāngcái xiǎng dào le yì tiáo gèng jìn de lù.」 Quán jiā rén dōu xiào le. Māma shuō: 「Nǐ bà búshì gēn dǎoháng chǎo jià, tā shì bú yuànyì chéngrèn zìjǐ bú rèn lù.」
text_en: My dad has a habit when driving — he likes to argue with the GPS. When the GPS says 'please turn right at the next intersection,' he insists on going straight, and then the GPS says 'recalculating your route.' Every time he hears that, he says unhappily: 'I've lived in this city for thirty years — do I still need you to tell me where to go?' But the routes he chooses often get stuck in traffic, so he ends up following the GPS anyway. Mom sits beside him, unable to keep from laughing every time. Once our whole family went on a trip to a city we'd never been to, and Dad finally listened quietly to the GPS. But after arriving at the destination, he still said: 'Actually, I just thought of a shorter route.' The whole family laughed. Mom said: 'Your dad isn't arguing with the GPS — he just doesn't want to admit he doesn't know the way.'

questions.q_zh: 作者想通过这个故事探讨什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author want to explore through this story?

options.text: 日常生活中的深层意义
options.pinyin: rìcháng shēnghuó zhōng de shēncéng yìyì

questions.explanation: The answer sits right in "Your dad isn't arguing with the GPS —."

questions.q_en: Which detail best reflects the author's attitude?
questions.explanation: The answer sits right in "My dad has a habit when driving."

questions.q_zh: 妈妈觉得爸爸为什么跟导航吵架？
questions.q_en: Why does Mom think Dad argues with the GPS?

options.text: 因为他不愿意承认自己不认路
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā bú yuànyì chéngrèn zìjǐ bú rèn lù
options.text_en: he doesn't want to admit he doesn't know the way

options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā juéde dǎoháng tài chǎo
options.text_en: he thinks the GPS is too noisy

questions.explanation: Pay attention to "Your dad isn't arguing with the GPS —" — it answers the why.

title: The Street Sweeper's Dawn
text_zh: 凌晨四点，城市还在睡觉，清洁工已经开始工作了。她穿着橙色的工作服，推着一辆小车，沿着街道慢慢地扫。扫帚在地上的声音很有节奏，刷——刷——刷——，像一首安静的歌。她把落叶、纸片、烟头一样一样地扫进簸箕里。有时候地上有口香糖粘住了，她就蹲下来用铲子把它铲掉。等到六点的时候，整条街都被她扫得干干净净，就好像昨天的脏和乱从来没有存在过。早上出门的人走在干净的街道上，大部分人都不会想到几个小时前这里是什么样子。她就像一个隐形的人，在别人看不到的时间里，默默地把城市擦亮了。
text_pinyin: Língchén sì diǎn, chéngshì hái zài shuìjiào, qīngjié gōng yǐjīng kāishǐ gōngzuò le. Tā chuānzhe chéng sè de gōngzuòfú, tuīzhe yí liàng xiǎo chē, yánzhe jiēdào mànmàn de sǎo. Sǎozhou zài dì shàng de shēngyīn hěn yǒu jiézòu, shuā——shuā——shuā——, xiàng yì shǒu ānjìng de gē. Tā bǎ luòyè, zhǐpiàn, yāntóu yíyàng yíyàng de sǎo jìn bòji lǐ. Yǒu shíhou dì shàng yǒu kǒuxiāngtáng zhān zhù le, tā jiù dūn xiàlái yòng chǎnzi bǎ tā chǎn diào. Děng dào liù diǎn de shíhou, zhěng tiáo jiē dōu bèi tā sǎo de gāngān-jìngjìng, jiù hǎoxiàng zuótiān de zāng hé luàn cónglái méiyǒu cúnzài guò. Zǎoshang chūmén de rén zǒu zài gānjìng de jiēdào shàng, dà bùfen rén dōu bú huì xiǎng dào jǐ gè xiǎoshí qián zhèlǐ shì shénme yàngzi. Tā jiù xiàng yí gè yǐnxíng de rén, zài biéren kàn bu dào de shíjiān lǐ, mòmò de bǎ chéngshì cā liàng le.
text_en: At four in the morning, the city is still asleep, but the street sweeper has already started working. She wears an orange uniform, pushing a small cart, slowly sweeping along the street. The broom on the ground has a steady rhythm — swish, swish, swish — like a quiet song. She sweeps fallen leaves, scraps of paper, and cigarette butts one by one into her dustpan. Sometimes gum is stuck to the ground, and she crouches down to scrape it off with a shovel. By six o'clock, she's swept the entire street spotless, as if yesterday's dirt and disorder never existed. People heading out in the morning walk on clean streets, and most never think about what it looked like a few hours ago. She's like an invisible person, silently polishing the city in hours nobody sees.

questions.q_zh: 从职业选择可以看出什么？
questions.q_en: What can we infer from the career choice?
questions.explanation: "At four in the morning, the city is still asleep" quietly carries the central idea.

questions.q_en: How does this passage treat the concept of 'solitude'?

options.pinyin: bǎ tā dàng zuò yì zhǒng xiǎngshòu
options.text_en: treats it as something to enjoy

questions.explanation: "At four in the morning, the city is still asleep" walks you through it.

questions.q_zh: 这段话对工作态度的看法是什么？
questions.q_en: What view of work attitude does this passage present?

options.text_en: work is less important than life

questions.explanation: Focus on "At four in the morning, the city is still asleep" — the rest follows.

title: The Construction Fence Paintings
text_zh: 我家旁边有一片工地，施工的围挡上画了很多漂亮的画。有蓝天白云、有花和树、还有小孩子放风筝的场景。围挡把工地跟街道隔开了，如果不仔细看，你甚至会以为那里有一个小公园。我觉得这是一个很聪明的设计——工地本来是又吵又难看的，但是有了这些画，路过的人心情就不会那么差了。不但大人会多看两眼，小孩子还会站在那里找画里面的动物：「妈妈你看，这里有一只兔子！」画是谁画的我不知道，但是它们把一个原本让人讨厌的地方变成了一个有趣的地方。施工大概还要半年，等建好以后围挡就会被拆掉。到那时候，那些画也会跟着消失。不过没关系，这条街的人会记住它们的。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā pángbiān yǒu yí piàn gōngdì, shīgōng de wéidǎng shàng huà le hěn duō piàoliang de huà. Yǒu lántiān báiyún, yǒu huā hé shù, hái yǒu xiǎo háizi fàng fēngzhēng de chǎngjǐng. Wéidǎng bǎ gōngdì gēn jiēdào gé kāi le, rúguǒ bù zǐxì kàn, nǐ shènzhì huì yǐwéi nàlǐ yǒu yí gè xiǎo gōngyuán. Wǒ juéde zhè shì yí gè hěn cōngming de shèjì——gōngdì běnlái shì yòu chǎo yòu nánkàn de, dànshì yǒu le zhèxiē huà, lùguò de rén xīnqíng jiù bú huì nàme chà le. Búdàn dàrén huì duō kàn liǎng yǎn, xiǎo háizi hái huì zhàn zài nàlǐ zhǎo huà lǐmiàn de dòngwù: 「Māma nǐ kàn, zhèlǐ yǒu yì zhī tùzi!」 Huà shì shéi huà de wǒ bù zhīdào, dànshì tāmen bǎ yí gè yuánběn ràng rén tǎoyàn de dìfang biàn chéng le yí gè yǒuqù de dìfang. Shīgōng dàgài hái yào bàn nián, děng jiàn hǎo yǐhòu wéidǎng jiù huì bèi chāi diào. Dào nà shíhou, nàxiē huà yě huì gēnzhe xiāoshī. Búguò méi guānxi, zhè tiáo jiē de rén huì jìzhù tāmen de.
text_en: Next to my home there's a construction site with many beautiful paintings on the construction fence. Blue skies and white clouds, flowers and trees, and scenes of children flying kites. The fence separates the site from the street — if you don't look carefully, you might think there's a small park there. I think it's a clever design: construction sites are normally noisy and ugly, but with these paintings, passersby's moods won't be as bad. Not only do adults take a second look, but children stand there searching for animals in the paintings: 'Mom, look — there's a rabbit here!' I don't know who painted them, but they've turned what would be an annoying place into an interesting one. Construction will take about another six months; once it's finished, the fence will be torn down. At that point, the paintings will disappear too. But that's okay — the people on this street will remember them.

questions.q_zh: 围挡上画了什么？
questions.q_en: What's painted on the construction fence?

options.text: 蓝天、花草和小孩放风筝
options.pinyin: lántiān, huācǎo hé xiǎohái fàng fēngzhēng
options.text_en: blue sky, plants, kids flying kites

questions.explanation: The answer sits right in "Blue skies and white clouds, flowers and trees, and scenes."

questions.q_zh: 小孩子在围挡前面做什么？
questions.q_en: What do children do in front of the fence?

options.text_en: search for animals in the paintings

questions.q_en: The 'quiet' in this passage is not just physical silence — what else does it represent?
questions.explanation: "Next to my home there's a construction site with many" — the parallel is stated, not implied.

title: The Sleeping Taxi Driver
text_zh: 中午十二点，很多出租车停在路边，司机们在车里睡午觉。有的人把座椅放倒，用报纸盖着脸。有的人靠在方向盘上，头歪着，睡得很沉。有一个司机打开了车门，一只脚伸到外面，享受着外面吹来的风。这些出租车司机每天从早上五六点开始工作，一直到晚上十一二点。中午这一两个小时的休息，是他们一天里最放松的时候。我有时候打车的时候会问司机累不累。他们总是说不累，但是等到红灯停下来的时候，我总能看到他们偷偷地打了一个哈欠。午睡结束以后，他们会擦擦脸，喝一口水，然后重新打开计价器，继续在城市的街道上来来回回地开着。
text_pinyin: Zhōngwǔ shí'èr diǎn, hěn duō chūzūchē tíng zài lùbiān, sījīmen zài chē lǐ shuì wǔjiào. Yǒude rén bǎ zuòyǐ fàng dǎo, yòng bàozhǐ gàizhe liǎn. Yǒude rén kào zài fāngxiàngpán shàng, tóu wāizhe, shuì de hěn chén. Yǒu yí gè sījī dǎkāi le chē mén, yì zhī jiǎo shēn dào wàimiàn, xiǎngshòuzhe wàimiàn chuī lái de fēng. Zhèxiē chūzūchē sījī měi tiān cóng zǎoshang wǔ liù diǎn kāishǐ gōngzuò, yìzhí dào wǎnshang shíyī shí'èr diǎn. Zhōngwǔ zhè yī liǎng gè xiǎoshí de xiūxi, shì tāmen yì tiān lǐ zuì fàngsōng de shíhou. Wǒ yǒu shíhou dǎ chē de shíhou huì wèn sījī lèi bu lèi. Tāmen zǒng shì shuō bú lèi, dànshì děng dào hóng dēng tíng xiàlái de shíhou, wǒ zǒng néng kàn dào tāmen tōutōu de dǎ le yí gè hāqian. Wǔshuì jiéshù yǐhòu, tāmen huì cā cā liǎn, hē yì kǒu shuǐ, ránhòu chóngxīn dǎkāi jìjiàqì, jìxù zài chéngshì de jiēdào shàng láilái-huíhuí de kāizhe.
text_en: At noon, many taxis are parked by the roadside, their drivers napping inside. Some recline their seats with newspapers covering their faces. Others lean on the steering wheel, head tilted, deep asleep. One driver has opened his door, one foot sticking out, enjoying the breeze. These taxi drivers work from five or six in the morning until eleven or midnight. These one or two hours of rest at noon are the most relaxing time of their day. Sometimes when I take a taxi, I ask the driver if he's tired. They always say no, but whenever they stop at a red light, I always catch them sneaking in a yawn. After the nap ends, they wipe their faces, take a sip of water, turn the meter back on, and continue driving back and forth through the city streets.

questions.q_zh: 这段话和说明文的写法有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How does this passage differ from an expository text?

options.text_en: focuses more on emotion and experience

questions.explanation: "At noon, many taxis are parked by the roadside" reveals the how.

questions.q_zh: 司机说自己累不累的时候怎么回答？
questions.q_en: How do drivers answer when asked if they're tired?
questions.explanation: "I ask the driver if he's tired" reveals the how.

questions.q_zh: 午睡以后司机做什么？
questions.q_en: What do drivers do after their nap?

options.text: 擦脸喝水，重新开始工作
options.pinyin: cā liǎn hē shuǐ, chóngxīn kāishǐ gōngzuò
options.text_en: wipe face, drink water, start working again

questions.explanation: Go back to "After the nap ends, they wipe their faces, take." That's where the answer lives.

title: The Traffic Light Countdown
text_zh: 我每天上班路上都要经过一个特别长的红灯路口。红灯有整整九十秒，比大部分路口都长。等红灯的人反应各不相同。有的人一直盯着倒计时的数字看，好像觉得看着它就能让时间过得快一点。有的人低头看手机，等绿灯亮了才抬头。有一个送外卖的小哥总是在红灯的时候吃东西——他把面包举在一只手上，另一只手扶着电动车。我最喜欢观察的是一对年轻情侣，他们每天都在这个路口等红灯。九十秒对他们来说好像不够用——每次绿灯亮了，他们好像还没说完话就急匆匆地分开了。也许同样的九十秒对不同的人来说是不一样的：对赶时间的人来说太长了，对相爱的人来说太短了。
text_pinyin: Wǒ měi tiān shàngbān lù shàng dōu yào jīngguò yí gè tèbié cháng de hóng dēng lùkǒu. Hóng dēng yǒu zhěngzhěng jiǔshí miǎo, bǐ dà bùfen lùkǒu dōu cháng. Děng hóng dēng de rén fǎnyìng gè bù xiāngtóng. Yǒude rén yìzhí dīngzhe dàojìshí de shùzì kàn, hǎoxiàng juéde kànzhe tā jiù néng ràng shíjiān guò de kuài yìdiǎn. Yǒude rén dī tóu kàn shǒujī, děng lǜ dēng liàng le cái tái tóu. Yǒu yí gè sòng wàimài de xiǎogē zǒng shì zài hóng dēng de shíhou chī dōngxi——tā bǎ miànbāo jǔ zài yì zhī shǒu shàng, lìng yì zhī shǒu fúzhe diàndòng chē. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan guānchá de shì yí duì niánqīng qínglǚ, tāmen měi tiān dōu zài zhège lùkǒu děng hóng dēng. Jiǔshí miǎo duì tāmen lái shuō hǎoxiàng bú gòu yòng——měi cì lǜ dēng liàng le, tāmen hǎoxiàng hái méi shuō wán huà jiù jícōngcōng de fēnkāi le. Yěxǔ tóngyàng de jiǔshí miǎo duì bùtóng de rén lái shuō shì bù yíyàng de: duì gǎn shíjiān de rén lái shuō tài cháng le, duì xiāng'ài de rén lái shuō tài duǎn le.
text_en: Every day on my way to work I pass an intersection with an especially long red light — a full ninety seconds, longer than most intersections. People waiting react differently. Some stare at the countdown numbers the whole time, as if watching them could make time pass faster. Others look down at their phones and don't look up until the light turns green. One delivery rider always eats during the red light — holding bread in one hand, the other steadying his electric scooter. My favorite people to watch are a young couple who wait at this intersection every day. Ninety seconds seems not enough for them — every time the light turns green, they seem to rush apart before finishing their conversation. Perhaps the same ninety seconds means different things to different people: too long for those in a hurry, too short for those in love.

questions.q_zh: 这个红灯有多长？
questions.q_en: How long is this red light?
questions.explanation: "Every day on my way to work I pass" walks you through it.

questions.q_zh: 重复的行为暗示了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the repeated behavior imply?

options.text_en: this matters a lot to them

options.pinyin: tā méiyǒu bié de shì zuò
options.text_en: they have nothing else to do
others require them to do it

questions.explanation: The key is "Every day on my way to work I pass" — read it with the answer in mind.

questions.q_zh: 「我」觉得九十秒对什么人来说太短了？
questions.q_en: For whom does 'I' think ninety seconds is too short?
questions.explanation: The key is "Perhaps the same ninety seconds means different things to different" — read it with the answer in mind.

title: The Worn-Out Doorstep
text_zh: 奶奶家的大门有一道石头门槛，已经被踩得很光滑了。奶奶说这个门槛比她的年纪还大，是爷爷的爷爷那时候建的。几十年来，一家人的脚一遍又一遍地踩过这道门槛——出门上学，出门工作，出门结婚，最后又回来。门槛中间最低的地方是被踩得最多的，已经凹下去了一小块。每次我跨过这道门槛，都能感受到一种说不出来的踏实感。有时候回家的时候天已经很黑了，但是只要脚踩到那个光滑的石头，我就知道自己到家了。去年奶奶家翻修房子，工人说门槛太旧了要换一个新的。但是奶奶坚决不同意：「这是全家人的脚印磨出来的，换了就不是我们家的门了。」最后工人只好绕着它把别的地方修好了。
text_pinyin: Nǎinai jiā de dà mén yǒu yí dào shítou ménkǎn, yǐjīng bèi cǎi de hěn guānghuá le. Nǎinai shuō zhège ménkǎn bǐ tā de niánjì hái dà, shì yéye de yéye nà shíhou jiàn de. Jǐ shí nián lái, yì jiā rén de jiǎo yí biàn yòu yí biàn de cǎi guò zhè dào ménkǎn——chūmén shàng xué, chūmén gōngzuò, chūmén jiéhūn, zuìhòu yòu huílái. Ménkǎn zhōngjiān zuì dī de dìfang shì bèi cǎi de zuì duō de, yǐjīng āo xiàqù le yì xiǎo kuài. Měi cì wǒ kuà guò zhè dào ménkǎn, dōu néng gǎnshòu dào yì zhǒng shuō bu chūlái de tàshígǎn. Yǒu shíhou huí jiā de shíhou tiān yǐjīng hěn hēi le, dànshì zhǐyào jiǎo cǎi dào nàge guānghuá de shítou, wǒ jiù zhīdào zìjǐ dào jiā le. Qùnián nǎinai jiā fānxiū fángzi, gōngrén shuō ménkǎn tài jiù le yào huàn yí gè xīn de. Dànshì nǎinai jiānjué bù tóngyì: 「Zhè shì quán jiā rén de jiǎoyìn mó chūlái de, huàn le jiù búshì wǒmen jiā de mén le.」 Zuìhòu gōngrén zhǐhǎo ràozhe tā bǎ biéde dìfang xiū hǎo le.
text_en: Grandmother's front door has a stone threshold, worn smooth from years of footsteps. Grandmother says this threshold is older than she is — built in her grandfather's grandfather's time. For decades, the family's feet have stepped over this threshold again and again — leaving for school, leaving for work, leaving to marry, and finally returning. The lowest spot in the middle, where feet have trod most, has been worn into a small indentation. Every time I step over this threshold, I feel an indescribable sense of solidity. Sometimes I come home when it's already dark, but as soon as my foot touches that smooth stone, I know I'm home. Last year, when renovating Grandmother's house, the workers said the threshold was too old and should be replaced. But Grandmother firmly refused: 'This was worn smooth by the whole family's footprints. Replace it and it's no longer our door.' In the end, the workers had to renovate everything else around it.

questions.q_zh: 这些人之间最重要的是什么？
questions.q_en: What matters most between these people?
questions.explanation: Go back to "Grandmother's front door has a stone threshold, worn smooth." That's where the answer lives.

questions.q_zh: 门槛中间为什么凹下去了？
questions.q_en: Why is the middle of the threshold indented?

options.text: 因为石头本来就是这个形状
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shítou běnlái jiù shì zhège xíngzhuàng
options.text_en: the stone was always this shape

options.pinyin: yīnwèi bèi cǎi de zuì duō
options.text_en: it's been stepped on the most

questions.explanation: "For decades, the family's feet have stepped over this threshold" — the motivation is clear.

questions.q_zh: 奶奶为什么不同意换门槛？
questions.q_en: Why did grandmother refuse to replace the threshold?

options.text: 因为门槛是全家人的脚印磨出来的
options.pinyin: yīnwèi ménkǎn shì quán jiā rén de jiǎoyìn mó chūlái de
options.text_en: it was worn by the whole family's footprints

title: The Village Clinic
text_zh: 我们村里有一个小诊所，只有一个医生——赵叔叔。他在这个村子当了三十年的医生，村里几乎每个人都看过他的病。诊所很小，只有两间房，一间是看病的，一间是放药的。墙上贴着已经发黄的健康知识海报，桌子上放着一个老式的血压计。赵叔叔不但给人看病，还经常骑着自行车去那些走不动路的老人家里出诊。他的手机号码全村人都知道，无论白天还是半夜，只要有人打电话说不舒服，他就会赶过去。村里年轻人越来越少了，留下来的大部分是老人。有人劝赵叔叔去城里的大医院工作，工资高很多。他摇摇头说：「我走了，这些老人怎么办？只要他们还在，我就在。」
text_pinyin: Wǒmen cūn lǐ yǒu yí gè xiǎo zhěnsuǒ, zhǐ yǒu yí gè yīshēng——Zhào shūshu. Tā zài zhège cūnzi dāng le sānshí nián de yīshēng, cūn lǐ jīhū měi gè rén dōu kàn guò tā de bìng. Zhěnsuǒ hěn xiǎo, zhǐ yǒu liǎng jiān fáng, yì jiān shì kàn bìng de, yì jiān shì fàng yào de. Qiáng shàng tiēzhe yǐjīng fā huáng de jiànkāng zhīshi hǎibào, zhuōzi shàng fàngzhe yí gè lǎoshì de xuèyājì. Zhào shūshu búdàn gěi rén kàn bìng, hái jīngcháng qízhe zìxíngchē qù nàxiē zǒu bu dòng lù de lǎorén jiā lǐ chū zhěn. Tā de shǒujī hàomǎ quán cūn rén dōu zhīdào, wúlùn báitiān háishi bànyè, zhǐyào yǒu rén dǎ diànhuà shuō bù shūfu, tā jiù huì gǎn guòqù. Cūn lǐ niánqīngrén yuè lái yuè shǎo le, liú xiàlái de dà bùfen shì lǎorén. Yǒu rén quàn Zhào shūshu qù chéng lǐ de dà yīyuàn gōngzuò, gōngzī gāo hěn duō. Tā yáoyao tóu shuō: 「Wǒ zǒu le, zhèxiē lǎorén zěnme bàn? Zhǐyào tāmen hái zài, wǒ jiù zài.」
text_en: Our village has a small clinic with only one doctor — Uncle Zhao. He's been the village doctor for thirty years; nearly everyone in the village has been his patient. The clinic is tiny — two rooms, one for seeing patients, one for storing medicine. On the wall hang health education posters that have yellowed with age; on the desk sits an old-fashioned blood pressure monitor. Uncle Zhao not only treats patients at the clinic but often rides his bicycle to make house calls on elderly villagers who can't walk. His phone number is known by everyone in the village. No matter if it's daytime or midnight, whenever someone calls saying they feel unwell, he hurries over. Fewer and fewer young people remain in the village; those left behind are mostly elderly. Some have urged Uncle Zhao to work at a big city hospital, where the salary is much higher. He shakes his head and says: 'If I leave, what will these elderly people do?

questions.q_zh: 这种坚持说明这个人怎么样？
questions.q_en: What does this persistence tell us about the person?

options.text_en: has no ideas of their own

questions.explanation: "Our village has a small clinic with only one doctor" — once you spot this, the answer clicks.

questions.q_zh: 赵叔叔怎么去老人家里看病？
questions.q_en: How does Uncle Zhao visit elderly patients at home?
questions.explanation: Look at "Uncle Zhao not only treats patients at the clinic" — it shows the mechanism.

questions.q_zh: 赵叔叔为什么不去城里的大医院？
questions.q_en: Why won't Uncle Zhao go to a big city hospital?

options.text: 因为担心村里的老人没人看病
options.pinyin: yīnwèi dānxīn cūn lǐ de lǎorén méi rén kàn bìng
options.text_en: worries about elderly villagers having no doctor

options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā de jìshù bú gòu hǎo

options.text: 因为他习惯了农村生活
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā xíguàn le nóngcūn shēnghuó

questions.explanation: Look at "Uncle Zhao not only treats patients at the clinic" — the reason is right there.

title: The Garden That Waters Itself
text_zh: 我出差两个星期回来以后，发现阳台上的花居然还活着，而且长得比我走之前还好。这让我非常奇怪，因为走之前我忘了让人帮我浇水。我检查了一下花盆，泥土是湿的。是谁进来给我的花浇了水？我家的门锁没有被打开过的痕迹，窗户也都关着。我问了物业和邻居，没有人知道是怎么回事。直到有一天下雨，我才发现了答案：原来阳台上面有一根空调的排水管，每次开空调的时候，水就会顺着管子滴到下面。而我的花盆刚好就在那根管子的正下方。也就是说，楼上的邻居每天开空调，就等于在帮我浇花。我跑去告诉楼上的邻居，他笑着说：「这么说，我还算半个园丁了？」
text_pinyin: Wǒ chūchāi liǎng gè xīngqī huílái yǐhòu, fāxiàn yángtái shàng de huā jūrán hái huózhe, érqiě zhǎng de bǐ wǒ zǒu zhīqián hái hǎo. Zhè ràng wǒ fēicháng qíguài, yīnwèi zǒu zhīqián wǒ wàng le ràng rén bāng wǒ jiāo shuǐ. Wǒ jiǎnchá le yíxià huāpén, nítǔ shì shī de. Shì shéi jìnlái gěi wǒ de huā jiāo le shuǐ? Wǒ jiā de mén suǒ méiyǒu bèi dǎkāi guò de hénjì, chuānghu yě dōu guānzhe. Wǒ wèn le wùyè hé línjū, méiyǒu rén zhīdào shì zěnme huí shì. Zhídào yǒu yì tiān xià yǔ, wǒ cái fāxiàn le dá'àn: yuánlái yángtái shàngmiàn yǒu yì gēn kōngtiáo de páishuǐ guǎn, měi cì kāi kōngtiáo de shíhou, shuǐ jiù huì shùnzhe guǎnzi dī dào xiàmiàn. Ér wǒ de huāpén gānghǎo jiù zài nà gēn guǎnzi de zhèng xiàfāng. Yě jiù shì shuō, lóu shàng de línjū měi tiān kāi kōngtiáo, jiù děngyú zài bāng wǒ jiāo huā. Wǒ pǎo qù gàosu lóu shàng de línjū, tā xiàozhe shuō: 「Zhème shuō, wǒ hái suàn bàn gè yuándīng le?」
text_en: After a two-week business trip, I came home to find the flowers on my balcony still alive — and actually growing better than before I left. This was very strange, because I'd forgotten to ask anyone to water them. I checked the flowerpots — the soil was moist. Who came in to water my flowers? My door lock showed no signs of being opened; all windows were closed. I asked property management and neighbors — nobody knew what happened. Until one rainy day, I found the answer: there's an air conditioner drainage pipe above my balcony, and every time the AC runs, water drips down through the pipe. And my flowerpot happens to be directly beneath that pipe. In other words, my upstairs neighbor running the AC every day was watering my flowers. I ran upstairs to tell my neighbor, and he laughed: 'So I'm half a gardener now?'

questions.q_zh: 「我」出差回来以后花怎么样？
questions.q_en: How were the flowers after 'I' returned from the trip?

options.text_en: still alive and growing even better

questions.q_zh: 花被谁浇了水？
questions.q_en: What watered the flowers?

options.text: 楼上空调排水管滴下来的水
options.pinyin: lóu shàng kōngtiáo páishuǐ guǎn dī xiàlái de shuǐ
options.text_en: water dripping from upstairs AC drainage

questions.q_zh: 楼上的邻居听完以后说什么？
questions.q_en: What did the upstairs neighbor say after hearing the story?

options.pinyin: tā suàn bàn gè yuándīng le
tā yào bǎ guǎnzi yí zǒu

questions.explanation: Go back to "So I'm half a gardener now?." That's where the answer lives.

title: The Noodle Shop's Secret Menu
text_zh: 我家附近有一家开了二十年的面馆，菜单上只有八种面。但是老客人都知道，这里其实还有一个「隐藏菜单」。只要你跟老板说「来一碗老样子」，他就会做一碗菜单上没有的特别面条。每个老客人的「老样子」都不一样：有人喜欢多放辣椒，有人喜欢加一个煎蛋，有人不要葱。老板都记得清清楚楚。有一次我带了一个新朋友来，他看着菜单选了半天。旁边的老客人笑着说：「别看菜单了，让老板给你做一碗就行了。」我的朋友半信半疑地点了点头。结果老板看了他一眼，问了一句：「你是不是不吃香菜？」我朋友惊讶地说：「你怎么知道的？」老板笑笑说：「做了二十年面条，看一眼就知道了。」
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā fùjìn yǒu yì jiā kāi le èrshí nián de miànguǎn, càidān shàng zhǐ yǒu bā zhǒng miàn. Dànshì lǎo kèrén dōu zhīdào, zhèlǐ qíshí hái yǒu yí gè「yǐncáng càidān」. Zhǐyào nǐ gēn lǎobǎn shuō「lái yì wǎn lǎo yàngzi」, tā jiù huì zuò yì wǎn càidān shàng méiyǒu de tèbié miàntiáo. Měi gè lǎo kèrén de「lǎo yàngzi」dōu bù yíyàng: yǒu rén xǐhuan duō fàng làjiāo, yǒu rén xǐhuan jiā yí gè jiān dàn, yǒu rén búyào cōng. Lǎobǎn dōu jìde qīngqīng-chǔchǔ. Yǒu yí cì wǒ dài le yí gè xīn péngyou lái, tā kànzhe càidān xuǎn le bàntiān. Pángbiān de lǎo kèrén xiàozhe shuō: 「Bié kàn càidān le, ràng lǎobǎn gěi nǐ zuò yì wǎn jiù xíng le.」 Wǒ de péngyou bànxìn-bànyí de diǎn le diǎn tóu. Jiéguǒ lǎobǎn kàn le tā yì yǎn, wèn le yí jù: 「Nǐ shì bu shì bù chī xiāngcài?」 Wǒ péngyou jīngyà de shuō: 「Nǐ zěnme zhīdào de?」 Lǎobǎn xiàoxiao shuō: 「Zuò le èrshí nián miàntiáo, kàn yì yǎn jiù zhīdào le.」
text_en: Near my home there's a noodle shop that's been open twenty years, with only eight types of noodles on the menu. But regular customers know there's actually a 'hidden menu.' Just tell the boss 'give me the usual' and he'll make a special bowl that's not on the menu. Every regular's 'usual' is different: some like extra chili, some like a fried egg added, some want no scallions. The boss remembers every detail. Once I brought a new friend, who spent ages studying the menu. A regular nearby smiled and said: 'Forget the menu — just let the boss make you a bowl.' My friend nodded, half-skeptical. The boss glanced at him and asked: 'You don't eat cilantro, right?' My friend said in amazement: 'How did you know?' The boss smiled: 'After twenty years of making noodles, you can tell at a glance.'

questions.q_zh: 这段话会让不同的人有不同的理解吗？
questions.q_en: Would different people interpret this passage differently?

options.text: 是的，因为涉及个人经历

options.text_en: yes, because it involves personal experience

questions.explanation: Focus on "Every regular's 'usual' is different: some like extra chili, some" — the rest follows.

questions.q_zh: 怎么点「隐藏菜单」上的面？
questions.q_en: How do you order from the 'hidden menu'?

options.text_en: tell the boss 'give me the usual'

options.text_en: twenty years' experience, can tell at a glance

title: The Wrong Stop
text_zh: 上个月我去一个从来没去过的城市出差，坐地铁的时候不小心下错了站。当我走出地铁站的时候，发现自己在一条完全不认识的街上。我本来应该着急的，但是那条街实在太好看了——两边都是旧式的楼房，楼下是各种各样的小店：一家卖旧书的、一家修表的、一家卖手工点心的。街上的人不多，走路的速度都很慢，好像没有人赶时间。我决定不马上回去，而是沿着这条街慢慢走一走。我买了一块点心，一边吃一边逛。拐了一个弯，发现了一个小公园，公园里有一棵开满花的树。我在树下坐了一会儿，想着如果没有下错站，我就不会发现这个地方。
text_pinyin: Shàng gè yuè wǒ qù yí gè cónglái méi qù guò de chéngshì chūchāi, zuò dìtiě de shíhou bù xiǎoxīn xià cuò le zhàn. Dāng wǒ zǒu chū dìtiě zhàn de shíhou, fāxiàn zìjǐ zài yì tiáo wánquán bú rènshi de jiē shàng. Wǒ běnlái yīnggāi zhāojí de, dànshì nà tiáo jiē shízài tài hǎokàn le——liǎng biān dōu shì jiùshì de lóufáng, lóu xià shì gè zhǒng gè yàng de xiǎo diàn: yì jiā mài jiù shū de, yì jiā xiū biǎo de, yì jiā mài shǒugōng diǎnxin de. Jiē shàng de rén bù duō, zǒulù de sùdù dōu hěn màn, hǎoxiàng méiyǒu rén gǎn shíjiān. Wǒ juédìng bù mǎshàng huíqù, érshì yánzhe zhè tiáo jiē mànmàn zǒu yì zǒu. Wǒ mǎi le yí kuài diǎnxin, yìbiān chī yìbiān guàng. Guǎi le yí gè wān, fāxiàn le yí gè xiǎo gōngyuán, gōngyuán lǐ yǒu yì kē kāi mǎn huā de shù. Wǒ zài shù xià zuò le yíhuìr, xiǎngzhe rúguǒ méiyǒu xià cuò zhàn, wǒ jiù bú huì fāxiàn zhège dìfang. Yěxǔ yǒu shíhou zǒu cuò lù búshì yí jiàn huài shì, tā zhǐ shì shēnghuó gěi nǐ ānpái de yí cì yìwài de lǚxíng.
text_en: Last month I went on a business trip to a city I'd never visited and accidentally got off at the wrong subway stop. When I walked out, I found myself on a completely unfamiliar street. I should have been anxious, but the street was just too beautiful — old-style buildings on both sides, with all sorts of small shops on the ground floors: a used bookshop, a watch repair shop, a handmade pastry shop. Few people were on the street, everyone walking slowly, as if nobody was in a hurry. I decided not to go back immediately but to stroll along the street. I bought a pastry and ate while wandering. Around a corner, I discovered a small park with a tree in full bloom. I sat under the tree for a while, thinking: if I hadn't gotten off at the wrong stop, I never would have found this place.

title: The Accent That Returns
text_zh: 我在上海住了十年，说话已经听不出是哪里人了。但是每年过年回老家的时候，一件奇怪的事情就会发生：我的口音会慢慢地回来。刚下火车的时候，我还在说普通话。但是到了第二天，跟亲戚聊了几个小时以后，我就开始不自觉地用家乡话了。到了第三天，我说话的方式完全变了——不但口音回来了，连说话的速度和习惯用的词都变了。妈妈每次都笑着说：「你看，这才是真正的你。」等到假期结束回上海的时候，口音又会在三四天以内慢慢消失。我觉得口音像一件看不见的衣服：在外面的时候你把它脱掉了，但是一回到家，它就会自己穿回来。不管你离开了多久，家乡的语言永远住在你身体的某个角落里。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài Shànghǎi zhù le shí nián, shuō huà yǐjīng tīng bu chū shì nǎlǐ rén le. Dànshì měi nián guò nián huí lǎojiā de shíhou, yí jiàn qíguài de shìqíng jiù huì fāshēng: wǒ de kǒuyīn huì mànmàn de huílái. Gāng xià huǒchē de shíhou, wǒ hái zài shuō pǔtōnghuà. Dànshì dào le dì èr tiān, gēn qīnqi liáo le jǐ gè xiǎoshí yǐhòu, wǒ jiù kāishǐ bú zìjué de yòng jiāxiāng huà le. Dào le dì sān tiān, wǒ shuō huà de fāngshì wánquán biàn le——búdàn kǒuyīn huílái le, lián shuō huà de sùdù hé xíguàn yòng de cí dōu biàn le. Māma měi cì dōu xiàozhe shuō: 「Nǐ kàn, zhè cái shì zhēnzhèng de nǐ.」 Děng dào jiàqī jiéshù huí Shànghǎi de shíhou, kǒuyīn yòu huì zài sān sì tiān yǐnèi mànmàn xiāoshī. Wǒ juéde kǒuyīn xiàng yí jiàn kàn bu jiàn de yīfu: zài wàimiàn de shíhou nǐ bǎ tā tuō diào le, dànshì yí huí dào jiā, tā jiù huì zìjǐ chuān huílái. Bùguǎn nǐ líkāi le duō jiǔ, jiāxiāng de yǔyán yǒngyuǎn zhù zài nǐ shēntǐ de mǒu gè jiǎoluò lǐ.
text_en: I've lived in Shanghai for ten years and my speech no longer reveals where I'm from. But every Chinese New Year when I go home, something strange happens: my accent slowly comes back. When I first step off the train, I'm still speaking standard Mandarin. But by the second day, after chatting with relatives for hours, I unconsciously slip into my hometown dialect. By the third day, my way of speaking has completely changed — not only the accent, but even the speed and the words I use. Mom always smiles and says: 'See, this is the real you.' When the holiday ends and I return to Shanghai, the accent fades again within three or four days. I think an accent is like an invisible piece of clothing: when you're away, you take it off, but the moment you get home, it puts itself back on. No matter how long you've been gone, your hometown's language always lives in some corner of your body.
