title: The Bookstore That Stays Open Late
text_zh: 大学旁边有一家书店，每天晚上十二点才关门。老板说他开到这么晚，是因为有些学生写论文写到半夜，想出来透透气、翻翻书。书店不大，但是书的种类很多。靠窗的位置放了两张旧沙发，谁都可以坐在那里看书，不一定要买。老板养了一只黑猫，晚上它就趴在收银台旁边睡觉。来这里的人大部分都不怎么说话，各自安静地翻着书。有时候整个晚上只卖出去两三本书，但是老板说他不在乎。他说：「一个城市如果连一家深夜书店都没有，那这个城市就太没有意思了。」我觉得他说得对。这家书店不只是在卖书，它在给那些睡不着的人提供一个安静的去处。
text_pinyin: Dàxué pángbiān yǒu yì jiā shūdiàn, měi tiān wǎnshang shí'èr diǎn cái guān mén. Lǎobǎn shuō tā kāi dào zhème wǎn, shì yīnwèi yǒuxiē xuéshēng xiě lùnwén xiě dào bànyè, xiǎng chūlái tòutou qì, fānfan shū. Shūdiàn bú dà, dànshì shū de zhǒnglèi hěn duō. Kào chuāng de wèizhì fàng le liǎng zhāng jiù shāfā, shéi dōu kěyǐ zuò zài nàlǐ kàn shū, bù yídìng yào mǎi. Lǎobǎn yǎng le yì zhī hēi māo, wǎnshang tā jiù pā zài shōuyíntái pángbiān shuìjiào. Lái zhèlǐ de rén dà bùfen dōu bù zěnme shuō huà, gèzì ānjìng de fānzhe shū. Yǒu shíhou zhěnggè wǎnshang zhǐ mài chūqù liǎng sān běn shū, dànshì lǎobǎn shuō tā bú zàihu. Tā shuō: 「Yí gè chéngshì rúguǒ lián yì jiā shēnyè shūdiàn dōu méiyǒu, nà zhège chéngshì jiù tài méiyǒu yìsi le.」 Wǒ juéde tā shuō de duì. Zhè jiā shūdiàn bù zhǐ shì zài mài shū, tā zài gěi nàxiē shuì bu zháo de rén tígōng yí gè ānjìng de qùchù.
text_en: Next to the university there's a bookstore that doesn't close until midnight. The owner says he stays open this late because some students write papers until the middle of the night and want to come out for air and browse books. The store isn't big, but it has many kinds of books. By the window are two old sofas where anyone can sit and read — no need to buy anything. The owner keeps a black cat that sleeps by the register at night. Most people who come here don't talk much, each quietly flipping through books. Sometimes only two or three books are sold in an entire evening, but the owner says he doesn't care. He says: 'If a city can't even have one late-night bookstore, then that city is far too boring.' I think he's right. This bookstore isn't just selling books — it's providing a quiet place for those who can't sleep.

title: The Fog in the Park
text_zh: 今天早上起了大雾，整个城市都被白色的雾包住了。我去公园跑步的时候，发现平时熟悉的公园变得完全不一样了。十米以外的东西都看不清楚，树变成了模糊的影子，湖面上的雾就像一层白色的棉被。跑步的人比平时少了很多，整个公园安静得像一个梦。我跑着跑着，突然从雾里走出来一个老人，吓了我一跳。他笑着说：「别怕，我也是来跑步的。」我们就这样一起跑了一会儿。雾慢慢散了以后，阳光照进来，公园又变回了平时的样子。树叶上全是露水，在阳光下闪闪发光。那个老人朝我挥了挥手，消失在人群里。我觉得今天早上的雾，让我看到了这个公园另一个不常见的表情。
text_pinyin: Jīntiān zǎoshang qǐ le dà wù, zhěnggè chéngshì dōu bèi bái sè de wù bāo zhù le. Wǒ qù gōngyuán pǎobù de shíhou, fāxiàn píngshí shúxī de gōngyuán biàn de wánquán bù yíyàng le. Shí mǐ yǐwài de dōngxi dōu kàn bu qīngchu, shù biàn chéng le móhu de yǐngzi, húmiàn shàng de wù jiù xiàng yì céng bái sè de miánbèi. Pǎobù de rén bǐ píngshí shǎo le hěn duō, zhěnggè gōngyuán ānjìng de xiàng yí gè mèng. Wǒ pǎozhe pǎozhe, tūrán cóng wù lǐ zǒu chūlái yí gè lǎorén, xià le wǒ yí tiào. Tā xiàozhe shuō: 「Bié pà, wǒ yě shì lái pǎobù de.」 Wǒmen jiù zhèyàng yìqǐ pǎo le yíhuìr. Wù mànmàn sàn le yǐhòu, yángguāng zhào jìnlái, gōngyuán yòu biàn huí le píngshí de yàngzi. Shùyè shàng quán shì lùshuǐ, zài yángguāng xià shǎnshǎn fāguāng. Nàge lǎorén cháo wǒ huī le huī shǒu, xiāoshī zài rénqún lǐ. Wǒ juéde jīntiān zǎoshang de wù, ràng wǒ kàn dào le zhège gōngyuán lìng yí gè bù chángjiàn de biǎoqíng.
text_en: This morning there was heavy fog, wrapping the whole city in white. When I went to the park to jog, the familiar park looked completely different. You couldn't see clearly beyond ten meters — trees became blurry shadows, and the fog over the lake was like a white quilt. Far fewer joggers than usual; the whole park was as quiet as a dream. As I ran, an old man suddenly emerged from the fog, startling me. He smiled and said: 'Don't be scared — I'm here to jog too.' We ran together for a while. As the fog slowly cleared and sunlight came through, the park returned to its usual self. The leaves were all covered in dew, sparkling in the sunlight. The old man waved at me and disappeared into the crowd. I think this morning's fog showed me another, rarely seen expression of this park.

title: The Pedestrian Tunnel
text_zh: 火车站前面有一条地下通道，连接着车站和对面的商业区。通道不长，大概只有一百米，但是里面有自己的生态。右边的墙上贴满了各种广告——找工作的、租房子的、学英语的。左边有几个小摊位：一个卖手机壳的、一个卖袜子的、一个帮人擦鞋的。最里面有一个拉二胡的老人，琴声在通道里回响，被放大了好几倍。赶火车的人提着行李箱快步走过，有时候箱子的轮子声和二胡声混在一起，形成一种奇怪的音乐。这条通道虽然不好看，但是它很真实。你可以在这里看到一个城市最底层的样子——忙碌的、粗糙的、但是充满了生活的力量。
text_pinyin: Huǒchē zhàn qiánmiàn yǒu yì tiáo dìxià tōngdào, liánjiēzhe chēzhàn hé duìmiàn de shāngyè qū. Tōngdào bù cháng, dàgài zhǐ yǒu yìbǎi mǐ, dànshì lǐmiàn yǒu zìjǐ de shēngtài. Yòubiān de qiáng shàng tiē mǎn le gè zhǒng guǎnggào——zhǎo gōngzuò de, zū fángzi de, xué Yīngyǔ de. Zuǒbiān yǒu jǐ gè xiǎo tānwèi: yí gè mài shǒujī ké de, yí gè mài wàzi de, yí gè bāng rén cā xié de. Zuì lǐmiàn yǒu yí gè lā èrhú de lǎorén, qín shēng zài tōngdào lǐ huíxiǎng, bèi fàngdà le hǎo jǐ bèi. Gǎn huǒchē de rén tízhe xíngli xiāng kuàibù zǒu guò, yǒu shíhou xiāngzi de lúnzi shēng hé èrhú shēng hùn zài yìqǐ, xíngchéng yì zhǒng qíguài de yīnyuè. Zhè tiáo tōngdào suīrán bù hǎokàn, dànshì tā hěn zhēnshí. Nǐ kěyǐ zài zhèlǐ kàn dào yí gè chéngshì zuì dǐcéng de yàngzi——mánglù de, cūcāo de, dànshì chōngmǎn le shēnghuó de lìliàng.
text_en: In front of the train station there's an underground tunnel connecting the station to the commercial district across the street. The tunnel isn't long — only about a hundred meters — but it has its own ecosystem. The right wall is plastered with ads: job postings, apartment rentals, English courses. On the left are a few small stalls: one selling phone cases, one selling socks, one shining shoes. At the far end, an old man plays the erhu, the music echoing and amplified several times over in the tunnel. People rushing for trains stride past with their suitcases, sometimes the sound of luggage wheels mixing with the erhu into a strange kind of music. This tunnel isn't pretty, but it's real. Here you can see a city's most grassroots face — busy, rough, but full of life's energy.

title: The Weathered Signboard
text_zh: 我们这条街上有很多老店的招牌已经褪色了。最老的那家杂货店，招牌上的字只能看到一半，但是邻居们都知道它叫「老王杂货」。旁边的面馆，招牌上的红色变成了粉红色，但是味道一点都没变。再往前走有一家裁缝店，招牌被太阳晒了二十多年，字已经快看不见了。有人建议换一块新招牌，老板摇摇头说：「新的招牌反而让老客人找不到我们了。」我觉得他说得有道理。这些褪色的招牌不是在变旧，而是在变老——就像人一样，老了不一定难看，反而多了一种时间的味道。如果有一天这条街的招牌全部换成了发光的新招牌，我一定会觉得少了什么东西。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen zhè tiáo jiē shàng yǒu hěn duō lǎo diàn de zhāopai yǐjīng tuìsè le. Zuì lǎo de nà jiā záhuò diàn, zhāopai shàng de zì zhǐ néng kàn dào yíbàn, dànshì línjūmen dōu zhīdào tā jiào「Lǎo Wáng Záhuò」. Pángbiān de miànguǎn, zhāopai shàng de hóng sè biàn chéng le fěnhóng sè, dànshì wèidao yìdiǎn dōu méi biàn. Zài wǎng qián zǒu yǒu yì jiā cáiféng diàn, zhāopai bèi tàiyáng shài le èrshí duō nián, zì yǐjīng kuài kàn bu jiàn le. Yǒu rén jiànyì huàn yí kuài xīn zhāopai, lǎobǎn yáoyao tóu shuō: 「Xīn de zhāopai fǎn'ér ràng lǎo kèrén zhǎo bu dào wǒmen le.」 Wǒ juéde tā shuō de yǒu dàoli. Zhèxiē tuìsè de zhāopai búshì zài biàn jiù, érshì zài biàn lǎo——jiù xiàng rén yíyàng, lǎo le bù yídìng nánkàn, fǎn'ér duō le yì zhǒng shíjiān de wèidao. Rúguǒ yǒu yì tiān zhè tiáo jiē de zhāopai quánbù huàn chéng le fā guāng de xīn zhāopai, wǒ yídìng huì juéde shǎo le shénme dōngxi. Yěxǔ shǎo de jiù shì zhè zhǒng ràng rén gǎn dào ānxīn de jiù gǎnjué.
text_en: Many old shops on our street have faded signboards. The oldest general store's sign is only half-legible, but every neighbor knows it's called 'Old Wang's.' The noodle shop next door — its red sign has turned pink, but the taste hasn't changed a bit. Further on is a tailor shop whose sign has been baked by the sun for over twenty years, the characters nearly invisible. Someone suggested getting a new sign, but the owner shook his head: 'A new sign would make our old customers unable to find us.' I think he has a point. These faded signs aren't becoming old — they're growing old, like people: aging doesn't necessarily mean ugly; it adds a flavor of time. If someday all the signs on this street were replaced with glowing new ones, I'd definitely feel something was missing.

questions.q_zh: 裁缝店的招牌为什么字看不见了？
questions.q_en: Why are the characters on the tailor shop sign barely visible?

options.pinyin: bèi tàiyáng shài le èrshí duō nián

questions.explanation: Once you read "Further on is a tailor shop whose sign has been," the cause clicks.

title: The Convenience Store at Three AM
text_zh: 我因为失眠，凌晨三点出门去便利店买水。推开门的时候，里面只有一个店员和一个客人。店员是个年轻的男生，正在低头整理货架。客人是一个穿着护士服的女生，站在冷柜前面，犹豫着买什么。灯光又白又亮，照在空空的货架上，有一种不真实的感觉。我拿了一瓶水走到收银台，店员抬头看了我一眼，什么也没说。我付完钱以后没有马上走，而是坐在门口的小桌子旁边喝水。那个护士也买好了东西，坐在我对面，打开了一盒饭团慢慢地吃。我们两个人谁都没说话，但是在凌晨三点的便利店里，两个陌生人的沉默反而让人觉得不孤单。有时候，不说话也是一种陪伴。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yīnwèi shīmián, língchén sān diǎn chūmén qù biànlì diàn mǎi shuǐ. Tuī kāi mén de shíhou, lǐmiàn zhǐ yǒu yí gè diànyuán hé yí gè kèrén. Diànyuán shì ge niánqīng de nánshēng, zhèngzài dī tóu zhěnglǐ huòjià. Kèrén shì yí gè chuānzhe hùshi fú de nǚshēng, zhàn zài lěngguì qiánmiàn, yóuyùzhe mǎi shénme. Dēngguāng yòu bái yòu liàng, zhào zài kōngkōng de huòjià shàng, yǒu yì zhǒng bù zhēnshí de gǎnjué. Wǒ ná le yì píng shuǐ zǒu dào shōuyíntái, diànyuán tái tóu kàn le wǒ yì yǎn, shénme yě méi shuō. Wǒ fù wán qián yǐhòu méiyǒu mǎshàng zǒu, érshì zuò zài ménkǒu de xiǎo zhuōzi pángbiān hē shuǐ. Nàge hùshi yě mǎi hǎo le dōngxi, zuò zài wǒ duìmiàn, dǎkāi le yì hé fàntuán mànmàn de chī. Wǒmen liǎng gè rén shéi dōu méi shuō huà, dànshì zài língchén sān diǎn de biànlì diàn lǐ, liǎng gè mòshēngrén de chénmò fǎn'ér ràng rén juéde bù gūdān. Yǒu shíhou, bù shuō huà yě shì yì zhǒng péibàn.
text_en: Because of insomnia, I went to the convenience store at three AM to buy water. When I pushed the door open, there was only one clerk and one customer inside. The clerk was a young man, head down, organizing shelves. The customer was a girl in a nurse's uniform, standing in front of the cold case, hesitating over what to buy. The light was white and bright, shining on empty shelves, creating an unreal feeling. I grabbed a bottle of water and walked to the register. The clerk glanced at me and said nothing. After paying, I didn't leave right away but sat at the small table by the door drinking my water. The nurse bought her things too and sat across from me, slowly eating a rice ball. Neither of us said a word, but in a convenience store at three AM, the silence between two strangers actually made me feel less alone. Sometimes, not speaking is also a form of companionship.

questions.q_zh: 「我」为什么凌晨三点出门？
questions.q_en: Why did 'I' go out at three AM?

options.pinyin: yīnwèi yǒu rén jiào tā chūqù

questions.explanation: "Of insomnia" — the motivation is clear.

questions.q_en: What was the girl in the nurse's uniform doing?

questions.q_zh: 这段话传达的情绪是克制的还是外露的？
questions.q_en: Is the emotion conveyed in this passage restrained or overt?
questions.explanation: Read "Of insomnia" slowly — the feeling is in the phrasing.

title: The Museum Guard
text_zh: 博物馆里有一个保安，每天的工作就是站在展厅里，确保没有人碰到展品。他每天站八个小时，面前是几千年前的文物，身后是来来去去的游客。大部分游客都不会注意到他的存在，就像他是墙壁的一部分。但是他自己告诉我，这份工作一点都不无聊。他说：「我每天都在看这些文物，看了三年了，越看越有意思。有时候游客会问我问题，我都能回答得上来。」他指着一个青铜器对我说：「你看这个花纹，三千年前的人跟我们一样喜欢美的东西。」我很惊讶——他对这些文物的了解，比很多参观的人都深。
text_pinyin: Bówùguǎn lǐ yǒu yí gè bǎo'ān, měi tiān de gōngzuò jiù shì zhàn zài zhǎntīng lǐ, quèbǎo méiyǒu rén pèng dào zhǎnpǐn. Tā měi tiān zhàn bā gè xiǎoshí, miànqián shì jǐ qiān nián qián de wénwù, shēn hòu shì láilái-qùqù de yóukè. Dà bùfen yóukè dōu bú huì zhùyì dào tā de cúnzài, jiù xiàng tā shì qiángbì de yí bùfen. Dànshì tā zìjǐ gàosu wǒ, zhè fèn gōngzuò yìdiǎn dōu bù wúliáo. Tā shuō: 「Wǒ měi tiān dōu zài kàn zhèxiē wénwù, kàn le sān nián le, yuè kàn yuè yǒu yìsi. Yǒu shíhou yóukè huì wèn wǒ wèntí, wǒ dōu néng huídá de shànglái.」 Tā zhǐzhe yí gè qīngtóngqì duì wǒ shuō: 「Nǐ kàn zhège huāwén, sān qiān nián qián de rén gēn wǒmen yíyàng xǐhuan měi de dōngxi.」 Wǒ hěn jīngyà——tā duì zhèxiē wénwù de liǎojiě, bǐ hěn duō cānguān de rén dōu shēn. Yěxǔ měi tiān miànduì tóng yí jiàn dōngxi bìng bù kěpà, kěpà de shì nǐ cónglái bú yòng xīn qù kàn tā.
text_en: There's a security guard at the museum whose daily job is to stand in the exhibition hall, making sure nobody touches the exhibits. He stands eight hours a day, ancient artifacts before him, tourists coming and going behind. Most visitors don't notice he exists, as if he were part of the wall. But he told me himself that this job isn't boring at all. He said: 'I look at these artifacts every day. After three years, they only get more interesting. Sometimes tourists ask me questions, and I can always answer.' He pointed at a bronze vessel and said to me: 'Look at this pattern — people three thousand years ago liked beautiful things just as we do.' I was amazed — his understanding of these artifacts is deeper than many visitors'.

questions.q_zh: 保安觉得这份工作怎么样？
questions.q_en: How does the guard feel about his job?
questions.explanation: "He told me himself that this job isn't boring" sets a tone the rest of the passage sustains.

title: The Disappearing Chalk Drawings
text_zh: 每天早上我去上班的时候，都会在人行道上看到用粉笔画的画。有时候是一只猫，有时候是一朵花，有时候是一颗星星。画得不太好，像是小孩子画的。但是到了中午，画就被路人的脚步踩没了。第二天早上，又会出现一幅新的画。这个情况持续了整整一个月。我很好奇是谁画的，就有一天特别早起了，六点钟到了那个地方等着。结果看到一个穿校服的小女孩蹲在地上，手里拿着彩色粉笔，认认真真地画着一只蝴蝶。我走过去问她为什么画。她抬起头说：「因为我想让走这条路的人早上看到的时候开心一下。」我问她不怕被踩掉吗。她摇摇头说：「明天我再画一个新的就好了呀。」
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang wǒ qù shàngbān de shíhou, dōu huì zài rénxíngdào shàng kàn dào yòng fěnbǐ huà de huà. Yǒu shíhou shì yì zhī māo, yǒu shíhou shì yì duǒ huā, yǒu shíhou shì yì kē xīngxīng. Huà de bú tài hǎo, xiàng shì xiǎo háizi huà de. Dànshì dào le zhōngwǔ, huà jiù bèi lùrén de jiǎobù cǎi méi le. Dì èr tiān zǎoshang, yòu huì chūxiàn yì fú xīn de huà. Zhège qíngkuàng chíxù le zhěngzhěng yí gè yuè. Wǒ hěn hàoqí shì shéi huà de, jiù yǒu yì tiān tèbié zǎo qǐ le, liù diǎn zhōng dào le nàge dìfang děngzhe. Jiéguǒ kàn dào yí gè chuān xiàofú de xiǎo nǚhái dūn zài dì shàng, shǒu lǐ názhe cǎisè fěnbǐ, rènrèn-zhēnzhēn de huàzhe yì zhī húdié. Wǒ zǒu guòqù wèn tā wèishénme huà. Tā tái qǐ tóu shuō: 「Yīnwèi wǒ xiǎng ràng zǒu zhè tiáo lù de rén zǎoshang kàn dào de shíhou kāixīn yíxià.」 Wǒ wèn tā bú pà bèi cǎi diào ma. Tā yáoyao tóu shuō: 「Míngtiān wǒ zài huà yí gè xīn de jiù hǎo le ya.」
text_en: Every morning on my way to work, I'd see chalk drawings on the sidewalk. Sometimes a cat, sometimes a flower, sometimes a star. The drawings weren't good — they looked like a child's. But by noon, they'd be trampled away by passersby's feet. The next morning, a new drawing would appear. This went on for a whole month. Curious about who drew them, I woke up especially early one day and arrived at six o'clock to wait. I saw a little girl in a school uniform crouching on the ground, holding colored chalk, carefully drawing a butterfly. I walked over and asked why she draws. She looked up and said: 'Because I want people walking this road to feel happy when they see it in the morning.' I asked if she minds them being stepped on. She shook her head: 'Tomorrow I'll just draw a new one.'

questions.q_en: What happens to the chalk drawings by noon?

title: The Sound of Keys
text_zh: 每天晚上七点左右，我都能听到楼道里传来叮叮当当的钥匙声，然后是开门的声音。我不用看就知道，是隔壁的陈叔叔回来了。他的钥匙串上有好多把钥匙，走路的时候会互相碰撞，发出很有特点的声音。我在这里住了五年，已经能通过钥匙声辨认出几个邻居了。三楼的阿姨开门前总是找半天钥匙，能听到她在包里翻来翻去。五楼的年轻人用的是密码锁，开门的声音是「滴——」的一声。住在我楼上的小两口回来得最晚，十点以后才能听到他们的脚步声。这些声音组成了这栋楼晚上的时间表。有时候某个邻居回来得特别晚或者特别早，我心里都会想：他们今天是不是有什么事情？
text_pinyin: Měi tiān wǎnshang qī diǎn zuǒyòu, wǒ dōu néng tīng dào lóudào lǐ chuán lái dīngdīng-dāngdāng de yàoshi shēng, ránhòu shì kāi mén de shēngyīn. Wǒ búyòng kàn jiù zhīdào, shì gébì de Chén shūshu huílái le. Tā de yàoshichuàn shàng yǒu hǎo duō bǎ yàoshi, zǒulù de shíhou huì hùxiāng pèngzhuàng, fā chū hěn yǒu tèdiǎn de shēngyīn. Wǒ zài zhèlǐ zhù le wǔ nián, yǐjīng néng tōngguò yàoshi shēng biànrèn chū jǐ gè línjū le. Sān lóu de āyí kāi mén qián zǒng shì zhǎo bàntiān yàoshi, néng tīng dào tā zài bāo lǐ fān lái fān qù. Wǔ lóu de niánqīngrén yòng de shì mìmǎ suǒ, kāi mén de shēngyīn shì「dī——」de yì shēng. Zhù zài wǒ lóu shàng de xiǎo liǎng kǒu huílái de zuì wǎn, shí diǎn yǐhòu cái néng tīng dào tāmen de jiǎobù shēng. Zhèxiē shēngyīn zǔ chéng le zhè dòng lóu wǎnshang de shíjiānbiǎo. Yǒu shíhou mǒu gè línjū huílái de tèbié wǎn huòzhě tèbié zǎo, wǒ xīnlǐ dōu huì xiǎng: tāmen jīntiān shì bu shì yǒu shénme shìqíng? Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì zhù zài tóng yí dòng lóu lǐ de mòqì.
text_en: Every evening around seven, I can hear the jingling of keys in the hallway, followed by a door opening. Without looking, I know it's Uncle Chen from next door. His keychain has many keys that clank together when he walks, making a distinctive sound. After five years here, I can identify several neighbors by their key sounds. The auntie on the third floor always rummages for her keys before opening the door — you can hear her digging through her bag. The young person on the fifth floor uses a keypad lock — the door opens with a 'beep.' The young couple above me come home latest — not until after ten can you hear their footsteps. These sounds form this building's evening timetable. Sometimes when a neighbor comes home especially late or early, I wonder: did something happen today?

title: The Best Bad Photos
text_zh: 去年我和朋友去旅行。她帮我拍了二十多张照片，回来一看——全部拍模糊了。每一张都在抖，像是在地震中拍的。她说她以为按一下就好了，不知道要拿稳。然后我帮她拍。我很认真，找了最好的角度。结果出来以后，她的脸只剩下一半——另一半被一个路过的游客挡住了。那个游客还在对着镜头微笑。我们在手机上翻这些照片的时候，比看任何一张完美的照片都开心。
text_pinyin: Qùnián wǒ hé péngyou qù lǚxíng. Tā bāng wǒ pāi le èrshí duō zhāng zhàopiàn, huílái yí kàn — quánbù pāi móhu le. Měi yì zhāng dōu zài dǒu, xiàng shì zài dìzhèn zhōng pāi de. Tā shuō tā yǐwéi àn yíxià jiù hǎo le, bù zhīdào yào ná wěn. Ránhòu wǒ bāng tā pāi. Wǒ hěn rènzhēn, zhǎo le zuì hǎo de jiǎodù. Jiéguǒ chūlái yǐhòu, tā de liǎn zhǐ shèng xià yíbàn — lìng yíbàn bèi yí gè lùguò de yóukè dǎng zhù le. Nà ge yóukè hái zài duì zhe jìngtóu wēixiào. Wǒmen zài shǒujī shang fān zhèxiē zhàopiàn de shíhou, bǐ kàn rènhé yì zhāng wánměi de zhàopiàn dōu kāixīn.
text_en: Last year my friend and I went traveling. She took twenty-something photos of me — every single one blurry. Each one shaking, like photographed during an earthquake. She said she thought you just press once, didn't know you had to hold still. Then I took her photos. I was careful, found the perfect angle. Result: half her face — the other half blocked by a passing tourist. That tourist was smiling at the camera. Scrolling through these photos on our phones later, we were happier than looking at any perfect photo.

questions.q_zh: 朋友拍的照片有什么问题？
questions.q_en: What was wrong with the friend's photos?
questions.explanation: Go back to "Every single one blurry." That's where the answer lives.

title: Why Chinese People Love Hot Water
title_zh: 中国人为什么爱喝热水
text_zh: 来中国的外国人经常会注意到一件事：中国人特别爱喝热水。不管是在餐厅、办公室还是医院，到处都能看到保温杯和热水壶。在西方国家，人们习惯喝凉水甚至冰水，但是大部分中国人觉得喝凉水对身体不好。这个习惯有几个原因。首先，中医认为人的身体需要保持温暖，喝热水可以帮助消化和血液循环。其次，以前中国很多地方的自来水不够干净，把水烧开再喝是为了杀菌。虽然现在水质好了很多，但是这个习惯已经传了好几代人了。另外，在中国文化里，「多喝热水」不只是一个健康建议，它也是一种关心人的方式。当你不舒服的时候，中国朋友一定会说：「多喝点热水。」这句话虽然简单，但是它的意思是「我关心你」。
text_pinyin: Lái Zhōngguó de wàiguórén jīngcháng huì zhùyì dào yí jiàn shì: Zhōngguórén tèbié ài hē rè shuǐ. Bùguǎn shì zài cāntīng, bàngōngshì háishi yīyuàn, dàochù dōu néng kàn dào bǎowēn bēi hé rè shuǐhú. Zài xīfāng guójiā, rénmen xíguàn hē liáng shuǐ shènzhì bīng shuǐ, dànshì dà bùfen Zhōngguórén juéde hē liáng shuǐ duì shēntǐ bù hǎo. Zhège xíguàn yǒu jǐ gè yuányīn. Shǒuxiān, zhōngyī rènwéi rén de shēntǐ xūyào bǎochí wēnnuǎn, hē rè shuǐ kěyǐ bāngzhù xiāohuà hé xuèyè xúnhuán. Qícì, yǐqián Zhōngguó hěn duō dìfang de zìlái shuǐ bú gòu gānjìng, bǎ shuǐ shāo kāi zài hē shì wèile shājūn. Suīrán xiànzài shuǐzhì hǎo le hěn duō, dànshì zhège xíguàn yǐjīng chuán le hǎo jǐ dài rén le. Lìngwài, zài Zhōngguó wénhuà lǐ,「duō hē rè shuǐ」bù zhǐ shì yí gè jiànkāng jiànyì, tā yě shì yì zhǒng guānxīn rén de fāngshì. Dāng nǐ bù shūfu de shíhou, Zhōngguó péngyou yídìng huì shuō:「Duō hē diǎn rè shuǐ.」 Zhè jù huà suīrán jiǎndān, dànshì tā de yìsi shì「wǒ guānxīn nǐ」.
text_en: Foreigners in China often notice something: Chinese people especially love drinking hot water. Whether in restaurants, offices, or hospitals, thermoses and hot water kettles are everywhere. In Western countries, people are used to cold or even ice water, but most Chinese people believe drinking cold water is bad for the body. There are several reasons for this habit. First, traditional Chinese medicine holds that the body needs to stay warm, and hot water aids digestion and blood circulation. Second, in many parts of China the tap water used to be unclean, so boiling water before drinking was for sterilization. Although water quality has improved greatly, this habit has been passed down for generations. Also, in Chinese culture, 'drink more hot water' isn't just health advice — it's a way of showing care. When you're feeling unwell, a Chinese friend will certainly say: 'Drink more hot water.' This simple phrase really means: 'I care about you.'

questions.q_zh: 中医觉得喝热水有什么好处？
questions.q_en: What benefits does Chinese medicine attribute to hot water?
questions.explanation: "First, traditional Chinese medicine holds that the body needs" — once you spot this, the answer clicks.

questions.q_zh: 以前为什么要把水烧开？
questions.q_en: Why did people boil water in the past?

options.text: 因为自来水不够干净，为了杀菌
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zìlái shuǐ bú gòu gānjìng, wèile shājūn
options.text_en: tap water wasn't clean enough, for sterilization

questions.explanation: Look at "Second, in many parts of China the tap water used" — the reason is right there.

questions.q_zh: 如果打破了这个习惯，可能会怎样？
questions.q_en: What might happen if this routine were broken?

options.pinyin: huì zhǎodào gèng hǎo de xíguàn

questions.explanation: Go back to "Foreigners in China often notice something: Chinese people especially love." That's where the answer lives.

title: The Drying Rack Symphony
text_zh: 刮大风的日子，整个小区的晾衣架都在响。金属杆碰在一起的声音，叮叮叮，像风铃一样。有的衣服被吹得飞了起来，像挣脱了绳子的风筝。我站在阳台上看了一会儿，发现每个人晾衣服的方式都不一样：有人把衣服用夹子夹得紧紧的，大风也吹不掉；有人只是随便搭在绳子上，一有风就掉了下来。楼下有个阿姨正在追一件被风吹到草地上的床单，跑了好远才追到。她把床单抱在怀里，气喘吁吁地笑着。风停了以后，一切恢复了安静。但是我注意到有一件红色的毛衣挂在了三楼的空调外机上，不知道是谁家的。它在那里挂了三天，才被人用一根长竹竿够下来。
text_pinyin: Guā dà fēng de rìzi, zhěnggè xiǎoqū de liàng yī jià dōu zài xiǎng. Jīnshǔ gǎn pèng zài yìqǐ de shēngyīn, dīng dīng dīng, xiàng fēnglíng yíyàng. Yǒude yīfu bèi chuī de fēi le qǐlái, xiàng zhēngtuō le shéngzi de fēngzhēng. Wǒ zhàn zài yángtái shàng kàn le yíhuìr, fāxiàn měi gè rén liàng yīfu de fāngshì dōu bù yíyàng: yǒu rén bǎ yīfu yòng jiāzi jiā de jǐnjǐn de, dà fēng yě chuī bu diào; yǒu rén zhǐ shì suíbiàn dā zài shéngzi shàng, yì yǒu fēng jiù diào le xiàlái. Lóu xià yǒu ge āyí zhèngzài zhuī yí jiàn bèi fēng chuī dào cǎodì shàng de chuángdān, pǎo le hǎo yuǎn cái zhuī dào. Tā bǎ chuángdān bào zài huái lǐ, qìchuǎn-xūxū de xiàozhe. Fēng tíng le yǐhòu, yíqiè huīfù le ānjìng. Dànshì wǒ zhùyì dào yǒu yí jiàn hóng sè de máoyī guà zài le sān lóu de kōngtiáo wàijī shàng, bù zhīdào shì shéi jiā de. Tā zài nàlǐ guà le sān tiān, cái bèi rén yòng yì gēn cháng zhúgān gòu xiàlái.
text_en: On windy days, all the drying racks in the compound ring out. Metal rods clanking together — ding, ding, ding — like wind chimes. Some clothes get blown into the air, like kites breaking free of their strings. I stood on the balcony watching and noticed everyone hangs laundry differently: some clip clothes on tightly with pegs, and even strong wind can't blow them off; others just drape things casually on the line, and they fall at the first breeze. Downstairs, an auntie was chasing a bedsheet the wind blew onto the lawn, running far before catching it. She hugged it to her chest, laughing and out of breath. After the wind died down, everything returned to quiet. But I noticed a red sweater had landed on the third floor's outdoor AC unit — who knows whose it was. It hung there for three days before someone reached it down with a long bamboo pole.

questions.q_zh: 大风天小区里什么在响？
questions.q_en: What's ringing in the compound on windy days?
questions.explanation: Focus on "On windy days, all the drying racks in the compound" — the rest follows.

questions.q_zh: 楼下的阿姨在追什么？
questions.q_en: What was the auntie downstairs chasing?

options.pinyin: yí jiàn bèi fēng chuī zǒu de chuángdān
options.text_en: a bedsheet blown away by wind

questions.explanation: "Downstairs, an auntie was chasing a bedsheet the wind blew" gives you what you need.

questions.q_zh: 红色的毛衣最后怎么拿下来的？
questions.q_en: How was the red sweater finally retrieved?

options.pinyin: fēng bǎ tā chuī xiàlái le
yòng cháng zhúgān gòu xiàlái de
options.text_en: reached down with a long bamboo pole

questions.explanation: The answer sits right in "It hung there for three days before someone reached."

title: The Grandfather Clock
text_zh: 客厅里有一个老座钟，是爷爷留下来的。它每到整点就会「当——当——」地响，几点就响几下。小时候我被这个声音吵得睡不着，现在反而离不开它了。有一次座钟坏了，家里突然安静得不对劲，我才意识到它的声音已经变成了家的一部分。爸爸找了一个修钟的师傅来，师傅说这种钟的零件很难找了，要花不少时间。在钟停了的那两个星期里，每到整点我都会习惯性地等那个声音，但是什么也没有。等钟修好重新响起来的那一刻，全家人都笑了。妈妈说：「这个钟比你们任何一个人的年纪都大，它是这个家里最老的成员。」我想她说得对——这个座钟不只是在报时，它在给一个家数着年龄。
text_pinyin: Kètīng lǐ yǒu yí gè lǎo zuòzhōng, shì yéye liú xiàlái de. Tā měi dào zhěng diǎn jiù huì「dāng——dāng——」de xiǎng, jǐ diǎn jiù xiǎng jǐ xià. Xiǎo shíhou wǒ bèi zhège shēngyīn chǎo de shuì bu zháo, xiànzài fǎn'ér lí bu kāi tā le. Yǒu yí cì zuòzhōng huài le, jiā lǐ tūrán ānjìng de bú duìjìn, wǒ cái yìshi dào tā de shēngyīn yǐjīng biàn chéng le jiā de yí bùfen. Bàba zhǎo le yí gè xiū zhōng de shīfu lái, shīfu shuō zhè zhǒng zhōng de língjiàn hěn nán zhǎo le, yào huā bù shǎo shíjiān. Zài zhōng tíng le de nà liǎng gè xīngqī lǐ, měi dào zhěng diǎn wǒ dōu huì xíguànxìng de děng nàge shēngyīn, dànshì shénme yě méiyǒu. Děng zhōng xiū hǎo chóngxīn xiǎng qǐlái de nà yí kè, quán jiā rén dōu xiào le. Māma shuō: 「Zhège zhōng bǐ nǐmen rènhé yí gè rén de niánjì dōu dà, tā shì zhège jiā lǐ zuì lǎo de chéngyuán.」 Wǒ xiǎng tā shuō de duì——zhège zuòzhōng bù zhǐ shì zài bàoshí, tā zài gěi yí gè jiā shùzhe niánlíng.
text_en: In the living room there's an old grandfather clock, left by my grandfather. On every hour it chimes — dong, dong — as many times as the hour. As a child, the sound kept me from sleeping; now I can't do without it. Once the clock broke, and the house went strangely quiet — only then did I realize its sound had become part of the home. Dad found a clock repairman, who said parts for this kind of clock are hard to find and would take a while. During the two weeks it was stopped, I'd instinctively wait for the chime at each hour, but nothing came. The moment the repaired clock chimed again, the whole family smiled. Mom said: 'This clock is older than any of you — it's the oldest member of this family.' I think she's right. This clock isn't just keeping time; it's counting a family's years.

questions.q_zh: 座钟是谁留下来的？
questions.q_en: Who left the clock behind?
questions.explanation: Go back to "In the living room there's an old grandfather clock, left." That's where the answer lives.

questions.q_zh: 座钟坏了以后「我」有什么感觉？
questions.q_en: How did 'I' feel when the clock broke?

options.text: 终于可以安静地睡觉了
options.pinyin: zhōngyú kěyǐ ānjìng de shuìjiào le
jiā lǐ ānjìng de bú duìjìn

questions.explanation: Read "Once the clock broke, and the house went strangely quiet" slowly — the feeling is in the phrasing.

questions.q_zh: 读完后，读者最可能有什么感受？
questions.q_en: How is the reader most likely to feel after reading this?
questions.explanation: The emotion comes through in choices like "In the living room there's an old grandfather clock, left."

title: The Shared Bicycle Graveyard
text_zh: 在城市的郊区，有一个空地上堆满了成千上万辆共享单车。黄色的、蓝色的、绿色的，堆成了一座小山。这些车大部分都是坏了的——有的没有座椅，有的轮子歪了，有的二维码被刮掉了。从远处看，它们像一幅色彩鲜艳的抽象画。从近处看，却让人觉得有点可惜。几年前，共享单车刚出来的时候，大家都觉得特别方便，走到哪里都能骑。但是后来太多的公司一起做，车越来越多，路上到处都是。很多车被随便扔在路边、河里、甚至树上。最后很多公司倒闭了，留下了这些没人管的车。这个「自行车坟场」让我想到：不管多好的东西，如果没有节制地发展，最后都可能变成一种浪费。
text_pinyin: Zài chéngshì de jiāoqū, yǒu yí gè kòngdì shàng duī mǎn le chéng qiān shàng wàn liàng gòngxiǎng dānchē. Huáng sè de, lán sè de, lǜ sè de, duī chéng le yí zuò xiǎo shān. Zhèxiē chē dà bùfen dōu shì huài le de——yǒude méiyǒu zuòyǐ, yǒude lúnzi wāi le, yǒude èrwéimǎ bèi guā diào le. Cóng yuǎnchù kàn, tāmen xiàng yì fú sècǎi xiānyàn de chōuxiàng huà. Cóng jìnchù kàn, què ràng rén juéde yǒudiǎn kěxī. Jǐ nián qián, gòngxiǎng dānchē gāng chūlái de shíhou, dàjiā dōu juéde tèbié fāngbiàn, zǒu dào nǎlǐ dōu néng qí. Dànshì hòulái tài duō de gōngsī yìqǐ zuò, chē yuè lái yuè duō, lù shàng dàochù dōu shì. Hěn duō chē bèi suíbiàn rēng zài lùbiān, hé lǐ, shènzhì shù shàng. Zuìhòu hěn duō gōngsī dǎobì le, liú xià le zhèxiē méi rén guǎn de chē. Zhège「zìxíngchē fénchǎng」ràng wǒ xiǎng dào: bùguǎn duō hǎo de dōngxi, rúguǒ méiyǒu jiézhì de fāzhǎn, zuìhòu dōu kěnéng biàn chéng yì zhǒng làngfèi.
text_en: On the outskirts of the city, an empty lot is piled high with thousands upon thousands of shared bicycles. Yellow, blue, green — stacked into a small mountain. Most of these bikes are broken: some missing seats, some with bent wheels, some with QR codes scraped off. From a distance they look like a vivid abstract painting. Up close, they feel a bit sad. A few years ago when shared bikes first appeared, everyone thought they were incredibly convenient — you could ride one anywhere. But then too many companies jumped in, bikes multiplied endlessly, cluttering streets everywhere. Many were carelessly tossed on roadsides, in rivers, even in trees. Eventually many companies went bankrupt, leaving these ownerless bikes behind. This 'bicycle graveyard' made me think: no matter how good something is, if it grows without restraint, it can end up as waste.

questions.q_zh: 这段话暗示了什么情感？
questions.q_en: What emotion does this passage hint at?
questions.explanation: "On the outskirts of the city, an empty lot" sets a tone the rest of the passage sustains.

questions.q_zh: 共享单车为什么变成了这样？
questions.q_en: Why did the shared bikes end up like this?

options.pinyin: yīnwèi dàjiā bù xǐhuan qí chē

options.text: 因为太多公司一起做，最后很多倒闭了
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tài duō gōngsī yìqǐ zuò, zuìhòu hěn duō dǎobì le
options.text_en: too many companies competed, many went bankrupt

options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhèngfǔ bú ràng qí le
yīnwèi chē de zhìliàng tài chà

questions.explanation: It comes down to "Eventually many companies went bankrupt, leaving these ownerless bikes behind" — cause and effect.

questions.q_zh: 这段话想要读者从中获得什么？
questions.q_en: What does the passage want the reader to gain from it?

options.text: 一种新的看事物的方式
options.pinyin: yì zhǒng xīn de kàn shìwù de fāngshì
options.text_en: a new way of seeing things

questions.explanation: Moments like "On the outskirts of the city, an empty lot" reach beyond the page.

title: The Paper Airplane
text_zh: 今天下午我在阳台上看书的时候，一架纸飞机突然从天上飞了下来，落在了我的腿上。纸飞机是用作业纸折的，上面还能看到几道数学题。我把纸飞机打开一看，反面写着一句话：「谁捡到了我的飞机，请扔回来。——五楼的小明。」我笑了笑，重新把纸折好，用力往上扔了回去。但是风太大了，飞机被吹到了隔壁楼的阳台上。我正不知道怎么办的时候，又一架纸飞机飞了下来。上面写着：「上一架没有回来，再试一次。」我这次站起来，对准五楼的方向使劲一扔。过了几秒钟，我听到楼上传来一声欢呼：「接住了！」虽然我不认识五楼的小明，但是这几分钟的「纸飞机对话」让我的下午变得有趣多了。
text_pinyin: Jīntiān xiàwǔ wǒ zài yángtái shàng kàn shū de shíhou, yí jià zhǐ fēijī tūrán cóng tiān shàng fēi le xiàlái, luò zài le wǒ de tuǐ shàng. Zhǐ fēijī shì yòng zuòyè zhǐ zhé de, shàngmiàn hái néng kàn dào jǐ dào shùxué tí. Wǒ bǎ zhǐ fēijī dǎkāi yí kàn, fǎnmiàn xiězhe yí jù huà: 「Shéi jiǎn dào le wǒ de fēijī, qǐng rēng huílái.——wǔ lóu de Xiǎo Míng.」 Wǒ xiào le xiào, chóngxīn bǎ zhǐ zhé hǎo, yònglì wǎng shàng rēng le huíqù. Dànshì fēng tài dà le, fēijī bèi chuī dào le gébì lóu de yángtái shàng. Wǒ zhèng bù zhīdào zěnme bàn de shíhou, yòu yí jià zhǐ fēijī fēi le xiàlái. Shàngmiàn xiězhe: 「Shàng yí jià méiyǒu huílái, zài shì yí cì.」 Wǒ zhè cì zhàn qǐlái, duìzhǔn wǔ lóu de fāngxiàng shǐjìn yì rēng. Guò le jǐ miǎozhōng, wǒ tīng dào lóu shàng chuán lái yì shēng huānhū: 「Jiē zhù le!」 Suīrán wǒ bú rènshi wǔ lóu de Xiǎo Míng, dànshì zhè jǐ fēnzhōng de「zhǐ fēijī duìhuà」ràng wǒ de xiàwǔ biàn de yǒuqù duō le.
text_en: This afternoon while reading on the balcony, a paper airplane suddenly flew down from above and landed on my lap. It was folded from homework paper — you could still see some math problems on it. I unfolded it and found a message on the back: 'Whoever finds my airplane, please throw it back. — Xiao Ming from the fifth floor.' I smiled, refolded the paper, and threw it back up hard. But the wind was too strong and blew it onto the neighboring building's balcony. Just as I was wondering what to do, another paper airplane flew down. It read: 'The last one didn't come back. Trying again.' This time I stood up, aimed at the fifth floor, and threw with all my strength. A few seconds later, I heard a cheer from upstairs: 'Caught it!' Although I don't know Xiao Ming from the fifth floor, these few minutes of 'paper airplane conversation' made my afternoon much more interesting.

questions.q_zh: 纸飞机是用什么纸折的？
questions.q_en: What paper was the airplane made from?
questions.explanation: The key is "It was folded from homework paper" — read it with the answer in mind.

questions.q_zh: 第一次扔回去为什么没成功？
questions.q_en: Why did the first throw back fail?

options.text: 风太大了，被吹到隔壁楼了
options.pinyin: fēng tài dà le, bèi chuī dào gébì lóu le
options.text_en: wind was too strong, blown to neighboring building

questions.explanation: The reason hides in plain sight: "The wind was too strong and blew it onto."

questions.q_zh: 这件事让「我」觉得怎么样？
questions.q_en: How did this make 'I' feel?

options.pinyin: xiàwǔ biàn de yǒuqù duō le

questions.explanation: The mood lives in specifics like "Caught it!' Although I don."

title: The Night Shift Nurse
text_zh: 我姐姐是一名护士，经常上夜班。她说夜班最难的不是工作本身，而是在半夜两三点的时候对抗困意。医院的夜晚和白天完全不一样：走廊的灯光调得很暗，护士站只有一盏小台灯亮着。大部分病人都睡着了，偶尔有人按呼叫铃，姐姐就马上跑过去看。她说最害怕听到的不是呼叫铃，而是半夜突然的安静——因为安静有时候意味着有病人出了问题。每次上完夜班回家，姐姐都会先洗一个热水澡，然后拉上窗帘，在白天睡觉。但是她说白天睡觉永远不如晚上睡得好，因为窗外总有阳光和声音提醒你现在是白天。虽然很辛苦，但她从来没有抱怨过。她说：「只要病人平平安安地过了一夜，我的夜班就是值得的。」
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiějie shì yì míng hùshi, jīngcháng shàng yèbān. Tā shuō yèbān zuì nán de búshì gōngzuò běnshēn, érshì zài bànyè liǎng sān diǎn de shíhou duìkàng kùnyì. Yīyuàn de yèwǎn hé báitiān wánquán bù yíyàng: zǒuláng de dēngguāng tiáo de hěn àn, hùshizhàn zhǐ yǒu yì zhǎn xiǎo táidēng liàngzhe. Dà bùfen bìngrén dōu shuìzháo le, ǒu'ěr yǒu rén àn hūjiào líng, jiějie jiù mǎshàng pǎo guòqù kàn. Tā shuō zuì hàipà tīng dào de búshì hūjiào líng, érshì bànyè tūrán de ānjìng——yīnwèi ānjìng yǒu shíhou yìwèizhe yǒu bìngrén chū le wèntí. Měi cì shàng wán yèbān huí jiā, jiějie dōu huì xiān xǐ yí gè rè shuǐ zǎo, ránhòu lā shàng chuānglián, zài báitiān shuìjiào. Dànshì tā shuō báitiān shuìjiào yǒngyuǎn bùrú wǎnshang shuì de hǎo, yīnwèi chuāng wài zǒng yǒu yángguāng hé shēngyīn tíxǐng nǐ xiànzài shì báitiān. Suīrán hěn xīnkǔ, dàn tā cónglái méiyǒu bàoyuàn guò. Tā shuō: 「Zhǐyào bìngrén píng píng ān ān de guò le yí yè, wǒ de yèbān jiù shì zhíde de.」
text_en: My sister is a nurse who often works night shifts. She says the hardest part isn't the work itself, but fighting sleepiness at two or three in the morning. The hospital at night is completely different from the day: hallway lights are dimmed, and only a small desk lamp glows at the nurses' station. Most patients are asleep; occasionally someone presses the call button, and she rushes over immediately. She says what she fears hearing most isn't the call button, but sudden silence in the middle of the night — because silence sometimes means a patient has had a problem. After every night shift, she goes home, takes a hot bath, draws the curtains, and sleeps during the day. But she says daytime sleep is never as good as nighttime sleep, because sunlight and sounds outside always remind you it's daytime. Though it's hard, she's never complained. She says: 'As long as the patients get through the night safely, my night shift is worth it.'

questions.q_en: What's the hardest part of the night shift?

title: The Extra Coffee
text_zh: 最近一个月，每个星期一早上我到办公室的时候，桌子上都会多出一杯咖啡。咖啡是热的，杯子上贴着一张纸条，写着「星期一快乐」。我问了办公室所有的同事，没有人承认是自己放的。我检查了一下咖啡，是我最喜欢的那种——冰美式，但是用保温杯装的，所以还是热的。这说明放咖啡的人知道我的喜好。我试过提前半个小时来，但是咖啡已经在那里了。我又试过来得更早，结果发现咖啡是在我到达之前五分钟放的——因为杯子还烫手。这个「咖啡侦探」游戏玩了一个月以后，我终于在一个星期一的早上，蹲在走廊的拐角处等到了答案：是前台的小张。她说：「因为你每个星期一看起来都很累，我想让你的一周有一个好的开始。」
text_pinyin: Zuìjìn yí gè yuè, měi gè xīngqīyī zǎoshang wǒ dào bàngōngshì de shíhou, zhuōzi shàng dōu huì duō chū yì bēi kāfēi. Kāfēi shì rè de, bēizi shàng tiēzhe yì zhāng zhǐtiáo, xiězhe「Xīngqīyī kuàilè」. Wǒ wèn le bàngōngshì suǒyǒu de tóngshì, méiyǒu rén chéngrèn shì zìjǐ fàng de. Wǒ jiǎnchá le yíxià kāfēi, shì wǒ zuì xǐhuan de nà zhǒng——bīng Měishì, dànshì yòng bǎowēn bēi zhuāng de, suǒyǐ háishi rè de. Zhè shuōmíng fàng kāfēi de rén zhīdào wǒ de xǐhào. Wǒ shì guò tíqián bàn gè xiǎoshí lái, dànshì kāfēi yǐjīng zài nàlǐ le. Wǒ yòu shì guò lái de gèng zǎo, jiéguǒ fāxiàn kāfēi shì zài wǒ dàodá zhīqián wǔ fēnzhōng fàng de——yīnwèi bēizi hái tàng shǒu. Zhège「kāfēi zhēntàn」yóuxì wán le yí gè yuè yǐhòu, wǒ zhōngyú zài yí gè xīngqīyī de zǎoshang, dūn zài zǒuláng de guǎijiǎo chù děng dào le dá'àn: shì qiántái de Xiǎo Zhāng. Tā shuō: 「Yīnwèi nǐ měi gè xīngqīyī kàn qǐlái dōu hěn lèi, wǒ xiǎng ràng nǐ de yì zhōu yǒu yí gè hǎo de kāishǐ.」
text_en: For the past month, every Monday morning when I arrive at the office, there's an extra cup of coffee on my desk. The coffee is hot, with a note on the cup reading 'Happy Monday.' I asked every colleague in the office — nobody admitted it. I examined the coffee: it's my favorite kind — iced Americano, but kept in a thermos so it's still hot. This means the person knows my preferences. I tried arriving half an hour early, but the coffee was already there. I tried coming even earlier and found the coffee was placed just five minutes before I arrived — because the cup was still burning hot. After a month of this 'coffee detective' game, I finally crouched around the corner of the hallway one Monday morning and got my answer: it was Xiao Zhang from reception. She said: 'Because you look so tired every Monday, I wanted your week to have a good start.'

questions.q_en: How does Xiao Zhang know what coffee the narrator likes?

title: The Photo I Can't Throw Away
text_zh: 整理房间的时候，我发现了一张小学毕业的集体照。照片已经发黄了，角上还有折痕。照片里的三十个孩子穿着白衬衫，站成三排，每个人都努力地笑着。我一个一个地看过去，发现很多人的名字我已经忘了。但是有几个人我还记得很清楚：坐在第一排最左边的是王小明，他总是考第一名；站在最后一排最高的是李强，他是班里最调皮的人。而站在中间、笑得最灿烂的那个女生，是我当时最好的朋友——但我们已经十几年没有联系了。我本来想把这张照片扔掉，因为它已经太旧了。但是拿在手里看了很久以后，我还是把它放回了抽屉里。有些东西你明明知道再也用不到了，但就是扔不掉。
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ fángjiān de shíhou, wǒ fāxiàn le yì zhāng xiǎoxué bìyè de jítǐ zhào. Zhàopiàn yǐjīng fā huáng le, jiǎo shàng hái yǒu zhéhén. Zhàopiàn lǐ de sānshí gè háizi chuānzhe bái chènshān, zhàn chéng sān pái, měi gè rén dōu nǔlì de xiàozhe. Wǒ yí gè yí gè de kàn guòqù, fāxiàn hěn duō rén de míngzi wǒ yǐjīng wàng le. Dànshì yǒu jǐ gè rén wǒ hái jìde hěn qīngchu: zuò zài dì yī pái zuì zuǒbiān de shì Wáng Xiǎomíng, tā zǒng shì kǎo dì yī míng; zhàn zài zuìhòu yì pái zuì gāo de shì Lǐ Qiáng, tā shì bān lǐ zuì tiáopí de rén. Ér zhàn zài zhōngjiān, xiào de zuì cànlàn de nàge nǚshēng, shì wǒ dāngshí zuì hǎo de péngyou——dàn wǒmen yǐjīng shí jǐ nián méiyǒu liánxì le. Wǒ běnlái xiǎng bǎ zhè zhāng zhàopiàn rēng diào, yīnwèi tā yǐjīng tài jiù le. Dànshì ná zài shǒu lǐ kàn le hěn jiǔ yǐhòu, wǒ háishi bǎ tā fàng huí le chōuti lǐ. Yǒuxiē dōngxi nǐ míngmíng zhīdào zài yě yòng bu dào le, dàn jiù shì rēng bu diào. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì huíyì de zhòngliàng.
text_en: While tidying my room, I found a group photo from elementary school graduation. The photo has yellowed, with creases at the corners. Thirty children in white shirts stand in three rows, each trying hard to smile. I looked at them one by one and found I'd forgotten many of their names. But a few I remember clearly: on the far left of the first row is Wang Xiaoming, who always came first in exams; the tallest in the back row is Li Qiang, the class troublemaker. And standing in the middle, smiling most brightly, is the girl who was my best friend at the time — but we haven't been in touch for over a decade. I originally wanted to throw this photo away because it's too old. But after holding it and looking at it for a long time, I put it back in the drawer. Some things you clearly know you'll never need again, yet you just can't throw them away.

title: The Lunch Box Exchange
text_zh: 办公室里有一个不成文的传统：每个星期五是「便当交换日」。愿意参加的同事自己带一份午饭来，然后随机跟另一个人交换。规则很简单——你不知道会吃到谁做的饭，对方也不知道会吃到你做的。这个活动是三年前一个同事提议的，刚开始只有四五个人参加，现在已经有十几个了。大家做的饭各种各样：有人擅长做面条，有人擅长做炒菜，有一个韩国同事每次都带泡菜炒饭。最有意思的是，你可以通过便当了解一个人的性格——做事认真的人连便当盒里的菜都摆得整整齐齐，随性的人就随便装在一个袋子里。有一次我交换到了一个特别好吃的红烧肉便当，从那以后我一直在找是谁做的，但是到现在还没找到。
text_pinyin: Bàngōngshì lǐ yǒu yí gè bù chéng wén de chuántǒng: měi gè xīngqīwǔ shì「biàndāng jiāohuàn rì」. Yuànyì cānjiā de tóngshì zìjǐ dài yí fèn wǔfàn lái, ránhòu suíjī gēn lìng yí gè rén jiāohuàn. Guīzé hěn jiǎndān——nǐ bù zhīdào huì chī dào shéi zuò de fàn, duìfāng yě bù zhīdào huì chī dào nǐ zuò de. Zhège huódòng shì sān nián qián yí gè tóngshì tíyì de, gāng kāishǐ zhǐ yǒu sì wǔ gè rén cānjiā, xiànzài yǐjīng yǒu shí jǐ gè le. Dàjiā zuò de fàn gè zhǒng gè yàng: yǒu rén shàncháng zuò miàntiáo, yǒu rén shàncháng zuò chǎo cài, yǒu yí gè Hánguó tóngshì měi cì dōu dài pàocài chǎo fàn. Zuì yǒu yìsi de shì, nǐ kěyǐ tōngguò biàndāng liǎojiě yí gè rén de xìnggé——zuò shì rènzhēn de rén lián biàndāng hé lǐ de cài dōu bǎi de zhěngzhěng-qíqí, suíxìng de rén jiù suíbiàn zhuāng zài yí gè dàizi lǐ. Yǒu yí cì wǒ jiāohuàn dào le yí gè tèbié hǎochī de hóngshāoròu biàndāng, cóng nà yǐhòu wǒ yìzhí zài zhǎo shì shéi zuò de, dànshì dào xiànzài hái méi zhǎo dào.
text_en: The office has an unwritten tradition: every Friday is 'lunch box exchange day.' Willing colleagues each bring a homemade lunch and randomly exchange with someone else. The rules are simple — you don't know whose food you'll eat, and they don't know they'll eat yours. This activity was proposed by a colleague three years ago. At first only four or five people joined; now there are over a dozen. Everyone makes different things: some are good at noodles, some at stir-fry. A Korean colleague always brings kimchi fried rice. The most interesting part is that you can understand a person's personality through their lunch box — meticulous people arrange food neatly even in the box, while easygoing people just stuff everything in a bag. Once I got a delicious braised pork lunch box. Since then I've been trying to find who made it, but I still haven't.

title: The Tree That Marks the Seasons
text_zh: 我办公室的窗户外面有一棵大银杏树。春天的时候，它先是光秃秃的，然后慢慢长出嫩绿色的小叶子。夏天叶子变得又大又密，像一把绿色的大伞。到了秋天，它是这条街上最好看的——所有的叶子在两三个星期内变成金黄色，然后一片一片地掉下来。冬天又变回了光秃秃的样子，只剩下黑色的树枝在天空下画出复杂的线条。我坐在同一个位置，每天看着同一棵树，但是它每天都在变。有时候我会忘了看窗外好几天，再一看的时候发现树已经完全不一样了。这让我想到，变化有时候是很慢的，慢到你几乎注意不到。但是只要你愿意停下来仔细看，就会发现世界每天都在不一样。这棵银杏树不需要说话，它只用颜色就能告诉你现在是哪个季节。
text_pinyin: Wǒ bàngōngshì de chuānghu wàimiàn yǒu yì kē dà yínxìng shù. Chūntiān de shíhou, tā xiān shì guāngtūtū de, ránhòu mànmàn zhǎng chū nèn lǜ sè de xiǎo yèzi. Xiàtiān yèzi biàn de yòu dà yòu mì, xiàng yì bǎ lǜ sè de dà sǎn. Dào le qiūtiān, tā shì zhè tiáo jiē shàng zuì hǎokàn de——suǒyǒu de yèzi zài liǎng sān gè xīngqī nèi biàn chéng jīnhuáng sè, ránhòu yí piàn yí piàn de diào xiàlái. Dōngtiān yòu biàn huí le guāngtūtū de yàngzi, zhǐ shèng xià hēi sè de shùzhī zài tiānkōng xià huà chū fùzá de xiàntiáo. Wǒ zuò zài tóng yí gè wèizhì, měi tiān kànzhe tóng yì kē shù, dànshì tā měi tiān dōu zài biàn. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì wàng le kàn chuāng wài hǎo jǐ tiān, zài yí kàn de shíhou fāxiàn shù yǐjīng wánquán bù yíyàng le. Zhè ràng wǒ xiǎng dào, biànhuà yǒu shíhou shì hěn màn de, màn dào nǐ jīhū zhùyì bu dào. Dànshì zhǐyào nǐ yuànyì tíng xiàlái zǐxì kàn, jiù huì fāxiàn shìjiè měi tiān dōu zài bù yíyàng. Zhè kē yínxìng shù bù xūyào shuō huà, tā zhǐ yòng yánsè jiù néng gàosu nǐ xiànzài shì nǎge jìjié.
text_en: Outside my office window there's a large ginkgo tree. In spring, it starts bare, then slowly grows tender green leaves. In summer the leaves grow big and dense, like a green umbrella. In autumn, it's the most beautiful tree on the street — all its leaves turn golden yellow within two or three weeks, then fall one by one. In winter it returns to bareness, with only black branches drawing complex lines against the sky. I sit in the same seat looking at the same tree every day, yet it changes daily. Sometimes I forget to look out the window for several days, and when I look again, the tree is completely different. This reminds me that change is sometimes slow — so slow you barely notice. But if you're willing to stop and look carefully, you'll find the world is different every day. This ginkgo tree doesn't need to speak; it tells you the season using only color.

questions.explanation: Follow the action in "This ginkgo tree doesn't need to speak; it tells" — that's how.

title: The Overly Polite Neighbors
text_zh: 我和对面的邻居有一个奇怪的问题——我们都太客气了。每次在楼道里遇到，他说「你先走」，我说「不不不，你先走」，然后我们两个人就站在那里互相让了好几分钟。有一次，我们在电梯门口让了太久，电梯关上了又走了。上个星期他给我送了一盒月饼，我第二天给他送了一袋水果。然后他又给我送了一包茶叶。现在我觉得我还得再送他点什么东西，但是我不知道该送什么了。我太太说「你们两个应该签一个合同，规定一年只能互相送一次礼物。」我觉得她说得对，但是如果我去找他谈这件事，他一定会先请我进去喝茶，然后我又得带一个礼物去……这个循环什么时候才能结束？
text_pinyin: Wǒ hé duìmiàn de línjū yǒu yí gè qíguài de wèntí——wǒmen dōu tài kèqi le. Měi cì zài lóudào lǐ yù dào, tā shuō「nǐ xiān zǒu」, wǒ shuō「bù bù bù, nǐ xiān zǒu」, ránhòu wǒmen liǎng gè rén jiù zhàn zài nàlǐ hùxiāng ràng le hǎo jǐ fēnzhōng. Yǒu yí cì, wǒmen zài diàntī ménkǒu ràng le tài jiǔ, diàntī guān shàng le yòu zǒu le. Shàng gè xīngqī tā gěi wǒ sòng le yì hé yuèbǐng, wǒ dì èr tiān gěi tā sòng le yí dài shuǐguǒ. Ránhòu tā yòu gěi wǒ sòng le yì bāo cháyè. Xiànzài wǒ juéde wǒ hái děi zài sòng tā diǎn shénme dōngxi, dànshì wǒ bù zhīdào gāi sòng shénme le. Wǒ tàitai shuō「nǐmen liǎng gè yīnggāi qiān yí gè hétong, guīdìng yì nián zhǐ néng hùxiāng sòng yí cì lǐwù.」Wǒ juéde tā shuō de duì, dànshì rúguǒ wǒ qù zhǎo tā tán zhè jiàn shì, tā yídìng huì xiān qǐng wǒ jìnqù hē chá, ránhòu wǒ yòu děi dài yí gè lǐwù qù…… zhè ge xúnhuán shénme shíhou cái néng jiéshù?
text_en: My neighbor across the hall and I have a strange problem — we're both too polite. Every time we meet in the hallway, he says 'you go first,' I say 'no no no, you go first,' and then we stand there yielding to each other for several minutes. Once we yielded so long at the elevator that the doors closed and it left. Last week he brought me a box of mooncakes. The next day I gave him a bag of fruit. Then he gave me a package of tea leaves. Now I feel I need to give him something else, but I don't know what. My wife said 'you two should sign a contract limiting gift exchanges to once a year.' I think she's right, but if I go talk to him about it, he'll definitely invite me in for tea first, and then I'll have to bring a gift… when will this cycle ever end?

questions.q_zh: 他们在楼道里遇到的时候会怎么做？
questions.q_en: What do they do when they meet in the hallway?

options.pinyin: hùxiāng dǎ zhāohu jiù zǒu le
hùxiāng ràng le hǎo jǐ fēnzhōng
options.text_en: yield to each other for minutes
pretend not to see each other

questions.explanation: The answer sits right in "Every time we meet in the hallway, he says 'you."

questions.q_zh: 这些人之间最重要的是什么？
questions.q_en: What matters most between these people?
questions.explanation: The answer sits right in "My neighbor across the hall and I have a strange."

questions.q_zh: 为什么他不敢去找邻居谈这件事？
questions.q_en: Why is he afraid to talk to the neighbor about it?

options.text_en: afraid the neighbor will be angry

options.text: 怕又要开始新一轮送礼
options.pinyin: pà yòu yào kāishǐ xīn yì lún sòng lǐ
options.text_en: afraid it'll start another gift cycle

questions.explanation: "My wife said 'you two should sign a contract limiting" — the motivation is clear.

title: The Bicycle Left Behind
text_zh: 我家小区的门口有一辆旧自行车，已经在那里停了半年了。车篮里积满了落叶，轮胎早就没气了，锁也生了锈。一开始大家都以为有人会来骑走它，但是一个星期过去了，一个月过去了，没有人来。慢慢地，这辆自行车变成了小区的一部分。有人把广告单子塞在车篮里，有只猫喜欢躺在车座上晒太阳。小朋友们路过的时候会按一下车铃，虽然铃已经不响了。我有时候想，当初把这辆车停在这里的人，是忘了它，还是不要它了？也许他搬到了很远的地方，也许他买了一辆新的。不管怎样，这辆自行车还在等着，像一个被忘记了的约定。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā xiǎoqū de ménkǒu yǒu yí liàng jiù zìxíngchē, yǐjīng zài nàlǐ tíng le bàn nián le. Chē lán lǐ jī mǎn le luòyè, lúntāi zǎo jiù méi qì le, suǒ yě shēng le xiù. Yì kāishǐ dàjiā dōu yǐwéi yǒu rén huì lái qí zǒu tā, dànshì yí gè xīngqī guòqù le, yí gè yuè guòqù le, méi yǒu rén lái. Mànmàn de, zhè liàng zìxíngchē biàn chéng le xiǎoqū de yí bùfen. Yǒu rén bǎ guǎnggào dānzi sāi zài chē lán lǐ, yǒu zhī māo xǐhuan tǎng zài chēzuò shàng shài tàiyáng. Xiǎopéngyǒumen lùguò de shíhou huì àn yíxià chē líng, suīrán líng yǐjīng bù xiǎng le. Wǒ yǒu shíhou xiǎng, dāngchū bǎ zhè liàng chē tíng zài zhèlǐ de rén, shì wàng le tā, háishi búyào tā le? Yěxǔ tā bān dào le hěn yuǎn de dìfang, yěxǔ tā mǎi le yí liàng xīn de. Bùguǎn zěnyàng, zhè liàng zìxíngchē hái zài děngzhe, xiàng yí gè bèi wàngjì le de yuēdìng.
text_en: At the entrance of my residential compound there's an old bicycle that's been parked there for half a year. The basket is full of fallen leaves, the tires lost their air long ago, and the lock has rusted. At first everyone thought someone would come ride it away, but a week passed, a month passed, and no one came. Gradually, this bicycle became part of the compound. Someone stuffed flyers in the basket, a cat likes to lie on the seat and sunbathe. Children ring the bell when passing by, though it no longer sounds. Sometimes I wonder — did the person who parked it here forget it, or abandon it? Maybe they moved far away, maybe they bought a new one. Either way, the bicycle is still waiting, like a forgotten promise.

questions.q_zh: 这辆自行车在那里停了多久？
questions.q_en: How long has the bicycle been parked there?
questions.explanation: The method is right there: "At the entrance of my residential compound there's an old."

questions.q_zh: 自然环境对故事有什么影响？
questions.q_en: How does the natural setting affect the story?
questions.explanation: "At the entrance of my residential compound there's an old" reveals the how.

questions.q_zh: 自然环境对故事有什么影响？
questions.q_en: How does the natural setting affect the story?
questions.explanation: "At the entrance of my residential compound there's an old" reveals the how.

title: The Sound of Rain on Different Roofs
title_zh: 雨落在不同屋顶上的声音
text_zh: 小时候我住在乡下，家里的屋顶是铁皮的。每次下雨，雨点打在铁皮上，声音又大又急，像有人在用力敲鼓。后来我搬到城里，住在公寓楼里，下雨的时候几乎听不到声音。窗户关着，空调开着，雨好像不存在一样。再后来我去日本旅行，住在一个传统的旅馆里。那天晚上下了一场小雨，雨水从瓦片上一滴一滴地落到石头上，声音轻轻的，特别好听。不是雨变了，是屋顶变了。同样的雨，落在不同的地方，会发出完全不一样的声音。
text_pinyin: Xiǎo shíhou wǒ zhù zài xiāngxià, jiā lǐ de wūdǐng shì tiěpí de. Měi cì xià yǔ, yǔdiǎn dǎ zài tiěpí shàng, shēngyīn yòu dà yòu jí, xiàng yǒu rén zài yònglì qiāo gǔ. Hòulái wǒ bān dào chéng lǐ, zhù zài gōngyù lóu lǐ, xià yǔ de shíhou jīhū tīng bu dào shēngyīn. Chuānghu guān zhe, kōngtiáo kāi zhe, yǔ hǎoxiàng bù cúnzài yíyàng. Zài hòulái wǒ qù Rìběn lǚxíng, zhù zài yí gè chuántǒng de lǚguǎn lǐ. Nà tiān wǎnshàng xià le yì chǎng xiǎo yǔ, yǔshuǐ cóng wǎpiàn shàng yì dī yì dī de luò dào shítou shàng, shēngyīn qīngqīng de, tèbié hǎotīng. Wǒ tūrán míngbai le, búshì yǔ de shēngyīn biàn le, érshì wūdǐng biàn le. Tóngyàng de yǔ, luò zài bùtóng de dìfang, huì fāchū wánquán bù yíyàng de shēngyīn. Zhè ràng wǒ xiǎng dào, yěxǔ shēnghuó yě shì zhèyàng——búshì shēnghuó běnshēn biàn le, érshì wǒmen jiēshōu tā de fāngshì biàn le.
text_en: When I was little I lived in the countryside, and our roof was made of sheet metal. Every time it rained, the drops hitting the metal were loud and urgent, like someone pounding a drum. Later I moved to the city, living in an apartment building where I could barely hear the rain. Windows closed, air conditioning on — the rain might as well not exist. Then I traveled to Japan and stayed at a traditional inn. That evening there was a light rain, and water dripped from the roof tiles onto the stones one drop at a time — a soft, beautiful sound. It wasn't the rain that changed. It was the roof.

questions.q_zh: 小时候家里的屋顶是什么做的？
questions.q_en: What was the childhood roof made of?
questions.explanation: The answer sits right in "When I was little I lived in the countryside."

questions.q_zh: 在日本的旅馆里，雨声是什么样的？
questions.q_en: What was the rain like at the Japanese inn?
questions.explanation: "That evening there was a light rain, and water dripped" — once you spot this, the answer clicks.

questions.q_zh: 作者最后明白了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author ultimately understand?

options.text: 不是雨变了，是屋顶变了
options.pinyin: búshì yǔ biàn le, shì wūdǐng biàn le
options.text_en: it's not the rain that changed, but the roof
the countryside is better than the city

questions.explanation: Go back to "It wasn't the rain that changed." That's where the answer lives.

title: The Old Man's Morning Walk
text_zh: 每天早上六点半，我都能看到一个老人在公园里散步。他走得很慢，但是每一步都走得很稳。他总是穿着一件深蓝色的外套，手里拿着一个收音机，放着京剧。公园里跑步的年轻人从他身边飞快地经过，但他好像一点也不在意。有一天早上下着小雨，我以为他不会来了，可是他还是来了，撑着一把黑色的伞，走得跟平时一样慢。我忍不住问他：「大爷，下雨天也出来走啊？」他笑着说：「我跟这个公园约好了，每天都要见面的。」我听了以后，心里觉得特别温暖。原来，坚持一件小事也可以是一种承诺——不是对别人，而是对自己。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang liù diǎn bàn, wǒ dōu néng kàn dào yí gè lǎorén zài gōngyuán lǐ sànbù. Tā zǒu de hěn màn, dànshì měi yí bù dōu zǒu de hěn wěn. Tā zǒng shì chuān zhe yí jiàn shēn lán sè de wàitào, shǒu lǐ ná zhe yí gè shōuyīnjī, fàng zhe jīngjù. Gōngyuán lǐ pǎobù de niánqīng rén cóng tā shēnbiān fēikuài de jīngguò, dàn tā hǎoxiàng yìdiǎn yě bú zàiyì. Yǒu yì tiān zǎoshang xià zhe xiǎo yǔ, wǒ yǐwéi tā bú huì lái le, kěshì tā háishi lái le, chēng zhe yì bǎ hēi sè de sǎn, zǒu de gēn píngshí yíyàng màn. Wǒ rěn bu zhù wèn tā:「Dàyé, xià yǔ tiān yě chūlái zǒu a?」Tā xiào zhe shuō:「Wǒ gēn zhège gōngyuán yuē hǎo le, měi tiān dōu yào jiànmiàn de.」Wǒ tīng le yǐhòu, xīn lǐ juéde tèbié wēnnuǎn. Yuánlái, jiānchí yí jiàn xiǎo shì yě kěyǐ shì yì zhǒng chéngnuò——búshì duì biérén, érshì duì zìjǐ.
text_en: Every morning at six-thirty, I see an old man walking in the park. He walks very slowly, but every step is steady. He always wears a dark blue jacket and carries a radio playing Peking opera. Young joggers fly past him, but he doesn't seem to mind at all. One rainy morning I thought he wouldn't come, but he still did, holding a black umbrella, walking just as slowly as usual. I couldn't help asking: 'Sir, you come out even in the rain?' He smiled and said: 'I have a date with this park — we meet every day.' After hearing that, my heart felt especially warm. It turns out that persisting in a small thing can also be a kind of promise — not to others, but to yourself.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的'安静'不只是物理上的安静，还代表什么？
questions.q_en: The 'quiet' in this passage is not just physical silence — what else does it represent?
questions.explanation: "Every morning at six-thirty" — the parallel is stated, not implied.

questions.q_zh: 下雨天老人做了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the old man do on the rainy day?

options.text_en: walked as usual with an umbrella

options.pinyin: zài tíngzi lǐ děng yǔ tíng

questions.explanation: "One rainy morning I thought he wouldn't come" — once you spot this, the answer clicks.

questions.q_zh: 老人说他跟公园有什么约定？
questions.q_en: What appointment did the old man say he has with the park?
questions.explanation: The key is "Sir, you come out even in the rain?' He smiled" — read it with the answer in mind.

title: The Coffee Shop That Never Opens on Time
title_zh: 从不准时开门的咖啡店
text_zh: 我们公司旁边有一家小咖啡店，门上写着「早上八点开门」，但是我从来没见它八点准时开过门。有时候八点一刻，有时候八点半，最晚的一次快九点了老板才慢慢地出现。他是一个三十多岁的男人，总是一脸还没睡醒的样子。但是他做的咖啡真的很好喝。有一次我忍不住问他：「你为什么不把开门时间改成九点呢？」他想了一下说：「如果写九点，那我可能十点才到。给自己一个早一点的目标，至少不会迟到太多。」大家都笑了。虽然这家店不太靠谱，但是附近上班的人都喜欢来这里。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen gōngsī pángbiān yǒu yì jiā xiǎo kāfēi diàn, mén shàng xiě zhe「zǎoshang bā diǎn kāi mén」, dànshì wǒ cónglái méi jiàn tā bā diǎn zhǔnshí kāi guò mén. Yǒu shíhou bā diǎn yí kè, yǒu shíhou bā diǎn bàn, zuì wǎn de yí cì kuài jiǔ diǎn le lǎobǎn cái mànmàn de chūxiàn. Tā shì yí gè sānshí duō suì de nánrén, zǒng shì yì liǎn hái méi shuì xǐng de yàngzi. Dànshì tā zuò de kāfēi zhēn de hěn hǎohē. Yǒu yí cì wǒ rěn bu zhù wèn tā:「Nǐ wèishénme bù bǎ kāi mén shíjiān gǎi chéng jiǔ diǎn ne?」Tā xiǎng le yíxià shuō:「Rúguǒ xiě jiǔ diǎn, nà wǒ kěnéng shí diǎn cái dào. Gěi zìjǐ yí gè zǎo yìdiǎn de mùbiāo, zhìshǎo bú huì chídào tài duō.」Dàjiā dōu xiào le. Suīrán zhè jiā diàn bú tài kào pǔ, dànshì fùjìn shàngbān de rén dōu xǐhuan lái zhèlǐ. Yěxǔ yǒuxiē dìfang xīyǐn rén de búshì xiàolǜ, érshì nà zhǒng suíyì de, ràng rén fàngsōng de gǎnjué.
text_en: Next to our company there's a small coffee shop. The door says 'opens at 8 AM,' but I've never seen it open on time. Sometimes it's 8:15, sometimes 8:30 — the latest was almost 9 when the owner finally showed up slowly. He's a man in his thirties who always looks half-asleep. But the coffee he makes is really good. Once I couldn't help asking: 'Why don't you change the opening time to nine?' He thought for a moment and said: 'If I write nine, I probably won't arrive until ten. Setting an earlier goal means I won't be too late at least.' Although this shop isn't very reliable, people working nearby all love coming here.

questions.q_zh: 咖啡店门上写的开门时间是几点？
questions.q_en: What time does the door say the shop opens?
questions.explanation: Focus on "The door says 'opens at 8 AM,' but I've never" — the rest follows.

questions.q_zh: 老板为什么不把时间改成九点？
questions.q_en: Why doesn't the owner change the time to nine?

options.pinyin: tā juéde bā diǎn hěn hǎo
pà xiě jiǔ diǎn jiù shí diǎn cái dào
options.text_en: afraid he'd arrive at 10 if he wrote 9

questions.explanation: The why is in the details: "Why don't you change the opening time to nine?."

questions.q_zh: 大家为什么喜欢这家咖啡店？
questions.q_en: Why do people like this coffee shop?

options.pinyin: yīnwèi měi tiān zhǔnshí kāi mén

options.text_en: because of the casual relaxing feeling

questions.explanation: The why is in the details: "Next to our company there's a small coffee shop."

title: The Shared Umbrella Stand
text_zh: 我们办公楼的大厅里有一个雨伞架，是前台小姐自己买的。她注意到每次突然下雨的时候，很多人都没有带伞，就站在门口等着，有的人等了半个小时雨还没停。所以她买了十把便宜的透明雨伞放在架子上，谁需要谁就拿走，第二天还回来就行。一开始只有几个人用，后来越来越多的人知道了。奇怪的是，雨伞的数量不但没有减少，反而慢慢增加了——有些人还伞的时候会多带一把新的来。现在架子上已经有二十多把伞了，各种各样的颜色。前台小姐说她只是想解决一个小问题，没想到大家会这样回应。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen bàngōng lóu de dàtīng lǐ yǒu yí gè yǔsǎn jià, shì qiántái xiǎojiě zìjǐ mǎi de. Tā zhùyì dào měi cì tūrán xià yǔ de shíhou, hěn duō rén dōu méi yǒu dài sǎn, jiù zhàn zài ménkǒu děngzhe, yǒu de rén děng le bàn gè xiǎoshí yǔ hái méi tíng. Suǒyǐ tā mǎi le shí bǎ piányi de tòumíng yǔsǎn fàng zài jiàzi shàng, shuí xūyào shuí jiù ná zǒu, dì èr tiān huán huílái jiù xíng. Yì kāishǐ zhǐ yǒu jǐ gè rén yòng, hòulái yuè lái yuè duō de rén zhīdào le. Qíguài de shì, yǔsǎn de shùliàng búdàn méi yǒu jiǎnshǎo, fǎn'ér mànmàn zēngjiā le——yǒuxiē rén huán sǎn de shíhou huì duō dài yì bǎ xīn de lái. Xiànzài jiàzi shàng yǐjīng yǒu èrshí duō bǎ sǎn le, gè zhǒng gè yàng de yánsè. Qiántái xiǎojiě shuō tā zhǐ shì xiǎng jiějué yí gè xiǎo wèntí, méi xiǎng dào dàjiā huì zhèyàng huíyìng. Yěxǔ shànyì jiù shì zhèyàng de, nǐ gěi chū yìdiǎn, huílái de zǒng shì gèng duō.
text_en: In our office building lobby there's an umbrella stand that the receptionist bought herself. She noticed that every time it rained suddenly, many people didn't have umbrellas and just stood at the door waiting — some waited half an hour and the rain still hadn't stopped. So she bought ten cheap transparent umbrellas and put them on the stand: whoever needs one takes it, just return it the next day. At first only a few people used them, but more and more people found out. Strangely, the number of umbrellas not only didn't decrease but gradually increased — some people brought an extra new one when returning theirs. Now there are over twenty umbrellas on the stand, in all sorts of colors. The receptionist said she just wanted to solve a small problem and didn't expect everyone to respond this way.

questions.q_zh: 雨伞架是谁买的？
questions.q_en: Who bought the umbrella stand?
questions.explanation: Focus on "In our office building lobby there's an umbrella stand" — the rest follows.

questions.q_zh: 雨伞的数量后来怎么样了？
questions.q_en: What happened to the number of umbrellas?
questions.explanation: "Strangely, the number of umbrellas not only didn't decrease" gives you what you need.

questions.q_zh: 这段话如何处理'孤独'这个概念？
questions.q_en: How does this passage treat the concept of 'solitude'?

options.pinyin: bǎ tā dàng zuò yì zhǒng xiǎngshòu
options.text_en: treats it as something to enjoy

questions.explanation: "In our office building lobby there's an umbrella stand" walks you through it.

title: The Library's Quiet Rule
text_zh: 我常去的那个图书馆有一条不成文的规定：三楼是绝对安静的区域，连翻书的声音都要尽量轻。一开始我觉得这个要求有点过分，但是坐了一个下午以后，我理解了。在那种安静里，你能听到自己的呼吸，能感觉到时间在慢慢地流动。有一次，一个年轻人的手机突然响了，虽然他马上就把手机关了，但是那几秒钟的铃声让所有人都吓了一跳。他的脸一下子红了，连忙小声说了好几句对不起。旁边的老奶奶笑着轻轻摇了摇头，表示没关系。我发现，在一个特别安静的地方，人和人之间反而会更加注意彼此。也许安静不只是没有声音，而是一种大家共同创造的氛围。
text_pinyin: Wǒ cháng qù de nàge túshūguǎn yǒu yì tiáo bù chéng wén de guīdìng: sān lóu shì juéduì ānjìng de qūyù, lián fān shū de shēngyīn dōu yào jǐnliàng qīng. Yì kāishǐ wǒ juéde zhège yāoqiú yǒudiǎn guòfèn, dànshì zuò le yí gè xiàwǔ yǐhòu, wǒ lǐjiě le. Zài nà zhǒng ānjìng lǐ, nǐ néng tīng dào zìjǐ de hūxī, néng gǎnjué dào shíjiān zài mànmàn de liúdòng. Yǒu yí cì, yí gè niánqīng rén de shǒujī tūrán xiǎng le, suīrán tā mǎshàng jiù bǎ shǒujī guān le, dànshì nà jǐ miǎozhōng de língshēng ràng suǒyǒu rén dōu xià le yí tiào. Tā de liǎn yíxiàzi hóng le, liánmáng xiǎo shēng shuō le hǎo jǐ jù duìbuqǐ. Pángbiān de lǎo nǎinai xiào zhe qīngqīng yáo le yáo tóu, biǎoshì méi guānxi. Wǒ fāxiàn, zài yí gè tèbié ānjìng de dìfang, rén hé rén zhījiān fǎn'ér huì gèngjiā zhùyì bǐcǐ. Yěxǔ ānjìng bù zhǐ shì méi yǒu shēngyīn, érshì yì zhǒng dàjiā gòngtóng chuàngzào de fēnwéi.
text_en: The library I often go to has an unwritten rule: the third floor is an absolutely quiet zone — even the sound of turning pages should be as soft as possible. At first I thought this was a bit extreme, but after sitting there one afternoon, I understood. In that kind of silence, you can hear your own breathing, feel time flowing slowly. Once, a young person's phone suddenly rang. Although they turned it off immediately, those few seconds of ringing startled everyone. Their face turned red instantly, and they hurriedly whispered several apologies. The elderly woman next to them smiled and gently shook her head, indicating it was fine. I discovered that in a quiet place, people actually pay more attention to each other. Perhaps silence isn't just the absence of sound, but an atmosphere everyone creates together.

questions.q_zh: 场景的选择对读者有什么影响？
questions.q_en: How does the choice of setting affect the reader?

options.text_en: makes the reader want to travel

questions.explanation: Look at "The library I often go to has an unwritten rule" — it shows the mechanism.

questions.q_zh: 手机响了以后，年轻人的反应是什么？
questions.q_en: How did the young person react after the phone rang?

options.pinyin: liǎn hóng le, lián shuō duìbuqǐ

questions.explanation: "Once, a young person's phone suddenly rang" walks you through it.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么不直接说出结论？
questions.q_en: Why doesn't the author state the conclusion directly?

options.text_en: to let the reader experience it themselves
the author doesn't know the answer

questions.explanation: It comes down to "The library I often go to has an unwritten rule" — cause and effect.

title: The Museum Guard Who Loves Art
text_zh: 去博物馆的时候，我注意到一个保安，他站在一幅画前面已经站了很久了。我以为他在监视参观的人，但是后来我发现，他的眼睛一直盯着那幅画看。我忍不住走过去问他：「你喜欢这幅画吗？」他有点不好意思地笑了：「我在这里工作了五年了，每天都能看到这些画。一开始我觉得它们都一样，但是看的时间长了，每幅画都变得不一样了。」他指着墙上的一幅风景画说：「你看这幅，早上的光照在上面和下午的光完全不同。我最喜欢下午三点的样子。」我没想到一个保安会这样看画。
text_pinyin: Qù bówùguǎn de shíhou, wǒ zhùyì dào yí gè bǎo'ān, tā zhàn zài yì fú huà qiánmiàn yǐjīng zhàn le hěn jiǔ le. Wǒ yǐwéi tā zài jiānshì cānguān de rén, dànshì hòulái wǒ fāxiàn, tā de yǎnjing yìzhí dīng zhe nà fú huà kàn. Wǒ rěn bu zhù zǒu guòqù wèn tā:「Nǐ xǐhuan zhè fú huà ma?」Tā yǒudiǎn bù hǎoyìsi de xiào le:「Wǒ zài zhèlǐ gōngzuò le wǔ nián le, měi tiān dōu néng kàn dào zhèxiē huà. Yì kāishǐ wǒ juéde tāmen dōu yíyàng, dànshì kàn de shíjiān cháng le, měi fú huà dōu biàn de bù yíyàng le.」Tā zhǐ zhe qiáng shàng de yì fú fēngjǐng huà shuō:「Nǐ kàn zhè fú, zǎoshang de guāng zhào zài shàngmiàn hé xiàwǔ de guāng wánquán bùtóng. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan xiàwǔ sān diǎn de yàngzi.」Wǒ méi xiǎng dào yí gè bǎo'ān huì zhèyàng kàn huà. Yěxǔ, zhēnzhèng de xīnshǎng bù xūyào zhuānyè zhīshi, zhǐ xūyào shíjiān hé nàixīn.
text_en: At the museum, I noticed a security guard who had been standing in front of a painting for a long time. I thought he was monitoring visitors, but then I realized his eyes were fixed on the painting. I couldn't help walking over to ask: 'Do you like this painting?' He smiled a bit shyly: 'I've worked here for five years, seeing these paintings every day. At first I thought they all looked the same, but after watching long enough, each one became different.' He pointed to a landscape painting on the wall: 'Look at this one — the morning light on it is completely different from the afternoon light. I like how it looks at 3 PM the most.' I never expected a security guard to look at paintings this way.

questions.q_zh: 保安在博物馆工作了多长时间？
questions.q_en: How long has the guard worked at the museum?
questions.explanation: "Do you like this painting?' He smiled a bit shyly" reveals the how.

questions.q_en: What deeper meaning does the 'repetition' in this passage carry?
questions.explanation: "At the museum" is a window into what the author really means.

questions.q_zh: 这段话的'安静'不只是物理上的安静，还代表什么？
questions.q_en: The 'quiet' in this passage is not just physical silence — what else does it represent?
questions.explanation: "At the museum" — the parallel is stated, not implied.

title: The Photograph That Moved
text_zh: 我的书桌上有一张照片，是我大学毕业的时候拍的。照片放在一个小相框里，一直靠在台灯旁边。但是有一天早上，我发现照片的位置变了——它被移到了书桌的另一边，靠着一摞书。我问我太太是不是她动的，她说不是。我又问我女儿，她也说不是。我把照片放回了原来的位置。但是过了两天，它又被移到了不同的地方。这次它面朝着窗户，好像在往外看。我开始觉得有点奇怪了。后来有一天晚上，我半夜起来喝水，看到我家的猫正用爪子把相框推到一个新的位置。原来是它！我不知道猫为什么喜欢移动这张照片，也许它只是觉得这个东西推起来手感不错。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de shūzhuō shàng yǒu yì zhāng zhàopiàn, shì wǒ dàxué bìyè de shíhou pāi de. Zhàopiàn fàng zài yí gè xiǎo xiàngkuāng lǐ, yìzhí kào zài táidēng pángbiān. Dànshì yǒu yì tiān zǎoshang, wǒ fāxiàn zhàopiàn de wèizhì biàn le——tā bèi yí dào le shūzhuō de lìng yì biān, kào zhe yì luò shū. Wǒ wèn wǒ tàitai shì bu shì tā dòng de, tā shuō búshì. Wǒ yòu wèn wǒ nǚ'ér, tā yě shuō búshì. Wǒ bǎ zhàopiàn fàng huí le yuánlái de wèizhì. Dànshì guò le liǎng tiān, tā yòu bèi yí dào le bùtóng de dìfang. Zhè cì tā miàn cháo zhe chuānghu, hǎoxiàng zài wǎng wài kàn. Wǒ kāishǐ juéde yǒudiǎn qíguài le. Hòulái yǒu yì tiān wǎnshàng, wǒ bànyè qǐlái hē shuǐ, kàn dào wǒ jiā de māo zhèng yòng zhǎozi bǎ xiàngkuāng tuī dào yí gè xīn de wèizhì. Yuánlái shì tā! Wǒ bù zhīdào māo wèishénme xǐhuan yídòng zhè zhāng zhàopiàn, yěxǔ tā zhǐ shì juéde zhège dōngxi tuī qǐlái shǒugǎn búcuò.
text_en: On my desk there's a photo taken when I graduated from college. It sits in a small frame, always leaning against the desk lamp. But one morning I found the photo had moved — it had been shifted to the other side of the desk, leaning against a stack of books. I asked my wife if she'd moved it; she said no. I asked my daughter; she also said no. I put it back. But two days later, it had moved again to a different spot. This time it was facing the window, as if looking outside. I started feeling a bit strange. Then one night I got up at midnight for water and saw our cat pushing the frame to a new position with its paw. It was the cat all along! I don't know why the cat likes moving this photo — maybe it just thinks the frame feels nice to push.

questions.q_zh: 这段话中什么是'看不见'但能感觉到的？
questions.q_en: What in this passage is 'invisible' but can be felt?

options.pinyin: rénwù de qínggǎn hé nèixīn shìjiè
options.text_en: the character's emotions and inner world

questions.q_en: What is the biggest difference between this passage and a news report?
questions.explanation: The key is "I started feeling a bit strange" — read it with the answer in mind.

questions.q_zh: 最后发现是谁移动了照片？
questions.q_en: Who was found to have moved the photo?
questions.explanation: Go back to "I don't know why the cat likes moving this photo." That's where the answer lives.

title: The Name I Chose for Myself
text_zh: 在中国，名字是父母给的，一般一辈子都不会改。但是我认识一个朋友，她在三十岁的时候给自己改了名字。她原来的名字是「淑华」，很传统的女孩名字。她说这个名字是奶奶取的，意思是「温柔有文化的女孩」。但是她觉得自己不是那样的人——她喜欢爬山，喜欢一个人旅行，说话也很直接。所以她把名字改成了「行远」，意思是「走得远」。她爸妈一开始很不高兴，觉得她不尊重长辈。但是她说：「名字应该是你想成为的样子，不是别人希望你成为的样子。」现在她的家人都习惯了叫她「行远」。有时候我想，一个名字真的能改变一个人吗？也许不能，但是它能让你每天记住你想做什么样的人。
text_pinyin: Zài Zhōngguó, míngzi shì fùmǔ gěi de, yìbān yí bèizi dōu bú huì gǎi. Dànshì wǒ rènshi yí gè péngyou, tā zài sānshí suì de shíhou gěi zìjǐ gǎi le míngzi. Tā yuánlái de míngzi shì「Shūhuá」, hěn chuántǒng de nǚhái míngzi. Tā shuō zhège míngzi shì nǎinai qǔ de, yìsi shì「wēnróu yǒu wénhuà de nǚhái」. Dànshì tā juéde zìjǐ búshì nàyàng de rén——tā xǐhuan pá shān, xǐhuan yí gè rén lǚxíng, shuō huà yě hěn zhíjiē. Suǒyǐ tā bǎ míngzi gǎi chéng le「Xíngyuǎn」, yìsi shì「zǒu de yuǎn」. Tā bà mā yì kāishǐ hěn bù gāoxìng, juéde tā bù zūnzhòng zhǎngbèi. Dànshì tā shuō:「Míngzi yīnggāi shì nǐ xiǎng chéngwéi de yàngzi, búshì biérén xīwàng nǐ chéngwéi de yàngzi.」Xiànzài tā de jiārén dōu xíguàn le jiào tā「Xíngyuǎn」. Yǒu shíhou wǒ xiǎng, yí gè míngzi zhēn de néng gǎibiàn yí gè rén ma? Yěxǔ bù néng, dànshì tā néng ràng nǐ měi tiān jì zhù nǐ xiǎng zuò shénme yàng de rén.
text_en: In China, names are given by parents and generally never changed for a lifetime. But I know a friend who changed her name at thirty. Her original name was 'Shuhua' — a very traditional girl's name. She said her grandmother chose it, meaning 'gentle and cultured girl.' But she felt that wasn't who she was — she likes climbing mountains, traveling alone, and speaks very directly. So she changed her name to 'Xingyuan,' meaning 'traveling far.' Her parents were unhappy at first, feeling she didn't respect her elders. But she said: 'A name should be who you want to become, not who others want you to be.' Now her family is used to calling her 'Xingyuan.' Sometimes I wonder: can a name really change a person? Maybe not, but it can remind you every day of the kind of person you want to be.

questions.q_zh: 朋友原来的名字是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What did the friend's original name mean?
questions.explanation: Go back to "She said her grandmother chose it, meaning 'gentle and cultured." That's where the answer lives.

questions.q_en: Why were her parents unhappy at first?

title: The Group Chat That Won't Stop
text_zh: 我有一个大学同学的微信群，里面有三十多个人。大部分时间都很安静，但是只要有一个人发消息，就会停不下来。上个星期有人在群里问：「有没有人知道北京哪里的火锅好吃？」这个问题一发出来，群里一下子热闹了起来。每个人都推荐了不同的火锅店。然后有人说「四川火锅比北京火锅好吃」，马上就有人反对。接着有人开始讨论到底哪个城市的火锅最正宗，这个话题又变成了「哪个城市的食物最好吃」。两个小时以后，大家已经在讨论「应不应该在粽子里放肉」了。我不知道话题是怎么从火锅跳到粽子的，但是我手机上已经有五百多条未读消息了。我只好把这个群设成了静音。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu yí gè dàxué tóngxué de wēixìn qún, lǐmiàn yǒu sānshí duō gè rén. Dà bùfen shíjiān dōu hěn ānjìng, dànshì zhǐyào yǒu yí gè rén fā xiāoxi, jiù huì tíng bu xiàlái. Shàng gè xīngqī yǒu rén zài qún lǐ wèn:「Yǒu méi yǒu rén zhīdào Běijīng nǎlǐ de huǒguō hǎochī?」Zhège wèntí yì fā chūlái, qún lǐ yíxiàzi rènao le qǐlái. Měi gè rén dōu tuījiàn le bùtóng de huǒguō diàn. Ránhòu yǒu rén shuō「Sìchuān huǒguō bǐ Běijīng huǒguō hǎochī」, mǎshàng jiù yǒu rén fǎnduì. Jiēzhe yǒu rén kāishǐ tǎolùn dàodǐ nǎge chéngshì de huǒguō zuì zhèngzōng, zhège huàtí yòu biàn chéng le「nǎge chéngshì de shíwù zuì hǎochī」. Liǎng gè xiǎoshí yǐhòu, dàjiā yǐjīng zài tǎolùn「yīng bu yīnggāi zài zòngzi lǐ fàng ròu」le. Wǒ bù zhīdào huàtí shì zěnme cóng huǒguō tiào dào zòngzi de, dànshì wǒ shǒujī shàng yǐjīng yǒu wǔbǎi duō tiáo wèi dú xiāoxi le. Wǒ zhǐhǎo bǎ zhège qún shè chéng le jìngyīn.
text_en: I have a WeChat group with over thirty college classmates. Most of the time it's quiet, but once someone sends a message, it won't stop. Last week someone asked in the group: 'Does anyone know where the good hotpot is in Beijing?' The moment this question went out, everyone started talking at once. Everyone recommended different hotpot restaurants. Then someone said 'Sichuan hotpot is better than Beijing hotpot,' and immediately someone disagreed. Then people started debating which city has the most authentic hotpot, which turned into 'which city has the best food.' Two hours later, everyone was discussing 'whether zongzi should have meat in them.' I don't know how the topic jumped from hotpot to zongzi, but I already had over five hundred unread messages. I had no choice but to mute the group.

questions.q_en: What was the original topic discussed in the group?

options.pinyin: zòngzi lǐ gāi bu gāi fàng ròu

questions.explanation: The key is "Does anyone know where the good hotpot is in Beijing?" — read it with the answer in mind.

questions.q_zh: 两个小时以后话题变成了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the topic become after two hours?

options.pinyin: zòngzi lǐ gāi bu gāi fàng ròu

questions.explanation: "People started debating which city has the most authentic hotpot" — once you spot this, the answer clicks.

questions.q_zh: 最后「我」怎么做了？
questions.q_en: What did 'I' end up doing?

options.pinyin: bǎ qún shè chéng le jìngyīn

questions.explanation: Focus on "Does anyone know where the good hotpot is in Beijing?" — the rest follows.

title: The Sound the Vending Machine Makes
text_zh: 公司楼下有一台很老的自动售货机。每次你投了硬币，按了按钮，它都会先安静几秒钟，好像在想要不要把东西给你。然后你能听到里面「咔嗒」一声，东西就掉下来了。有时候它还会多掉一个出来，像是送你的礼物。晚上加班的时候，走廊里只有这台机器的嗡嗡声和亮着的灯。它不会问你为什么这么晚还在工作，也不会说「你应该早点回家」。它只是安静地等着，不管什么时候你过来，它都在。有一天公司说要换一台新的，大家居然都有点舍不得。新机器又快又安静，但是少了那个「咔嗒」的声音。有些东西，你觉得它不重要，但是等它不在了，你才发现你已经习惯了它。
text_pinyin: Gōngsī lóu xià yǒu yì tái hěn lǎo de zìdòng shòuhuò jī. Měi cì nǐ tóu le yìngbì, àn le ànniǔ, tā dōu huì xiān ānjìng jǐ miǎozhōng, hǎoxiàng zài xiǎng yào bu yào bǎ dōngxi gěi nǐ. Ránhòu nǐ néng tīng dào lǐmiàn「kādā」yì shēng, dōngxi jiù diào xiàlái le. Yǒu shíhou tā hái huì duō diào yí gè chūlái, xiàng shì sòng nǐ de lǐwù. Wǎnshàng jiābān de shíhou, zǒuláng lǐ zhǐ yǒu zhè tái jīqì de wēngwēng shēng hé liàng zhe de dēng. Tā bú huì wèn nǐ wèishénme zhème wǎn hái zài gōngzuò, yě bú huì shuō「nǐ yīnggāi zǎo diǎn huí jiā」. Tā zhǐ shì ānjìng de děngzhe, bùguǎn shénme shíhou nǐ guòlái, tā dōu zài. Yǒu yì tiān gōngsī shuō yào huàn yì tái xīn de, dàjiā jūrán dōu yǒudiǎn shěbude. Xīn jīqì yòu kuài yòu ānjìng, dànshì shǎo le nàge「kādā」de shēngyīn. Yǒuxiē dōngxi, nǐ juéde tā bú zhòngyào, dànshì děng tā bú zài le, nǐ cái fāxiàn nǐ yǐjīng xíguàn le tā.
text_en: Downstairs at the company there's a old vending machine. Every time you put in coins and press the button, it goes quiet for a few seconds, as if deciding whether to give you the item. Then you hear a 'click' inside, and the item drops down. Sometimes it drops an extra one, like a gift. During late-night overtime, the hallway has only this machine's hum and its glowing light. It won't ask why you're still working so late, won't say 'you should go home earlier.' It just waits quietly — whenever you come, it's there. One day the company said they'd replace it with a new one, and everyone surprisingly felt reluctant to let it go. The new machine is fast and quiet, but it's missing that 'click' sound. Some things you think aren't important, but once they're gone, you realize you were already used to them.

questions.q_zh: 这种感情最可能来自什么？
questions.q_en: What is the most likely source of this feeling?
questions.explanation: The emotion comes through in choices like "Downstairs at the company there's a old vending machine."

questions.q_en: How did everyone react when told the machine would be replaced?
questions.explanation: The method is right there: "One day the company said they'd replace."
